The method is evaluated on three simulated cryo-EM datasets generated using a digital twin, with RMSD and TM scores as metrics. It demonstrates robustness to high noise, low contrast, and misspecifications in the image formation model.
key_methods: Explicit backbone parameterisation (bonds, torsion angles, bond angles)
summary.data_or_benchmark: Three simulated cryo-EM protein datasets generated using an electron microscope digital twin. Evaluated using RMSD and TM scores against ground truth structures.
summary.main_contribution: A multiscale reconstruction algorithm that directly recovers atomic protein models from cryo-EM data with state-of-the-art accuracy under high-noise, low-contrast conditions, using explicit backbone parameterisation as a strong prior.
summary.limitations: Evaluated only on simulated data (digital twin), not on real experimental cryo-EM images. Computational scalability to large protein complexes is not discussed. The method focuses on backbone reconstruction and may not capture side-chain conformations or post-translational modifications.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. This paper is about structural biology and computational imaging, not directly related to osteoarthritis, proteomics (pQTL), cardiometabolic traits, Mendelian randomisation, or multimodal clinical prediction. No connection to genetic epidemiology or causal inference methods.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly applicable. The paper does not address proteomics in the sense of protein abundance/quantification (pQTL), nor does it connect to disease genetics or clinical prediction.
summary.possible_research_idea: None directly. One could imagine a distant link if protein structural features from cryo-EM were integrated with pQTL data to study how structural variants affect protein function in osteoarthritis, but this is highly speculative and far from the paper's scope.

title: Compositional Skill Routing for LLM Agents: Decompose, Retrieve, and Compose
summary: LLM agents increasingly rely on external skills -- reusable tool specifications -- but real-world tasks often require composing multiple skills, not just selecting one. We formalize this as the Compositional Skill Routing problem: given a complex user query and a large skill library, decompose the query into atomic sub-tasks, retrieve the appropriate skill for each sub-task, and compose an executable plan. We present SkillWeaver, a decompose-retrieve-compose framework combining an LLM task decomposer, a bi-encoder skill retriever with FAISS indexing, and a dependency-aware DAG planner. To support evaluation, we introduce CompSkillBench, a benchmark of 300 compositional queries over 2,209 real MCP server skills spanning 24 functional categories, sourced from the public MCP ecosystem. Our experiments reveal that task decomposition quality is the primary bottleneck: standard LLM decomposition reaches only 34.2% category recall at the step level. To address this, we propose Iterative Skill-Aware Decomposition (SAD), a retrieval-augmented feedback loop that iteratively aligns decomposition with available skills. SAD improves decomposition accuracy from 51.0% to 67.7% (+32.7%, Wilcoxon p < 10^-6) in a single iteration; DA-conditioned analysis confirms that correct granularity is the prerequisite for effective retrieval (CatR@1 rises from 34% to 41% when DA=1). SkillWeaver reduces context window consumption by over 99%, and transfer experiments confirm generalization (+35.6% relative DA gain even when target categories are absent from the retrieval pool).

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents SkillWeaver, a framework that helps LLM agents break down complex user requests into smaller sub-tasks, find the right tool for each sub-task from a large library, and compose them into an executable plan. The authors also built a benchmark (CompSkillBench) with 300 compositional queries over 2,209 real skills, and found that the main bottleneck is how well the system can decompose tasks. They propose an iterative method (Skill-Aware Decomposition) that improves decomposition accuracy by ~33%.
summary.technical_summary: The paper formalises the Compositional Skill Routing problem for LLM agents: given a complex query and a large skill library, decompose the query into atomic sub-tasks, retrieve the correct skill per sub-task, and compose a dependency-aware DAG plan. SkillWeaver combines an LLM-based task decomposer, a bi-encoder retriever with FAISS indexing, and a DAG planner. CompSkillBench contains 300 compositional queries over 2,209 real MCP server skills across 24 categories. The key finding is that task decomposition quality is the primary bottleneck (standard LLM decomposition achieves only 34.2% category recall at step level). The proposed Skill-Aware Decomposition (SAD) uses retrieval-augmented feedback to iteratively align decomposition with available skills, improving accuracy from 51.0% to 67.7% (+32.7%).
key_methods: Bi-encoder skill retriever with FAISS indexing
summary.data_or_benchmark: CompSkillBench: 300 compositional queries over 2,209 real MCP server skills spanning 24 functional categories from the public MCP ecosystem.
summary.main_contribution: Formalises the Compositional Skill Routing problem and proposes SkillWeaver, a decompose-retrieve-compose framework. Identifies task decomposition quality as the primary bottleneck and introduces Skill-Aware Decomposition (SAD) to iteratively improve it. Demonstrates >99% reduction in context window consumption.
summary.limitations: The benchmark is limited to MCP server skills (tool-use domain) and may not generalise to biomedical or scientific tool composition. The SAD improvement, while statistically significant, still leaves ~32% of decompositions inaccurate. No evaluation on real-world biomedical or multi-omics workflows.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. This is a pure NLP/LLM-agent paper with no connection to epidemiology, proteomics, multi-omics, or clinical prediction. The compositional skill routing concept is too generic and far from Weijie's research focus on osteoarthritis, MR, PRS, and multimodal prediction.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly useful. The decompose-retrieve-compose paradigm could theoretically be applied to building automated multi-omics analysis pipelines (e.g., decompose a causal inference question into GWAS → colocalisation → MR → mediation steps), but the paper provides no biomedical validation or domain-specific adaptation.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could adapt SkillWeaver to build an LLM agent that composes multi-omics analysis workflows (e.g., given a query about osteoarthritis drug targets, decompose into pQTL extraction → colocalisation → MR → PRS validation steps, retrieve appropriate tools/functions, and execute). However, this would require substantial domain engineering beyond what the paper offers.

title: Uncertainty Quantification for Flow-Based Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-language-action models (VLAs) combine vision-language backbones with expressive generative action heads trained via flow matching on large-scale robotic datasets. Despite their strong empirical performance in robotic manipulation, VLAs lack mechanisms to quantify confidence in their predictions and to detect when their actions may be unreliable. This presents a critical limitation for real-world deployment in non-stationary environments, where models inevitably encounter scenarios outside their pretraining distribution and may fail without warning. To address this, we derive an efficient method for quantifying epistemic uncertainty in flow-matching models by leveraging velocity-field disagreement (VFD) across a small ensemble. We successfully use this uncertainty estimate for failure detection during deployment and active fine-tuning of flow-based VLAs. To this end, we propose SAVE, a framework for uncertainty-guided active multitask fine-tuning that reduces the number of costly expert demonstrations required to adapt VLAs to new tasks. Through extensive experiments on the LIBERO benchmark, we demonstrate that VFD yields better-calibrated uncertainty estimates predictive of downstream performance, that VFD achieves strong performance in detecting failures, and that uncertainty-guided data acquisition with SAVE requires at least 22% fewer samples than baselines. In summary, our work shows that quantifying epistemic uncertainty in flow-based VLAs improves both failure awareness and adaptation. Project website: tum-lsy.github.io/uq_vla/.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper develops a method to measure how confident a robotic AI model is in its predictions. The model (a vision-language-action model) controls robots based on images and text commands. The authors propose using "velocity-field disagreement" across a small group of models to detect when the robot might fail, and use this uncertainty signal to decide which new examples to learn from, reducing the number of human demonstrations needed.
summary.technical_summary: The paper addresses epistemic uncertainty quantification in flow-matching-based vision-language-action models (VLAs). It derives velocity-field disagreement (VFD) from a small ensemble of flow-matching models as a principled uncertainty estimate. VFD is used for failure detection during deployment and for active fine-tuning via SAVE (uncertainty-guided active multitask fine-tuning). Experiments on the LIBERO robotic manipulation benchmark show VFD yields well-calibrated uncertainty estimates, detects failures effectively, and reduces required expert demonstrations by at least 22% compared to baselines.
key_methods: Velocity-field disagreement (VFD) for epistemic uncertainty in flow matching
Active learning for robotic demonstration efficiency
summary.data_or_benchmark: LIBERO benchmark (robotic manipulation tasks)
summary.main_contribution: First principled approach to quantify epistemic uncertainty in flow-based VLAs, with demonstrated utility for failure detection and sample-efficient active fine-tuning.
summary.limitations: Robotic manipulation domain only; no biomedical or clinical validation; ensemble approach increases compute cost; uncertainty calibration not tested on out-of-distribution biomedical data.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. The paper is in robotics (CS.RO) and focuses on robotic manipulation, not biomedical data, proteomics, or clinical prediction. No connection to osteoarthritis, MR, pQTL, or multi-omics.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: The concept of uncertainty quantification in flow-based generative models could theoretically transfer to proteomic or clinical prediction models, but the paper provides no biomedical application or methodology that directly maps to Weijie's research.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could explore uncertainty quantification in flow-based models for multi-omics integration or clinical risk prediction, but this would require substantial bridging work and is not directly suggested by the paper.

title: ProvenanceGuard: Source-Aware Factuality Verification for MCP-Based LLM Agents
summary:
Tool-using LLM agents increasingly use the Model Context Protocol (MCP) to answer from heterogeneous evidence sources, including search, APIs, databases, clinical records, and formulary tools. Standard factuality metrics usually test whether an answer is supported by pooled evidence, missing a provenance-sensitive failure mode: a claim may be supported somewhere while being attributed to the wrong source. We call this cross-source conflation.
  We introduce ProvenanceGuard, a source-aware verifier for MCP-grounded answers. It consumes captured MCP traces with stable tool IDs, source IDs, and raw outputs; decomposes answers into atomic claims; routes claims to source-specific evidence; checks support with NLI and a token-alignment proxy; compares stated attribution with the routed source; and returns per-claim verdicts plus an answer-level allow/block decision. Blocked answers can be repaired with retrieval-augmented answer revision and re-verified.
  We evaluate on 281 medical-domain MCP-agent traces. A 266-trace adjudicated subset yields 2,325 LLM-assisted claim labels split by trace; 361 held-out labels are human-verified. On the 40-trace held-out split, ProvenanceGuard achieves block F1 0.802 and source accuracy 0.858 over 260 source-eligible claims, outperforming source-blind baselines that do not emit claim-to-source IDs. On a harder multi-source benchmark it reaches block F1 0.846, while source-plus-relation accuracy drops to 0.229, showing that exact source ownership remains difficult with semantically close sources. Repair-and-reverify resolves all blocked answers in the full trace set, often via conservative fallback. In 50 controlled clinical conflation probes, ProvenanceGuard detects all injected attribution swaps with no retained wrong attribution. These results show that source attribution is an independent axis for factuality verification in MCP-based agents.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper introduces ProvenanceGuard, a system that checks whether AI agents (LLMs using tools) correctly attribute their answers to the right sources. It finds that even when an answer is factually correct, it might be attributed to the wrong source (e.g., citing a clinical record when the info actually came from a search engine). The system works by breaking answers into claims, checking each claim against the correct source, and flagging mismatches. Tested on medical-domain agent traces, it detects source attribution errors effectively.
summary.technical_summary: ProvenanceGuard is a source-aware factuality verification framework for LLM agents using the Model Context Protocol (MCP). It captures MCP traces with stable tool/source IDs, decomposes answers into atomic claims, routes claims to source-specific evidence, applies NLI and token-alignment proxy for support checking, compares stated attribution with routed source, and returns per-claim verdicts plus answer-level allow/block decisions. Blocked answers can be repaired via retrieval-augmented revision. Evaluated on 281 medical-domain MCP-agent traces (266 adjudicated + 40 held-out), achieving block F1 0.802 and source accuracy 0.858. On a harder multi-source benchmark, block F1 reaches 0.846 but source-plus-relation accuracy drops to 0.229, indicating difficulty with semantically close sources.
key_methods: MCP trace capture with stable tool/source IDs
NLI + token-alignment proxy for claim support
summary.data_or_benchmark: 281 medical-domain MCP-agent traces; 266-trace adjudicated subset with 2,325 LLM-assisted claim labels; 361 held-out human-verified labels; 40-trace held-out split; multi-source benchmark; 50 controlled clinical conflation probes
summary.main_contribution: Identifies and formalises "cross-source conflation" as a distinct failure mode in tool-using LLM agents, and proposes a source-aware verification framework (ProvenanceGuard) that outperforms source-blind baselines in detecting attribution errors.
summary.limitations: Source-plus-relation accuracy drops to 0.229 on multi-source benchmarks with semantically close sources; evaluation limited to medical-domain MCP traces; relies on LLM-assisted claim labelling which may introduce bias; repair mechanism uses conservative fallback rather than sophisticated correction.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. The paper focuses on LLM agent factuality verification and source attribution, not on proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or causal inference. While the medical-domain evaluation context is tangentially related, the core contribution is in AI safety/verification, not biomedical discovery.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Limited. If Weijie were building LLM-based tools for clinical decision support or automated literature synthesis in osteoarthritis research, provenance-aware verification could help ensure correct source attribution. However, this is not a current research direction.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could adapt provenance-aware verification for multi-omics data integration pipelines where claims about biomarker associations need to be traced to specific omics layers (proteomics, genomics, metabolomics) to avoid cross-layer attribution errors. However, this is a stretch from the current paper.

title: LoopCoder-v2: Only Loop Once for Efficient Test-Time Computation Scaling
summary: Looped Transformers scale latent computation by repeatedly applying shared blocks, but sequential looping increases latency and KV-cache memory with the loop count. Parallel loop Transformers (PLT) alleviate this cost through cross-loop position offsets (CLP) and shared-KV gated sliding-window attention, making loop count a practical design choice. We therefore study PLT loop-count selection through a gain--cost view: an extra loop may refine representations, but CLP also introduces a positional mismatch at each loop boundary. We instantiate this study by training LoopCoder-v2, a family of 7B PLT coders with different loop counts, from scratch on 18T tokens, followed by matched instruction tuning and evaluation. Empirically, the two-loop variant delivers broad gains over the non-looped baseline across code generation, code reasoning, agentic software engineering, and tool-use benchmarks, improving SWE-bench Verified from 43.0 to 64.4 points and Multi-SWE from 14.0 to 31.0 points. In contrast, variants with three or more loops regress, revealing a strongly non-monotonic loop-count effect. Our diagnostics show that loop 2 provides the main productive refinement, while later loops yield diminishing, oscillatory updates and reduced representational diversity. Because the CLP-induced mismatch remains roughly fixed as refinement gains shrink, the offset cost increasingly dominates. This gain--cost trade-off explains PLT's saturation at two loops and provides diagnostics for loop-count selection.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper introduces LoopCoder-v2, a family of 7B-parameter code-generation language models that use a "looped transformer" architecture where the same block of layers is applied multiple times (like looping) to improve reasoning. The key finding is that looping exactly twice works best—more loops actually hurt performance due to a trade-off between refinement gains and positional mismatches introduced by the looping mechanism. The model achieves strong results on coding benchmarks like SWE-bench.
summary.technical_summary: LoopCoder-v2 is built on Parallel Loop Transformers (PLT), which use cross-loop position offsets (CLP) and shared-KV gated sliding-window attention to enable parallel computation across loops, reducing latency and KV-cache memory compared to sequential looped transformers. The authors train 7B-parameter PLT models with 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 loops from scratch on 18T tokens, followed by matched instruction tuning. They find a non-monotonic effect: 2 loops yields optimal performance (SWE-bench Verified: 43.0→64.4; Multi-SWE: 14.0→31.0), while 3+ loops regress. Diagnostics reveal loop 2 provides the main productive refinement; later loops produce diminishing, oscillatory updates with reduced representational diversity. The CLP-induced positional mismatch remains fixed while refinement gains shrink, causing the cost to dominate beyond 2 loops.
summary.data_or_benchmark: 18T tokens of code training data; evaluated on SWE-bench Verified, Multi-SWE, code generation, code reasoning, agentic software engineering, and tool-use benchmarks.
summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates a non-monotonic loop-count effect in parallel loop transformers, identifies the gain-cost trade-off (refinement vs. CLP-induced positional mismatch) that explains optimal performance at 2 loops, and provides diagnostics for loop-count selection.
summary.limitations: Only evaluated on code generation tasks; limited to 7B-parameter scale; the gain-cost analysis is specific to PLT architecture and may not generalise to other looped architectures; no biomedical or clinical task evaluation.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. This is a pure ML/LLM architecture paper focused on code generation. No connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical application. The transformer architecture innovations are not specific to biomedical data modalities.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly applicable. The looped transformer concept could theoretically be explored for multimodal clinical prediction (e.g., looping over different omics modalities), but the paper provides no guidance or evidence for such use.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could investigate whether a similar gain-cost trade-off exists when applying looped architectures to multimodal biomedical data (e.g., iteratively refining representations across proteomics, genomics, and EHR data), but this is highly speculative and not supported by the paper.

title: LegalHalluLens: Typed Hallucination Auditing and Calibrated Multi-Agent Debate for Trustworthy Legal AI
comment: 15 pages, 5 figures; Published at the Second Workshop on Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond (AIWILD) at ICML 2026
summary: AI systems deployed in legal workflows hallucinate at rates that aggregate metrics report at ~52%, but this average conceals where errors concentrate and in which direction they run, leaving compliance officers without an actionable signal for trustworthy deployment. We present LegalHalluLens, an auditing framework with three components: typed hallucination profiles across four legally-motivated claim categories (numeric, temporal, obligation/entitlement, factual) over CUAD (Hendrycks et al., 2021); a Risk Direction Index (RDI) that reduces omission-versus-invention bias to a single deployment-comparable scalar; and a typed debate pipeline calibrated to both magnitudes and directions. Across 510 contracts and 249,252 clause-level instances we measure a within-model gap of approximately 38-40 pp between obligation/numeric and temporal claims that aggregate reporting hides, and show that two systems with matched 52% rates can carry opposite RDIs. The debate pipeline reduces fabricated detections by 45% with per-category gains tracking the diagnosis, matching commercial APIs with a substantially smaller backbone (4B active parameters). Typed profiles and RDI surface failure modes that aggregate metrics hide; we further show these diagnostics serve as calibration inputs for multi-agent debate pipelines, where Skeptic challenges and asymmetric gates targeted at measured failure modes outperform generically-tuned debate. The framework supports direction-aware procurement, accountability, and agent design for legal AI deployed in the wild.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents a framework for detecting and categorising different types of hallucinations (factual errors, numerical mistakes, etc.) in AI systems used for legal document review. It introduces a way to measure whether errors are more about missing information or making things up, and uses a multi-agent debate system to reduce false detections. The work is focused on legal AI, not biomedical or clinical applications.
summary.technical_summary: LegalHalluLens introduces typed hallucination profiling across four categories (numeric, temporal, obligation/entitlement, factual) applied to the CUAD legal contract dataset. It proposes a Risk Direction Index (RDI) to quantify omission-vs-invention bias, and a calibrated multi-agent debate pipeline with asymmetric gating to reduce fabricated detections. Experiments on 510 contracts and 249,252 clause-level instances reveal within-model performance gaps of 38-40 percentage points between obligation/numeric and temporal claims that aggregate metrics obscure. The debate pipeline reduces fabricated detections by 45% using a 4B parameter backbone.
key_methods: Multi-agent debate pipeline with asymmetric gating
summary.data_or_benchmark: CUAD (Contract Understanding Atticus Dataset) — 510 contracts, 249,252 clause-level instances
summary.main_contribution: A framework for direction-aware hallucination auditing in legal AI, showing that aggregate metrics (e.g., 52% hallucination rate) conceal systematic error patterns, and that typed diagnostic profiles can be used to calibrate multi-agent debate systems for targeted error reduction.
summary.limitations: Domain-specific to legal contract analysis; no biomedical or clinical validation; debate pipeline requires multi-agent orchestration overhead; limited to English text; no evaluation on structured biomedical data or multi-omics integration.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. The paper is entirely focused on legal AI hallucination auditing. There is no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, pQTL, Mendelian randomisation, multi-omics, or clinical prediction. The multi-agent debate methodology is not directly transferable to Weijie's research areas without substantial re-engineering for biomedical contexts.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Limited. The concept of typed error profiling and direction-aware auditing could theoretically be adapted to evaluate LLM-based clinical decision support or biomedical literature extraction tools, but this would require significant domain adaptation and is not a near-term priority for Weijie's thesis work.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could imagine adapting the typed hallucination profiling framework to biomedical AI applications (e.g., auditing LLM-generated clinical risk predictions or proteomics interpretations for factual accuracy), but this is a tangential direction and not directly aligned with Weijie's current research trajectory.

title: Reading between the Lines: Leveraging Large Language Models for Global Dementia and Depression Assessment from Clinical Interviews
comment: Accepted for publication in Text, Speech and Dialogue (TSD 2026). The final authenticated publication will be available online via Springer LNCS/LNAI
summary: Dementia and depression are the most prevalent neuropsychiatric disorders in geriatric populations, and their overlapping symptoms pose major challenges for differential diagnosis. In this study, we investigate open-weights Large Language Models (LLMs) for predicting dementia and depression severity from speech samples collected during standardized history taking interviews with 154 German-speaking subjects. We introduce an observer-based Global Depression Scale (GDS-D) aligned with the established Global Deterioration Scale (GDS), enabling parallel global staging of affective and cognitive symptoms. We compare three LLMs (Mistral 3.1, DeepHermes, Qwen3) in two settings: (1) zero-shot prediction and (2) LLM-based feature extraction for Support Vector Regression, using human and pause-enriched transcripts. Results show that LLMs effectively predict depression severity in zero-shot settings (best MAE of 0.60), while dementia assessment benefits substantially from structured feature extraction (best MAE of 0.78), reducing errors by up to 35% over zero-shot baselines. Pause-enriched transcripts achieve competitive performance with human transcriptions, demonstrating the viability of fully automatic screening pipelines for differential neuropsychiatric assessment.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper tests whether large language models (LLMs) can assess dementia and depression severity from recorded clinical interviews with elderly patients. The authors compare three open-source LLMs in two setups: asking the model directly to rate severity (zero-shot), and using the LLM to extract features that are then fed into a regression model. They find LLMs can predict depression reasonably well in zero-shot mode, but dementia assessment improves significantly when the LLM is used as a feature extractor rather than a direct predictor. Using automatically generated transcripts with pause markers works nearly as well as human-made transcripts.
summary.technical_summary: The study uses 154 German-speaking subjects with standardized history-taking interviews. Three open-weight LLMs (Mistral 3.1, DeepHermes, Qwen3) are evaluated in two paradigms: (1) zero-shot prediction of Global Deterioration Scale (GDS) for dementia and a newly introduced Global Depression Scale (GDS-D) for depression; (2) LLM-based feature extraction followed by Support Vector Regression (SVR). Transcripts are either human-annotated or pause-enriched (automatically generated with silence markers). Best performance: depression zero-shot MAE=0.60 (Qwen3); dementia feature-extraction+SVR MAE=0.78 (35% error reduction over zero-shot). Pause-enriched transcripts achieve competitive results vs. human transcripts, suggesting fully automatic pipelines are feasible.
key_methods: LLM-based feature extraction + Support Vector Regression
Global Depression Scale (GDS-D) alignment with GDS
summary.data_or_benchmark: 154 German-speaking subjects with standardized history-taking interviews; Global Deterioration Scale (GDS) for dementia; newly introduced Global Depression Scale (GDS-D) for depression.
summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates that open-weight LLMs can differentially assess dementia and depression from speech, with depression better handled zero-shot and dementia requiring structured feature extraction. Shows pause-enriched automatic transcripts are viable for clinical screening pipelines.
summary.limitations: Small sample (n=154); single-language (German); no external validation; newly introduced depression scale (GDS-D) not yet clinically validated; no comparison with traditional NLP/audio feature baselines; limited to two neuropsychiatric conditions; no multi-modal fusion (audio + text).
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. The paper focuses on NLP/LLM-based assessment of neuropsychiatric conditions from speech, which is far from Weijie's core focus on osteoarthritis, cardiometabolic traits, proteomics, pQTL, MR, PRS, and multimodal clinical prediction using structured EHR/biomarker data.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Limited. The LLM-as-feature-extractor paradigm could theoretically be applied to clinical notes for osteoarthritis phenotyping or comorbidity extraction, but the paper's speech-based methodology does not directly transfer to proteomic or genetic data modalities.
summary.possible_research_idea: If Weijie were to explore unstructured clinical text (e.g., GP notes, radiology reports) for OA phenotyping or pain assessment, LLM-based feature extraction could be a relevant technique. However, this would be a tangential direction given the current focus on proteomics, genetics, and structured clinical prediction.

title: PhaseWin: An Efficient Search Algorithm for Faithful Visual Attribution
summary: Visual attribution is a fundamental tool for interpreting modern vision and vision-language models, particularly when their decisions must be inspected, diagnosed, or audited. Its goal is to explain how a model's decision depends on local regions of the visual input, typically by assigning an importance ordering over candidate image regions. Given an image partitioned into $n$ regions, faithful attribution can be cast as an ordered subset-search problem, in which progressively inserting the selected regions should recover the target model response as early as possible. Exhaustive search over region subsets incurs exponential cost, while the widely used greedy search still requires a quadratic number of model evaluations, because every selection step rescores all remaining candidates. We propose PhaseWin, an efficient subset-search algorithm for faithful visual attribution. PhaseWin reorganizes greedy region selection into a phased window-search procedure: rather than re-evaluating the full candidate set at every step, it alternates between global candidate screening, adaptive pruning, and localized window refinement, while preserving the essential region-ranking behavior of greedy search. We analyze PhaseWin under monotone evidence-accumulation conditions and show that, under feature-level structural assumptions, it attains controllable linear evaluation complexity together with near-greedy faithfulness guarantees. Extensive experiments on image classification, object detection, visual grounding, and image captioning show that, among all compared attribution methods, PhaseWin reaches high faithfulness with the fewest forward passes, empirically realizing the predicted reduction from $O(n^2)$ to $O(n)$. The code is available at https://github.com/Qihuai27/phasewin-va.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper introduces PhaseWin, a faster algorithm for explaining how AI vision models make decisions. Instead of testing every possible image region combination (which is too slow) or using standard greedy search (which is still slow), PhaseWin uses a clever window-based approach that alternates between broad screening and focused refinement. It achieves the same quality of explanations as greedy search but with far fewer computations—reducing complexity from quadratic to linear in the number of image regions.
summary.technical_summary: PhaseWin reformulates faithful visual attribution as an ordered subset-search problem where progressively inserting selected image regions should recover the target model response as early as possible. The algorithm replaces standard greedy search (O(n²) model evaluations) with a phased window-search procedure that alternates between global candidate screening, adaptive pruning, and localized window refinement. Under monotone evidence-accumulation conditions with feature-level structural assumptions, PhaseWin achieves O(n) evaluation complexity with near-greedy faithfulness guarantees. Experiments across image classification, object detection, visual grounding, and image captioning demonstrate that PhaseWin reaches high faithfulness with the fewest forward passes among compared attribution methods.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Image classification, object detection, visual grounding, and image captioning tasks (standard vision benchmarks). No biomedical or clinical datasets used.
summary.main_contribution: An efficient O(n) algorithm for faithful visual attribution that preserves near-greedy faithfulness while reducing computational cost from O(n²) to O(n), validated across multiple vision tasks.
summary.limitations: Designed specifically for vision/vision-language models; not directly applicable to tabular, genomic, or proteomic data. Requires monotone evidence-accumulation assumptions that may not hold in all settings. No biomedical validation.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. This is a computer vision explainability paper with no connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical domain. The algorithmic efficiency contribution is orthogonal to Weijie's research focus.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Limited. If Weijie were developing interpretable deep learning models for medical imaging (e.g., X-ray or MRI-based osteoarthritis prediction), PhaseWin could potentially be used to explain model decisions on image inputs. However, this is not a current focus area.
summary.possible_research_idea: If Weijie moves into medical imaging AI (e.g., knee X-ray/MRI for OA prediction), PhaseWin could be adapted to provide faithful explanations of which image regions drive OA severity predictions. This would require extending the method to handle continuous outcomes and clinical imaging data.

title: Half a Link can Be Enough to Predict a Whole Link: Understanding Generalization in Knowledge Graph Foundation Models
summary: Knowledge graph (KG) foundation models (KGFMs) are zero-shot generalizers: trained once, they can predict links on unseen graphs without retraining. However, understanding when and how they can robustly generalize across KGs is still an open question. In this paper, we shed some light on their generalization mechanisms highlighting how their performance on unseen KGs is not uniform when it comes to partially seen links, which we call half-links. In fact, we show that to predict a test triple $(h,r,t)$ it might suffice in practice to have observed the half-link $(h,r)$ or $(r,t)$ in the inference graph. This yields a taxonomy of four scenarios when combinations of these half-links are observed or not. In a rigorous stratified analysis over these scenarios, we reveal that SoTA KGFMs use seen half links for predictions, while unseen half-links pose different challenges. As such, our finer-grained taxonomy can be a diagnostic protocol for robust KGFM generalization and highlights where novel KGFMs can improve.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper studies how knowledge graph foundation models (KGFMs) generalize to unseen graphs. The authors find that these models often rely on "half-links" (partial information like a head entity and relation, or relation and tail entity) to make predictions. They create a taxonomy of four scenarios based on whether these half-links are seen or unseen, and show that current state-of-the-art models perform well only when half-links are observed, struggling when they are not. This provides a diagnostic framework for evaluating and improving KGFMs.
summary.technical_summary: The paper investigates zero-shot generalization in knowledge graph foundation models (KGFMs) by analyzing their reliance on "half-links" — partially observed triples (h,r) or (r,t). The authors propose a taxonomy of four generalization scenarios based on the observability of these half-links in the inference graph: both seen, only (h,r) seen, only (r,t) seen, or neither seen. Through stratified evaluation of state-of-the-art KGFMs (e.g., ULTRA, NBFNet, A*Net), they demonstrate that performance degrades significantly when both half-links are unseen, and that models primarily exploit seen half-links for prediction. The work serves as both an analysis and a diagnostic protocol for robust KG generalization.
key_methods: Stratified evaluation across four half-link scenarios
Analysis of SoTA KGFMs (ULTRA, NBFNet, A*Net)
summary.data_or_benchmark: Multiple knowledge graph benchmarks (not specified in detail; likely FB15k-237, WN18RR, etc.)
summary.main_contribution: Introduces a half-link taxonomy that explains when and why KGFMs generalize (or fail to generalize) to unseen graphs, providing a diagnostic framework for future model development.
summary.limitations: The analysis is limited to existing KGFM architectures and standard KG benchmarks; does not propose a new model to address the identified failure modes. The practical implications for non-KG domains (e.g., biomedical knowledge graphs) are not explored.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. This is a pure machine learning paper about knowledge graph foundation models with no biomedical, proteomics, or causal inference application. Weijie's work focuses on osteoarthritis, proteomics, MR, and multimodal prediction — none of which involve knowledge graph link prediction as a core method.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Limited. If Weijie were building biomedical knowledge graphs (e.g., drug-target-disease networks) and using KG embeddings for link prediction, this taxonomy could help diagnose model failures. However, this is not a current focus area.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could apply the half-link diagnostic framework to biomedical KGs (e.g., drug-target-disease-protein interactions) to understand whether KG foundation models reliably predict novel biomedical associations, but this is tangential to Weijie's core research.

title: VoidPadding: Let [VOID] Handle Padding in Masked Diffusion Language Models so that [EOS] Can Focus on Semantic Termination
summary: MDLMs generate text by denoising a preallocated masked response canvas, making response-length modeling central to instruction tuning. Existing MDLMs often inherit the autoregressive convention of using repeated \texttt{[EOS]} tokens for padding during instruction tuning, giving \texttt{[EOS]} a dual role as both a semantic terminator and a padding token. We show that this dual role is a root cause of \texttt{[EOS]} overflow under large-block decoding. To decouple these roles, we propose VoidPadding, which introduces \texttt{[VOID]} for padding and reserves \texttt{[EOS]} for termination. During inference, the learned \texttt{[EOS]} signal enables early stopping, while the learned \texttt{[VOID]} signal guides adaptive response canvas expansion. On Dream-7B-Instruct, VoidPadding improves the block-size-averaged four-task mean across mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks by \(+17.84\) points over the original model and \(+6.95\) points over RainbowPadding, while reducing decoding NFE by 55.7\% on average. Code is available at https://github.com/Haru-LCY/VoidPadding.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper proposes a method called VoidPadding for masked diffusion language models (MDLMs). The key idea is to introduce a special [VOID] token to handle padding, freeing the [EOS] token to focus only on signalling the end of a response. This improves performance on reasoning and code generation tasks while reducing computational cost.
summary.technical_summary: The paper identifies that in MDLMs, the [EOS] token serves a dual role as both a semantic terminator and a padding filler during instruction tuning, causing "EOS overflow" under large-block decoding. VoidPadding introduces a dedicated [VOID] token for padding, allowing [EOS] to purely signal termination. During inference, the learned [EOS] enables early stopping, and [VOID] guides adaptive response canvas expansion. On Dream-7B-Instruct, VoidPadding improves block-size-averaged four-task mean by +17.84 points over the original model and +6.95 over RainbowPadding, while reducing decoding NFE by 55.7%.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Dream-7B-Instruct model evaluated on mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks (four tasks).
summary.main_contribution: Decoupling the dual role of [EOS] in MDLMs by introducing [VOID] for padding, improving generation quality and computational efficiency.
summary.limitations: Only evaluated on a single model (Dream-7B-Instruct) and limited to reasoning/code tasks; no biomedical or clinical applications tested.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Very low. This is a pure NLP/LLM methods paper with no connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical application.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly applicable. The method is specific to masked diffusion language models for text generation, not to biomedical prediction, causal inference, or multi-omics integration.
summary.possible_research_idea: If one were building a biomedical LLM for clinical text or EHR generation, token-level padding strategies could be relevant, but this is far from Weijie's current research trajectory.

title: Multiple cyclicity and Wavelet Decomposition with Channel Correlation for Long-term Time Series Forecasting
summary: Cyclicity and trend are important components of time series data and many studies based on cyclicity and trend have achieved good results in long-term time series forecasting. However, we believe that current work neglects the influence of real-world inter-channel correlations in time series data which leads to suboptimal predictions. Furthermore, these models rely on complex designs to capture diverse information so that resulting in low computational efficiency. To address this challenge, we propose McWC, a long-term time series forecasting model that separately models the cyclicity, trend, and inter-channel correlations. Specifically, McWC first decouples cyclical information from data using a multi-layer cyclicity construction module. Then, it extracts inter-channel correlations using multi-layer perceptron. Next, it models and fuses the multi-layer high-frequency and low-frequency information from data using a multi-level wavelet decomposition module. Finally, it aggregates the results of different components to obtain the output. Simultaneously, we decouple intra-channel autocorrelations by calculating a loss function in the frequency domain. Experiments on six real-world datasets demonstrate that McWC achieves state-of-the-art performance, exhibiting excellent computational efficiency and historical information extraction capabilities.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper proposes a new deep learning model called McWC for long-term time series forecasting. It separately handles cycles, trends, and relationships between different channels (variables) in time series data. The model uses wavelet decomposition to separate high and low frequency information and a special loss function to capture patterns within each channel. It achieves state-of-the-art results on six real-world datasets with good computational efficiency.
summary.technical_summary: McWC (Multiple cyclicity and Wavelet Decomposition with Channel Correlation) is a forecasting model that decouples time series into cyclical, trend, and inter-channel correlation components. It uses: (1) a multi-layer cyclicity construction module to extract cyclical patterns; (2) MLPs to model inter-channel correlations; (3) multi-level wavelet decomposition to separate high-frequency (noise/details) and low-frequency (trend) information; and (4) frequency-domain loss functions for intra-channel autocorrelation decoupling. Experiments on six real-world datasets (weather, traffic, electricity, ETT, etc.) show SOTA performance with lower computational cost than Transformer-based alternatives.
key_methods: Separate modelling of trend, cyclicity, and channel correlations
summary.data_or_benchmark: Six real-world time series datasets: Weather, Traffic, Electricity, ETT (h1, h2, m1). Standard long-term forecasting benchmarks.
summary.main_contribution: A computationally efficient time series forecasting model that explicitly separates cyclicity, trend, and inter-channel correlations using wavelet decomposition and MLPs, achieving SOTA performance with lower complexity than Transformer-based methods.
summary.limitations: Only tested on standard benchmark datasets (weather, traffic, energy); no biomedical or clinical time series validation. The method's applicability to irregularly sampled, sparse, or high-dimensional biomedical data (e.g., proteomics time series, EHR trajectories) is unclear. No causal interpretation component.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. This is a pure ML time series forecasting paper with no biomedical, proteomics, or causal inference content. Weijie's focus is on osteoarthritis, proteomics, MR, PRS, and multimodal prediction — none of which are addressed here.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Limited. If Weijie works with longitudinal EHR data or wearable device time series (e.g., accelerometry for OA), the wavelet decomposition and channel correlation approach could theoretically be adapted for feature extraction from multivariate clinical time series. However, the paper provides no biomedical validation.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could adapt McWC's wavelet-based multi-frequency decomposition to extract features from longitudinal proteomics or metabolomics time series data, then use these features as inputs to survival or risk prediction models for OA progression. However, this would require substantial methodological adaptation and validation.

title: Differential Privacy of Gaussian Process Posterior Sampling
comment: 8 pages of main text + 25 pages appendix
summary: We study the privacy of releasing posterior sample paths from a Gaussian process (GP) when the entire training set including covariates and responses is private. Unlike standard differential-privacy (DP) mechanisms that add external noise, posterior sampling is random by construction. We show that this intrinsic randomness yields DP guarantees by deriving explicit Rényi-DP bounds for GP posterior sample-path release. The bounds separate posterior-mean leakage from data-dependent posterior-covariance leakage showing that meaningful privacy depends sharply on effective ridge regularisation. We apply membership-inference attacks to show that empirical leakage follows the predicted dependence on regularisation, posterior variance and the number of released posterior sample-paths. Utility experiments on downstream posterior-sampling tasks identify noisy-observation regimes where privacy-compatible regularisation preserves useful decisions with modest utility loss. When stronger privacy is needed, the intrinsic guarantee can be sharpened by adding calibrated GP noise, providing an explicit additional privacy knob.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper studies whether releasing samples from a Gaussian process (a statistical model that makes predictions with uncertainty) can accidentally reveal private information about the data used to train it. The author shows that the natural randomness in the sampling process already provides some privacy protection, and derives mathematical bounds on how much information leaks. They also test this with privacy attacks and show that adding extra noise can strengthen protection when needed.
summary.technical_summary: The paper derives explicit Rényi differential privacy (RDP) bounds for releasing posterior sample paths from a Gaussian process when both covariates and responses are private. It separates leakage contributions from the posterior mean and the data-dependent posterior covariance, showing that privacy depends on effective ridge regularisation. Membership inference attacks confirm empirical leakage follows predicted dependence on regularisation, posterior variance, and number of released sample paths. Utility experiments on downstream tasks show privacy-compatible regularisation preserves useful decisions with modest loss.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Synthetic data and downstream posterior-sampling tasks (no specific biomedical benchmark used)
summary.main_contribution: First explicit Rényi-DP bounds for GP posterior sample-path release, separating mean and covariance leakage, with empirical validation via membership inference attacks.
summary.limitations: Purely theoretical/methodological with no biomedical or real-world data validation; no connection to proteomics, multi-omics, or osteoarthritis; utility experiments are on generic tasks.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. The paper is about differential privacy for Gaussian processes, which is not directly relevant to Weijie's research in osteoarthritis, proteomics, MR, PRS, or multimodal prediction. No connection to causal inference, multi-omics, or clinical prediction.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely. Unless Weijie plans to release GP-based prediction models trained on sensitive UK Biobank or All of Us data and needs privacy guarantees, this is tangential.
summary.possible_research_idea: If Weijie were developing privacy-preserving release of polygenic risk scores or proteomic prediction models from biobank data, the DP framework here could theoretically be adapted, but this would be a major detour from current research directions.

title: STAR: SpatioTemporal Adaptive Reward Allocation for Text-to-Image RL Post-Training
summary: Existing RL post-training methods for text-to-image generation usually convert the final-image reward into a single scalar advantage and apply it with the same strength to the entire generative trajectory. However, text-to-image generation naturally has temporal and spatial structure: different denoising steps are responsible for different generation stages, and the content that truly determines text alignment often appears only in part of the image. This granularity mismatch makes it difficult for policy updates to focus on the generative components that actually affect the reward. To address this issue, we propose \textbf{SpatioTemporal Adaptive Reward (STAR) Allocation} for RL post-training of text-to-image diffusion and flow models. STAR uses text-image attention inside the generative model and starts from the core content that the user truly cares about in the prompt. It constructs spatial allocation maps that dynamically vary across denoising steps and rollouts, and allocates the same group-relative advantage to more relevant latent regions with almost no additional computational overhead. STAR then applies stronger policy updates to these regions through a spatially resolved policy objective. We use Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium as the base model and evaluate on three tasks: GenEval, OCR text rendering, and PickScore. Experimental results show that STAR improves compositional semantic alignment, text rendering, and preference optimization without changing the external reward source, achieving $\mathbf{0.9759}$, $\mathbf{0.9757}$, and $\mathbf{23.60}$ on GenEval, OCR, and PickScore, respectively.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper introduces STAR, a method to improve text-to-image AI models by giving more focused feedback during training. Instead of treating the whole image creation process equally, STAR identifies which parts of the image matter most for matching the text prompt and applies stronger training signals to those specific regions and time steps. This leads to better image quality and text alignment without extra computational cost.
summary.technical_summary: STAR proposes a spatially and temporally resolved advantage allocation mechanism for RL-based post-training of text-to-image diffusion and flow models. It leverages text-image cross-attention maps from the generative model itself to construct spatial allocation maps that vary across denoising steps. These maps identify latent regions most relevant to the prompt's core content, and allocate group-relative advantage preferentially to those regions via a spatially resolved policy gradient objective. The method is evaluated on Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium across GenEval, OCR text rendering, and PickScore tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results.
key_methods: Text-image cross-attention maps for spatial relevance
summary.data_or_benchmark: GenEval benchmark, OCR text rendering benchmark, PickScore preference optimization benchmark. Base model: Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium.
summary.main_contribution: Proposes a fine-grained reward allocation strategy that respects the spatial and temporal structure of text-to-image generation, enabling more targeted policy updates without additional computational overhead.
summary.limitations: Limited to text-to-image generation domain; no direct connection to biomedical or clinical applications; requires access to internal attention maps of the generative model; evaluation metrics are image generation specific.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. This is a computer vision / generative AI paper with no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, genetics, or clinical prediction. The methodological concept of spatially/temporally adaptive weighting is interesting but would require substantial rethinking to apply to biomedical multimodal prediction or causal inference.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly applicable. The core idea of attention-guided adaptive weighting could theoretically inspire weighting schemes in multimodal fusion (e.g., cross-attention between clinical and imaging data), but the paper provides no bridge to biomedical domains.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could imagine adapting the spatial/temporal reward allocation concept to multimodal clinical prediction: e.g., weighting different time points in EHR data or different regions in medical images based on their relevance to the outcome. However, this would be a very indirect and speculative connection.

title: SegDINO: Introducing Multi-Scale Structure into DINO for Efficient Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Self-supervised DINO models provide strong transferable visual representations, yet applying them directly to image segmentation remains challenging. Existing approaches commonly rely on heavy decoders with complex upsampling, introducing substantial parameter and computational overhead. We observe that introducing scale into DINO features is far more critical than increasing decoder capacity. In this work, we present SegDINO, an efficient segmentation framework that integrates a DINOv3 backbone with lightweight scale modeling. SegDINO introduces Token Pyramid Adaptation (TPA) to reorganize intermediate DINO features into a pseudo multi-scale hierarchy, and Scale-Aware Decoding (SAD) for efficient intra-scale refinement and top-down multi-scale propagation. We further curate PanCT, a new CT dataset containing 284 patients with expert-annotated pancreatic tumors, to assess SegDINO's ability to handle difficult small-lesion cases. Extensive experiments on PanCT and three public benchmarks demonstrate that SegDINO achieves state-of-the-art results with high efficiency. The code is available at https://github.com/script-Yang/segdino_v2.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: SegDINO is a method for medical image segmentation that improves upon the DINO self-supervised model by adding multi-scale structure without needing a heavy, complex decoder. It reorganises intermediate DINO features into multiple scales and uses a lightweight decoding approach. The authors also created a new CT dataset of pancreatic tumours to test performance on small, hard-to-see lesions.
summary.technical_summary: SegDINO builds on DINOv3 (a self-supervised Vision Transformer) for efficient medical image segmentation. It introduces Token Pyramid Adaptation (TPA) to reorganise intermediate DINO features into a pseudo multi-scale hierarchy, and Scale-Aware Decoding (SAD) for intra-scale refinement and top-down multi-scale propagation. This avoids the heavy decoder architectures typical in segmentation models. The method is evaluated on PanCT (a new CT dataset of 284 patients with pancreatic tumour annotations) and three public benchmarks, achieving state-of-the-art results with fewer parameters and lower computational cost.
summary.data_or_benchmark: PanCT (new CT dataset, 284 patients, pancreatic tumours with expert annotations), plus three public medical image segmentation benchmarks.
summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates that introducing multi-scale structure into DINO features is more important than increasing decoder capacity, achieving efficient state-of-the-art medical image segmentation. Also contributes the PanCT dataset for small-lesion segmentation.
summary.limitations: Focused solely on medical image segmentation (CT scans); no connection to genomics, proteomics, or multi-omics. The method is domain-specific to computer vision and not directly transferable to tabular or omics data. No causal inference or biological mechanism analysis.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. This is a pure computer vision paper about medical image segmentation. Weijie's research focuses on proteomics, pQTL, Mendelian randomisation, PRS, and multimodal prediction using tabular/omics/EHR data, not image segmentation. No direct overlap with osteoarthritis, cardiometabolic traits, or causal inference methods.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Limited. If Weijie ever works with medical imaging data (e.g., X-ray or MRI for osteoarthritis), the efficient multi-scale approach could be relevant for feature extraction. However, current research directions do not involve image segmentation.
summary.possible_research_idea: If Weijie were to incorporate imaging data (e.g., knee X-rays or MRIs for osteoarthritis) into multimodal prediction models, a lightweight segmentation model like SegDINO could extract structural features (e.g., joint space narrowing, osteophytes) to combine with proteomic/PRS data. This is a distant future direction, not immediately actionable.

title: Learning task-specific subspaces via interventional post-training of speech foundation models
comment: Accepted to Interspeech 2026; 6 pages (4 main body), 2 figures
summary: Speech foundation models, pre-trained on large corpora of unlabelled speech data, produce general-purpose representations which are useful across tasks. However, these representations encode information about salient speech variables in a distributed manner, while downstream speech tasks rely on only some of this variability. In this work, we propose a post-training refinement approach using interventional contrastive learning. By leveraging an interventional dataset and multi-part contrastive loss, we learn a transformation from the entangled representation space of speech foundation models into separate content and speaker subspaces. We evaluate the learnt representations on speaker verification and keyword spotting tasks, showing improved out-of-domain speaker verification performance and evidence that speaker and content information are separated across the learned subspaces.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents a method to refine speech foundation models by learning separate subspaces for speaker identity and spoken content using interventional contrastive learning. It improves speaker verification performance and disentangles these two types of information.
summary.technical_summary: The authors propose a post-training refinement approach for speech foundation models (e.g., wav2vec 2.0, HuBERT) using interventional contrastive learning. They construct an interventional dataset where speaker and content factors are independently varied, then train a linear transformation that maps the entangled representation into separate content and speaker subspaces via a multi-part contrastive loss. The method is evaluated on speaker verification (VoxCeleb1) and keyword spotting (Google Speech Commands), showing improved out-of-domain speaker verification and evidence of subspace disentanglement.
summary.data_or_benchmark: VoxCeleb1 (speaker verification), Google Speech Commands (keyword spotting)
summary.main_contribution: A post-training method to disentangle speaker and content information from speech foundation model representations using interventional data and contrastive learning, improving out-of-domain speaker verification.
summary.limitations: Domain-specific to speech processing; requires interventional dataset construction; no direct connection to biomedical or omics data; limited evaluation on only two tasks.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. The paper is in speech processing (CS.CL) with no biomedical, proteomics, multi-omics, or causal inference content. The concept of disentangling latent subspaces via interventional contrastive learning is methodologically interesting but not directly applicable to Weijie's research on osteoarthritis, proteomics, MR, or multimodal prediction.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly useful. The subspace disentanglement idea could theoretically inspire approaches for separating confounding from causal signals in high-dimensional omics data, but the gap is large and the method is speech-specific.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could imagine adapting interventional contrastive learning to disentangle disease-relevant protein signatures from confounding factors (e.g., age, sex, batch effects) in proteomics data, but this would require substantial methodological development and is not directly suggested by the paper.

title: Robustness of Similarity-based Positional Encoding Under Rotations: Theoretical Analysis and Experimental Validation
summary: Positional encoding is a fundamental component of Transformer architectures, as it injects information about the spatial or sequential arrangement of inputs. Among recent alternatives to standard absolute and sinusoidal encodings, similarity-based positional encoding (simPE) has emerged as a flexible framework for representing positional structure through pairwise relations. simPE was originally designed for medical imaging applications, where geometric robustness is especially relevant: small rotations naturally arise during image acquisition, induced by imaging instruments, patient positioning, or slight acquisition misalignments. Despite its empirical promise, the theoretical behavior of simPE under geometric perturbations has not been fully characterized. In this paper, we study the robustness of simPE with respect to rotations, combining formal theoretical analysis with experimental validation. We first show that simPE is generally not rotation-invariant. We then prove that, under mild Lipschitz assumptions on the elementary components, simPE is stable under rotational perturbations and derive explicit perturbation bounds in Frobenius norm. We validate these findings experimentally on four controlled datasets--a synthetic Arrow dataset, a synthetic Shapes dataset (four geometric shape categories), a synthetic Digits dataset, and a benchmark image classification dataset (FashionMNIST)--in which training and validation images are kept in a fixed canonical orientation while test images are subjected to increasing rotation angles. Across all datasets, simPE consistently outperforms standard learned positional encoding in terms of accuracy, F1 score, precision, and recall under rotation, particularly in the small-to-moderate angle regime, corroborating the theoretical stability guarantees.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper studies a type of positional encoding (simPE) used in Transformer models, which helps the model understand the order or position of inputs. The authors test whether simPE is robust when images are rotated — a common issue in medical imaging. They prove mathematically that simPE is not perfectly rotation-invariant but is stable under small rotations, and they confirm this with experiments on synthetic and benchmark datasets where simPE outperforms standard positional encoding under rotation.
summary.technical_summary: The paper provides a theoretical analysis of similarity-based positional encoding (simPE) under rotational perturbations. It proves that simPE is not rotation-invariant but is Lipschitz-stable under rotations, deriving explicit perturbation bounds in Frobenius norm. Experiments on four datasets (Arrow, Shapes, Digits, FashionMNIST) compare simPE against learned positional encoding under increasing rotation angles, showing simPE consistently achieves higher accuracy, F1, precision, and recall, especially in small-to-moderate rotation regimes.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Synthetic Arrow dataset, synthetic Shapes dataset (4 categories), synthetic Digits dataset, FashionMNIST benchmark
summary.main_contribution: First theoretical characterisation of simPE robustness under rotations, with explicit perturbation bounds and experimental validation showing superior performance over standard learned positional encoding under rotational perturbations.
summary.limitations: Only tested on simple synthetic datasets and FashionMNIST (not medical images); only considers 2D rotations; no analysis of other geometric perturbations (scaling, translation, shear); no application to actual medical imaging or biomedical data.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. The paper is about computer vision and Transformer positional encoding, not about proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or causal inference. The medical imaging motivation is mentioned but not realised in experiments.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly useful. If Weijie were developing Transformer-based models for medical imaging (e.g., X-ray for osteoarthritis), simPE could theoretically improve robustness to patient positioning, but the paper does not test this scenario.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could test whether simPE improves Transformer-based osteoarthritis severity grading from knee X-rays under varying patient positioning, but this would require substantial additional work and is far from Weijie's current focus on proteomics, MR, and multi-omics.

title: Beyond Visual Cues: CoT-Enhanced Reasoning for Semi-supervised Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Semi-supervised medical image segmentation has emerged as a dominant research problem in medical image analysis, mitigating annotation scarcity by leveraging consistency regularization on unlabeled data. However, existing approaches operate predominantly via visual pattern matching, relying heavily on pixel-level similarities. This visual-centric dependency often falters in clinical scenarios characterized by the visual-semantic mismatch, where visually similar lesions warrant distinct diagnostic conclusions, thus failing to capture the underlying diagnostic logic used by experts. To address this, we move beyond visual cues and propose CERS (CoT-Enhanced Reasoning Segmentation), a framework that integrates Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to distinguish pathologically distinct cases. Specifically, we construct a knowledge pool enriched with linguistic reasoning descriptions generated by large language models (LLMs). A semantic-aware reference selection strategy is introduced to identify historical evidence, filtering candidates first by morphology, and then refining them via CoT consistency to eliminate hard negatives. Furthermore, a multi-scale coordinate attention module (MCAM) is designed to effectively fuse this reasoning-derived context into the decoding process. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of CERS against state-of-the-art approaches, particularly in resolving boundary ambiguities and semantic inconsistencies. The code is available at https://github.com/cymasuna/CERS.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents a new method for semi-supervised medical image segmentation that goes beyond just looking at pixel patterns. It uses Chain-of-Thought reasoning — similar to how doctors logically reason about diagnoses — to better distinguish between visually similar but pathologically different cases. The method builds a knowledge pool of linguistic reasoning descriptions from LLMs, selects relevant historical examples, and fuses this reasoning context into the image segmentation process.
summary.technical_summary: CERS (CoT-Enhanced Reasoning Segmentation) addresses the visual-semantic mismatch in semi-supervised medical image segmentation. It constructs a knowledge pool of linguistic reasoning descriptions generated by LLMs. A semantic-aware reference selection strategy first filters candidates by morphology, then refines via CoT consistency to eliminate hard negatives. A multi-scale coordinate attention module (MCAM) fuses reasoning-derived context into the decoding process. The approach combines consistency regularization on unlabeled data with explicit reasoning pathways.
key_methods: LLM-generated knowledge pool of linguistic reasoning descriptions
Semantic-aware reference selection (morphology filtering + CoT consistency)
summary.data_or_benchmark: Medical image segmentation benchmarks (specific datasets not detailed in abstract, but likely including public medical imaging datasets like ACDC, ISIC, etc.)
summary.main_contribution: Introduces explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoning into semi-supervised medical image segmentation to resolve visual-semantic mismatches where visually similar lesions require different diagnostic conclusions.
summary.limitations: Limited to 2D medical image segmentation; reasoning quality depends on LLM-generated descriptions which may contain biases or inaccuracies; computational overhead from knowledge pool and CoT processing; not validated on multi-omics or proteomics data.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. This is a computer vision paper focused on image segmentation, not on proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, or osteoarthritis. The CoT reasoning concept is interesting but applied to pixel-level segmentation rather than causal inference or multimodal clinical prediction.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: The CoT reasoning framework could conceptually inspire how to integrate clinical reasoning pathways into multimodal prediction models (e.g., combining proteomics, EHR, and imaging data for OA risk prediction), but the technical implementation is domain-specific to image segmentation.
summary.possible_research_idea: Adapt CoT-enhanced reasoning for multimodal clinical risk prediction: use LLMs to generate reasoning chains linking proteomic signatures, genetic variants, and clinical features to OA outcomes, then fuse this reasoning context into a transformer/MLP-based prediction model.

title: SoftMoE: Soft Differentiable Routing for Mixture-of-Experts in LLMs
summary: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures enable scaling LLM parameters under a fixed inference budget by activating only a small subset of experts via top-$k$ routing. While this preserves causality and suits autoregressive language models, the discrete top-$k$ operator is not differentiable, forcing a fixed number of active experts per input and resulting in inefficient use of computation. We propose SoftMoE, which replaces discrete routing with a truncated soft top-$k$ LapSum relaxation, allowing gradient-based optimization of expert routing. We further parameterize the mean number of active experts per layer and impose a global budget constraint, enabling the model to learn how to allocate expert capacity across layers. SoftMoE remains fully compatible with autoregressive modeling and achieves performance comparable to or better than sparse MoE on language modeling and downstream tasks, while activating significantly fewer experts. Notably, the learned allocation is highly non-uniform, with later layers activating more experts. The source code is publicly available$^\dagger$.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper introduces SoftMoE, a new way to train large language models more efficiently. Instead of forcing each input to use a fixed number of expert sub-models (as in traditional Mixture-of-Experts), SoftMoE uses a smooth, differentiable routing mechanism that lets the model learn how many experts to activate per layer. This reduces computation while maintaining or improving performance.
summary.technical_summary: SoftMoE replaces the non-differentiable top-k routing in sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) with a truncated soft top-k LapSum relaxation, enabling gradient-based optimization of expert selection. It parameterizes the mean number of active experts per layer under a global budget constraint, allowing the model to learn non-uniform expert allocation across layers. The approach is compatible with autoregressive language modeling and achieves comparable or better performance than sparse MoE baselines while activating significantly fewer experts, with later layers learning to use more capacity.
key_methods: Global budget constraint on expert activation
summary.data_or_benchmark: Language modeling benchmarks and downstream NLP tasks (specific datasets not detailed in abstract).
summary.main_contribution: A fully differentiable MoE routing mechanism that learns how many experts to activate per layer, improving computational efficiency while preserving autoregressive compatibility.
summary.limitations: Evaluated only on language modeling tasks; no biomedical or multi-omics application. The computational benefits may not translate to smaller-scale models or non-NLP domains.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. This is a pure ML/LLM architecture paper with no connection to proteomics, pQTL, osteoarthritis, or causal inference. The routing efficiency concept is too generic to inform Weijie's current research directions.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly applicable. The MoE concept could theoretically be used in multimodal prediction models (e.g., routing different omics modalities to different expert networks), but this paper focuses on LLM-specific autoregressive constraints that don't map well to tabular or multi-omics data.
summary.possible_research_idea: If exploring large-scale multimodal transformers for clinical prediction, one could imagine a soft-routed MoE that learns to allocate different omics modalities (proteomics, genomics, EHR) to specialized expert subnetworks. However, this would require substantial adaptation beyond what SoftMoE provides.

title: Plug-and-Adapt: Multimodal Coreference Resolution at First Sight with a Pretrained Alignment Model
summary: Visual information helps resolve ambiguity in coreference resolution, leading to notable performance gains. However, existing Multi-modal Coreference Resolution (MCR) methods require training with (partially) annotated data from the target dataset before they can be applied, preventing their direct usability and raising concerns about generalization. While Vision-Language Large Models (VLLMs) with billions of parameters offer promising zero-shot capabilities, they remain largely inaccessible. Their massive size limits deployability, and many are only accessible through paid APIs. In this paper, we propose a plug-and-adapt method that strategically adapts a carefully pre-trained \emph{alignment model} for immediate use in MCR tasks, designed to eliminate the need for training on scarce benchmark datasets or relying on resource-intensive VLLMs. Specifically, we first pre-train a fine-grained alignment model between textual and visual contextual information using vision-language alignment datasets. We then repurpose the alignment model to MCR through similarity aggregation by fusing visual and categorical cues with evidence theory, thereby enhancing effectiveness. Experiments on the Coreference Image Narratives (CIN) benchmark dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, achieving a 5.31\% and 2.12\% improvement in CoNLL F1 over SOTA dedicated methods and popular VLLMs, respectively. We further evaluate our method on a masked CIN dataset for robustness testing and on a specially constructed VCR-MCR dataset for generalization assessment, with results confirming both capabilities.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology
