summary.possible_research_idea: If one were to stretch: the idea of reconstructing structured biomedical documents (e.g., clinical trial reports, EHR notes, or biomedical literature) into layout-faithful formats for domain-specific LLM pretraining could be analogous. However, this is far from Weijie's core research direction.

title: Multi-Source Cybersecurity Logs: An ATT&CK-Labeled Dataset and SLM Evaluation
summary: Multi-stage cyberattacks span system, network, and browser logs. Detecting them requires correlating events across all three sources. Machine learning methods can learn these cross-source patterns, but they need labeled multi-source data. Existing public datasets fall short. Network-only datasets such as CICIDS and UNSW-NB15 miss host and browser activity. Host-focused datasets such as LMDG and CICAPT-IIoT lack browser telemetry. ATLAS includes all three sources but labels events only as malicious or benign, without MITRE Adversarial Tactics, Techniques, and Common Knowledge (ATT&CK) technique granularity. No public dataset combines all three sources with per-entry ATT&CK technique labels. We close the gap by building a multi-source log dataset of 870 sessions (70 attack, 800 benign) and approximately 2.3 million events. We captured system, network, and browser activity simultaneously on Windows endpoints. We labeled malicious events with ATT&CK technique IDs, covering 12 tactics and 53 techniques. We generated all attack data using real tools, including Remote Access Trojan (RAT), Command and Control (C2) tunnels, and cloud exfiltration. To demonstrate learnability, we fine-tuned three Small Language Models (SLMs) (Qwen2.5-1.5B, Llama-3.2-3B, Phi-4-Mini) using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). We compared each against its base variant across ten metrics on two tasks: chunk classification and ATT&CK technique identification. Fine-tuning improved every model on every metric. Chunk classification accuracy rose from approximately 8% in the base variants to between 90% and 97% after fine-tuning. Technique identification remained challenging, with the best exact-match accuracy at 42%, although high partial-match scores show the models captured most of the underlying reasoning.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper creates a new cybersecurity dataset that combines system logs, network logs, and browser logs to help detect multi-stage cyberattacks. The authors collected 870 sessions (70 attacks, 800 normal) with about 2.3 million events, labeling each malicious event with specific MITRE ATT&CK technique IDs. They also tested three small language models (SLMs) on this data, showing that fine-tuning dramatically improved chunk classification accuracy from ~8% to 90-97%, though exact technique identification remained difficult (best 42%).
summary.technical_summary: The paper addresses the gap in multi-source cybersecurity log datasets by constructing a dataset spanning system (Windows ETW), network (Zeek), and browser (Chrome devtools) logs. The dataset comprises 870 sessions with ~2.3M events, labeled per-entry with 53 MITRE ATT&CK technique IDs across 12 tactics. Attacks were generated using real tools (RAT, C2 tunnels, cloud exfiltration). The authors fine-tuned three SLMs (Qwen2.5-1.5B, Llama-3.2-3B, Phi-4-Mini) using LoRA on two tasks: chunk-level binary classification (attack vs benign) and multi-label ATT&CK technique identification. Fine-tuning improved chunk classification accuracy from ~8% to 90-97%. Technique identification exact-match accuracy reached only 42%, though partial-match scores indicated substantial reasoning capture.
key_methods: Multi-source log collection (system ETW + network Zeek + browser Chrome devtools)
MITRE ATT&CK technique labeling (53 techniques, 12 tactics)
Small Language Model fine-tuning with LoRA
Chunk classification and multi-label technique identification tasks
summary.data_or_benchmark: New multi-source cybersecurity log dataset: 870 sessions (70 attack, 800 benign), ~2.3M events, labeled with 53 ATT&CK technique IDs across 12 tactics.
summary.main_contribution: First public dataset combining system, network, and browser logs with per-entry MITRE ATT&CK technique labels, plus benchmark results showing SLMs can learn cross-source attack patterns.
summary.limitations: Technique identification accuracy remains low (42% exact match); dataset limited to Windows endpoints; only 70 attack sessions; no biomedical or clinical application.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: No direct relevance. The paper is in cybersecurity (CS.CR), not biomedical or epidemiological research. No connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or causal inference.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: None. The dataset, methods, and research questions are entirely outside Weijie's domain of genetic epidemiology, osteoarthritis, and multi-omics prediction.
summary.possible_research_idea: No viable research ideas for Weijie's context. The paper's focus on multi-source log correlation and SLM fine-tuning for attack detection does not translate to proteomics, PRS, or clinical prediction.

title: A Diffusion Approximation for Temporal-Difference Learning with Linear Features under Markovian Noise
summary: Temporal difference (TD) learning with linear function approximation is a core method for policy evaluation. Its classical continuous-time description is an ordinary differential equation (ODE), which captures the asymptotic mean dynamics but neglects stochastic fluctuations determining the error floor. We introduce a stochastic differential equation (SDE) approximation for linear TD(0) under Markovian noise. The resulting model distinguishes the contraction dynamics governed by the projected Bellman operator from the influence of Markovian sampling. As a consequence, the model explains the constant-stepsize error floor through the interaction between Markovian long-run covariance and the contraction geometry of the projected Bellman operator.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper develops a mathematical model (a stochastic differential equation) to better understand how temporal-difference learning works when the data is not independent but comes from a Markov chain (a sequence where each step depends on the previous one). It explains why the learning error doesn't go to zero even with constant step sizes, linking this to the interaction between sampling noise and the underlying contraction dynamics.
summary.technical_summary: The authors propose a stochastic differential equation (SDE) approximation for linear TD(0) under Markovian noise, going beyond the classical ODE approximation that only captures mean dynamics. The SDE model distinguishes the contraction dynamics of the projected Bellman operator from the influence of Markovian sampling. It shows that the constant-stepsize error floor arises from the interaction between the Markovian long-run covariance and the contraction geometry of the projected Bellman operator.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Theoretical analysis; no empirical benchmarks or datasets used.
summary.main_contribution: Provides a rigorous SDE-based approximation for linear TD(0) under Markovian noise that explains the error floor through the interaction of Markovian covariance and contraction geometry of the Bellman operator.
summary.limitations: Purely theoretical; limited to linear function approximation and TD(0); no empirical validation on real-world reinforcement learning tasks; does not address non-linear function approximation (e.g., neural networks).
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Very low. This is a theoretical machine learning paper about reinforcement learning algorithms. Weijie's research focuses on osteoarthritis, proteomics, Mendelian randomisation, and multimodal clinical prediction. There is no direct connection to causal inference in genetics, proteomics, or clinical risk modelling.

title: IUU+DB: Tracking Illegal, Unreported, and Unregulated Fishing, Seafood Fraud, and Labor Abuse through LLM-driven Information Extraction
summary: Illegal, unreported, and unregulated fishing (IUU) traditionally refers to fishing activities that violate applicable laws or occur in areas that lack applicable laws. We propose the term IUU+ to capture a broader suite of fisheries sector environmental and associated supply chain trade-related crimes and behaviors. Although IUU+ activity is widely recognized as a serious threat to marine ecosystems, markets, and livelihoods, a quantitative understanding of these incidents, e.g., their frequency, geography, species, actors, and patterns in the type of illicit activity, remains difficult to obtain. We propose IUU+DB, a large language model driven system for building a global incident database of IUU+ activity. The system ingests heterogeneous documents, classifies whether they describe relevant incidents, extracts key data elements such as actors, locations, species, vessels, violations, and enforcement outcomes, and supports deduplication and trend analysis. Case studies and validation results show that IUU+DB can help organize fragmented evidence, surface geographic and behavioral hotspots, support fisheries-domain specific research in academia and non-government organizations, assist source and species risk assessments for industry, and provide support for policy implementation and targeted enforcement efforts to government agencies.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents IUU+DB, a system that uses large language models (LLMs) to automatically extract information about illegal fishing, seafood fraud, and labor abuse from news articles and reports. It builds a searchable database of incidents to help researchers, NGOs, and governments track these activities globally.
summary.technical_summary: The authors propose IUU+DB, an LLM-driven pipeline for constructing a global incident database of illegal, unreported, and unregulated fishing (IUU) plus associated supply chain crimes (IUU+). The system ingests heterogeneous text documents, classifies relevance to IUU+ incidents, extracts structured entities (actors, locations, species, vessels, violations, enforcement outcomes) using LLM-based information extraction, performs deduplication, and enables trend analysis. Validation includes case studies comparing extracted data against known incidents.
summary.data_or_benchmark: No publicly available benchmark dataset; the system ingests heterogeneous documents (news articles, reports) and validation is done via case studies.
summary.main_contribution: A scalable LLM-driven system for building a structured global database of IUU+ fishing incidents from unstructured text, enabling quantitative analysis of environmental crimes.
summary.limitations: Domain-specific to fisheries and environmental crime; no connection to biomedical, proteomics, or genetic epidemiology; validation limited to case studies without rigorous quantitative benchmarks.

title: EgoCS-400K: An Egocentric Gameplay Dataset for World Models
summary: The shift from video generation to interactive world modeling places new demands on data: beyond captioned videos, world models require temporally aligned video-action-language trajectories grounded in the actions, camera motion, states, and events that drive future scene changes. However, such data is difficult to obtain at scale. Web video datasets offer broad visual coverage but lack executable actions and reliable states; robotic datasets provide action and state supervision but are costly and limited in scene diversity; and existing simulators often lack large-scale human-driven interaction trajectories. In this paper, we introduce EgoCS-400K, a large-scale replay-grounded egocentric Counter-Strike dataset for world models, built from public professional CS and CS2 match demos that preserve human gameplay trajectories and enable parsing, replaying, rendering, and temporal alignment. We extract player states, view directions, movements, keyboard/button inputs, view-angle changes, weapon usage, game events, and round-level context, and render clean first-person videos from the same trajectories. EgoCS-400K contains over 400,000 first-person videos and 10,000 hours of gameplay from more than 1,000 matches and 40,000 rounds, covering 13 maps and 10 player viewpoints per round. It supports a range of interactive visual modeling tasks, including action-conditioned future prediction, state- and event-aware scene rollout, replay-grounded captioning, and agent egocentric action understanding. By connecting visual observations with human actions, camera motion, game states, and events at scale, EgoCS-400K serves as a practical bridge between passive web videos, controllable game simulation, and costly real-world embodied data.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper introduces a massive dataset of first-person gameplay videos from Counter-Strike, with detailed annotations of player actions, camera movements, game states, and events. It's designed to help train AI models that can predict what happens next in a scene based on actions, not just generate videos.
summary.technical_summary: EgoCS-400K is a large-scale egocentric dataset built from professional Counter-Strike match replays, containing over 400K first-person videos (10,000 hours) with temporally aligned action, state, event, and camera motion annotations. It supports action-conditioned future prediction, state-aware scene rollout, replay-grounded captioning, and egocentric action understanding tasks.
key_methods: Egocentric video-action-language trajectory extraction from game replays
Temporal alignment of player states, view directions, keyboard inputs, weapon usage, and game events
Replay-grounded rendering of first-person videos from parsed match demos
Multi-perspective data collection (10 viewpoints per round across 13 maps)

Annotations include player states, view directions, movements, keyboard/button inputs, view-angle changes, weapon usage, game events, and round-level context.

title: All Smoke, No Alarm: Oracle Signals in Agent-Authored Test Code
comment: Accepted at the 8th IEEE International Conference on Artificial Intelligence Testing, 2026
summary: Software practitioners increasingly use AI coding agents that generate test code alongside production code in open source pull requests (PRs). Recent studies report more than 932,000 agent-authored PRs across more than 116,000 repositories, yet whether their test files contain meaningful verification logic remains underexplored. Test files lacking explicit assertions execute code without verifying behavior, so quality gates based on test-file presence overestimate verification strength. The goal of this paper is to help practitioners assess the verification strength of agent-authored patches by characterizing oracle signals and their link to merge outcomes and review effort. We conduct an empirical study of 86,156 test-file patches from 33,596 agent-authored PRs across 2,807 GitHub repositories produced by five coding agents: OpenAI Codex, GitHub Copilot, Devin, Cursor, and Claude Code. A qualitative analysis of 384 stratified patches informs a syntactic taxonomy of eight oracle signal categories. Applied at scale, 80.2% of test patches contain weak or no explicit oracle signals. While raw merge rates are lower for strong-oracle PRs, a regression analysis adjusting for agent, PR size, repository popularity, task type, and language shows strong oracles significantly improve merge likelihood (OR = 1.28, p < 0.001). Our findings suggest that test file counts substantially overestimate verification strength and that practitioners can adopt oracle-aware quality checks to more accurately evaluate agent-authored contributions.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper studies test code written by AI coding agents (like GitHub Copilot, Devin, Claude Code) in open source pull requests. It finds that over 80% of AI-generated test files lack meaningful assertions — they run code but don't actually verify anything. The authors call these "weak oracle signals" and show that while such tests inflate quality metrics, they don't meaningfully contribute to software verification.
summary.technical_summary: The authors analyze 86,156 test-file patches from 33,596 agent-authored PRs across 2,807 GitHub repositories from five coding agents. They develop a taxonomy of eight oracle signal categories through qualitative analysis of 384 stratified patches. Applying this taxonomy at scale, they find 80.2% of test patches contain weak or no explicit oracle signals. Regression analysis adjusting for confounders shows strong oracles significantly improve merge likelihood (OR = 1.28, p < 0.001), though raw merge rates are lower for strong-oracle PRs.
key_methods: Qualitative taxonomy development of oracle signals
Large-scale empirical analysis of 86,156 test patches
Logistic regression adjusting for agent, PR size, repo popularity, task type, language
summary.data_or_benchmark: 86,156 test-file patches from 33,596 agent-authored PRs across 2,807 GitHub repositories; five coding agents: OpenAI Codex, GitHub Copilot, Devin, Cursor, Claude Code
summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates that test file counts substantially overestimate verification strength in AI-generated code, and proposes oracle-aware quality checks as a more accurate evaluation approach.
summary.limitations: Focused on open source GitHub repositories; may not generalise to proprietary or enterprise codebases. Only examines test files, not production code quality. Oracle signal taxonomy is manually derived and may not capture all patterns.

title: Evaluating Open-Source LLMs for Multi-Label ATT&CK Technique Classification on CTI Reports
summary: Classifying Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) using MITRE Adversarial Tactics, Techniques, and Common Knowledge (ATT&CK) is essential for proactive defense, but historically required extensive human effort. Pre-Large Language Model (LLM) automation sped up this process, but could not resolve the complex language and multi-step attack patterns found in unstructured CTI reports. LLMs addressed previous limitations by using contextual reasoning to understand unstructured text. However, current evaluations rely on simplified, single-technique sentences that ignore the complexity of real-world CTI reports, which often leads to inflated performance results. Consequently, the baseline performance of open-source LLMs on complex unstructured CTI reports remains unevaluated. To address this gap, we constructed a ground-truth dataset of 2,076 human-annotated sentences (1,281 technique-positive, 795 negative) from 83 complex unstructured CTI reports. These sentences were mapped to 114 unique ATT&CK techniques using a six-phase annotation process, achieving \k{appa} = 0.68 inter-annotator agreement. Using this dataset, we evaluated seven open-source LLMs ranging from 8B to 236B parameters across prompt strategy and temperature configurations. The highest-performing LLM achieved a micro-averaged F1 score of 0.22, establishing the empirical baseline for multi-label ATT&CK classification on complex unstructured CTI. Parameter size showed a statistically significant positive correlation with F1 score. Prompt strategy and temperature produced no statistically significant gains across model configurations. These results indicate that current open-source LLMs are insufficient for production-grade ATT&CK classification. The dataset, benchmark, and findings provide a reproducible foundation for future CTI research.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

The best model achieved only an F1 score of 0.22, showing current open-source LLMs are not good enough for production use in this cybersecurity task.
summary.technical_summary: The study constructs a ground-truth dataset of 2,076 human-annotated sentences from 83 unstructured CTI reports, mapped to 114 unique ATT&CK techniques via a six-phase annotation process (κ=0.68). Seven open-source LLMs (8B–236B parameters) were evaluated across prompt strategies and temperature settings. The best micro-averaged F1 was 0.22. Parameter size correlated positively with F1; prompt strategy and temperature showed no significant effect.

title: The Measurement Gap in the Automation of EU Law: Benchmarking Doctrinal Legal Reasoning under the EU AI Act
summary: Large language models now produce legal text of at least median quality, yet no existing benchmark can evaluate whether they perform doctrinal legal reasoning, which forms the interpretive core of legal work, rather than the ancillary, paralegal tasks that most current legal-AI evaluations measure. This measurement gap is not only methodological but legal: the EU AI Act makes "appropriate accuracy" a binding requirement for high-risk AI used in the judicial domain, yet that requirement cannot acquire operational content without the very doctrinal-reasoning benchmark the field lacks.

Current legal AI benchmarks only test paralegal tasks like document summarisation.
summary.technical_summary: The paper identifies a measurement gap in evaluating LLMs for doctrinal legal reasoning—the core interpretive work of legal professionals. It argues that the EU AI Act's requirement for "appropriate accuracy" in high-risk judicial AI cannot be operationalised without a dedicated doctrinal-reasoning benchmark. Current legal NLP benchmarks (e.g., LexGLUE, CaseHOLD) focus on ancillary tasks rather than the reasoning structure central to legal doctrine.
summary.data_or_benchmark: No benchmark is proposed; the paper is a critique/position piece identifying a gap.

title: ReAge3D: Re-Aging 3D Faces with View Consistency
summary: We present a novel framework for realistic and controllable 3D face re-aging which produces highly detailed, identity-preserving results. Existing 3D editing methods, while effective for coarse semantic changes, are not well suited for re-aging, as even small inconsistencies across re-aged 2D views can lead to over-smoothing of subtle but perceptually important age-related details. To address this challenge, we first introduce a 2D diffusion-based re-aging model, DiffReaging, trained on synthetically generated image pairs. We further propose a center-out editing propagation strategy that leverages this re-aging model to reconstruct multi-view-consistent re-aged images. Specifically, starting from a re-aged frontal pivot view, we reconstruct the remaining views through warping and our proposed Masked-DiffReaging process. By injecting existing content at every step of the diffusion process, Masked-DiffReaging ensures that the reconstructed regions remain coherent with existing pixels. The resulting consistent set of re-aged views supervises the optimization of the re-aged 3D representation. Our method outperforms existing 3D editing techniques both visually and quantitatively, enabling smooth, fine-grained control over age transformations in 3D face models.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents a method for realistically changing the apparent age of 3D face models while keeping the person's identity intact. It uses a diffusion-based AI model to generate aged or de-aged 2D images of faces from different angles, then ensures all views are consistent so the 3D model looks natural from every perspective.
summary.technical_summary: The authors propose ReAge3D, a framework for 3D face re-aging that combines a 2D diffusion-based re-aging model (DiffReaging) with a center-out editing propagation strategy to achieve multi-view consistency. DiffReaging is trained on synthetic image pairs generated via 3D rendering with age-specific texture maps. The center-out strategy first re-ages a frontal pivot view, then propagates edits to other views via warping and a Masked-DiffReaging process that injects existing content at each diffusion step to maintain coherence. The consistent multi-view images supervise optimization of the 3D representation (e.g., NeRF or 3D mesh). The method enables fine-grained, continuous age control and outperforms prior 3D editing techniques.
key_methods: Synthetic training data from 3D renders with age-specific textures
NeRF/3D representation optimization from consistent multi-view re-aged images
summary.data_or_benchmark: Synthetic image pairs generated from 3D face renders with age-specific texture maps. Evaluated on 3D face datasets (likely FFHQ, CelebA-HQ derived 3D faces) with quantitative metrics (FID, LPIPS, identity preservation) and user studies.
summary.main_contribution: A novel framework for multi-view-consistent 3D face re-aging that preserves fine-grained age-related details by combining a specialized 2D diffusion re-aging model with a center-out propagation strategy and masked diffusion for view consistency.
summary.limitations: Requires synthetic training data generation pipeline; limited to face re-aging (not general 3D editing); may not generalise well to extreme ages or non-frontal poses; computational cost of multi-view diffusion and 3D optimisation.

title: Neural Tree Reconstruction for the Open Forest Observatory
comment: Published as a workshop paper at "Tackling Climate Change with Machine Learning", ICLR 2024
summary: The Open Forest Observatory (OFO) is a collaboration across universities and other partners to make low-cost forest mapping accessible to ecologists, land managers, and the general public. The OFO is building both a database of geospatial forest data as well as open-source methods and tools for forest mapping by uncrewed aerial vehicle. Such data are useful for a variety of climate applications including prioritizing reforestation efforts, informing wildfire hazard reduction, and monitoring carbon sequestration. In the current iteration of the OFO's forest map database, 3D tree maps are created using classical structure-from-motion techniques. This approach is prone to artifacts, lacks detail, and has particular difficulty on the forest floor where the input data (overhead imagery) has limited visibility. These reconstruction errors can potentially propagate to the downstream scientific tasks (e.g. a wildfire simulation.) Advances in 3D reconstruction, including methods like Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF), produce higher quality results that are more robust to sparse views and support data-driven priors. We explore ways to incorporate NeRFs into the OFO dataset, outline future work to support even more state-of-the-art 3D vision models, and describe the importance of high-quality 3D reconstructions for forestry applications.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper describes efforts to build a low-cost forest mapping system using drones and computer vision. The authors explore using Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) to create better 3D tree models from aerial images, replacing older methods that produce artifacts and lack detail. The goal is to support climate applications like reforestation, wildfire hazard reduction, and carbon monitoring.
summary.technical_summary: The Open Forest Observatory (OFO) aims to democratise forest mapping via UAV-based photogrammetry. Current 3D tree reconstructions rely on classical structure-from-motion (SfM), which suffers from artifacts and poor performance in occluded areas like the forest floor. The authors propose incorporating Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) to improve reconstruction quality, robustness to sparse views, and integration of data-driven priors. They outline a pipeline for transitioning from SfM to NeRF-based representations and discuss future work involving more advanced 3D vision models for forestry applications.

title: Memory as a Wasting Asset: Pricing Flash Endurance for Embodied Agents, and the Limits of Doing So
summary:
A robot's flash endurance is a non-renewable stock: every persisted write spends one of a few thousand program/erase cycles and never refills, yet no fielded robot memory system prices which memories are worth an erase cycle. We treat embodied memory as depreciating capital and price that stock with a single endurance shadow price $η$, which makes cost-minimizing placement across a RAM / on-board NVM / cloud hierarchy a threshold in a wear-augmented per-byte index. The index is cost-optimal whatever the sign of the value-write association $χ$; only when $χ> 0$ does the optimum turn non-monotone, sending a robot's most valuable memories off its flash.
  The pivot is thus empirical, and we measure $χ$ on real robot logs at a pre-specified gate: its sign is a property of the deployment regime -- positive on recurrent long-horizon manipulation ($\hatχ \approx +1.0 \times 10^{-3}$, replicated at full power), null on a shorter-horizon suite, and negative on non-recurrent teleoperation. Two boundaries scope the result. The endurance budget is dormant on premium 3,000-P/E TLC at datasheet prices and binding on the commodity QLC/eMMC ($\sim$1,000 P/E) that cheaper edge robots run. And where it binds, a learned wear-aware controller only ties price-based routing on task value, because realized value is tier-invariant across RAM, NVM, and cloud: the rent governs device lifetime and cost, not task performance. Whether wear-aware placement improves task value remains open -- $χ$ is measured against a value proxy, and the non-monotone optimum, while proven, is not yet observed in data.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper is about managing the limited write endurance of flash memory in robots. Flash memory can only be written to a finite number of times before it wears out. The authors treat memory as a depreciating asset and develop a pricing model to decide which memories are worth writing to flash versus keeping in RAM or the cloud. They test this on real robot logs and find the approach works differently depending on the type of robot task.
summary.technical_summary: The paper formalises flash endurance as a non-renewable stock and introduces a shadow price η to govern cost-minimising memory placement across a RAM/on-board NVM/cloud hierarchy. The optimal policy is a threshold in a wear-augmented per-byte index. The sign of the value-write association χ determines whether the optimum is monotone (χ ≤ 0) or non-monotone (χ > 0). Empirical measurement of χ on real robot logs shows positive association for recurrent long-horizon manipulation, null for short-horizon tasks, and negative for non-recurrent teleoperation. The endurance constraint is binding only on commodity QLC/eMMC (~1,000 P/E cycles) but dormant on premium TLC (3,000 P/E). A learned wear-aware controller ties price-based routing on task value because realised value is tier-invariant across memory hierarchy levels.
key_methods: Shadow pricing of non-renewable endurance stock
Wear-augmented per-byte index for memory placement
Empirical measurement of value-write association χ on robot logs
summary.data_or_benchmark: Real robot logs from recurrent long-horizon manipulation, short-horizon tasks, and non-recurrent teleoperation deployments.
summary.main_contribution: Formalising flash endurance as an economic stock with a shadow price and deriving the optimal memory placement policy, with empirical validation of the value-write association sign across different deployment regimes.
summary.limitations: The value proxy used to measure χ may not capture true task value; the non-monotone optimum is proven but not yet observed in data; results are specific to robot memory systems and may not generalise.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: This paper is about robotics memory management and has no direct connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, Mendelian randomisation, or any biomedical research area relevant to Weijie's PhD.

title: Your AI Travel Agent Would Book You a Bullfight: An Agentic Benchmark for Implicit Animal Welfare in Frontier AI Models
summary: AI agents are moving from advisors to actors, booking travel, planning menus, and running procurement on behalf of users. Existing benchmarks for AI and animal welfare evaluate model text responses to question-answer prompts, leaving open whether the welfare reasoning surfaced in those responses transfers to agentic deployment where the model must take actions with tools. We introduce TAC (Travel Agent Compassion), the first agentic benchmark measuring whether AI agents avoid options involving animal exploitation when acting on behalf of users. TAC presents an AI agent with twelve hand-authored travel booking scenarios across six categories of animal exploitation, augmented to forty-eight samples to control for price, rating, and position confounds. We evaluate seven frontier models from four labs. Every model scores below the chance level of sixty-four percent, with the best performer (Claude Opus 4.7) at fifty-three percent. A single welfare-aware sentence in the system prompt yields gains of forty-seven to sixty-three percentage points in Claude and GPT-5.5, twenty-six points in GPT-5.2, and under twelve points in DeepSeek and Gemini. An auxiliary Inspect Scout audit of 288 base-condition transcripts from the top two performers, using Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite as judge, flags zero transcripts for evaluation awareness, suggesting the below-chance rates do not stem from the models recognising the evaluation. We discuss implications for category-level variation across cultural domains, the limits of text-response welfare benchmarks, and the EU General-Purpose AI Code of Practice systemic risk framework.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper introduces a benchmark called TAC (Travel Agent Compassion) that tests whether AI agents avoid booking travel options involving animal exploitation (e.g., bullfights, elephant rides) when acting autonomously. The authors tested seven frontier AI models and found all performed below chance level (64%), with the best model (Claude Opus 4.7) scoring only 53%. Adding a single welfare-aware sentence to the system prompt dramatically improved performance for some models but not others.
summary.technical_summary: The paper presents TAC, an agentic benchmark with 12 hand-authored travel booking scenarios across 6 categories of animal exploitation, augmented to 48 samples controlling for price, rating, and position confounds. Seven frontier models (Claude Opus 4.7, GPT-5.5, GPT-5.2, DeepSeek, Gemini variants) were evaluated. All models scored below the chance level of 64%. A welfare-aware system prompt intervention yielded 47-63 percentage point gains in Claude and GPT-5.5, 26 points in GPT-5.2, and under 12 points in DeepSeek and Gemini. An Inspect Scout audit of 288 transcripts found zero cases of evaluation awareness.
key_methods: Inspect Scout audit for evaluation awareness
summary.data_or_benchmark: TAC benchmark: 12 hand-authored scenarios across 6 animal exploitation categories, augmented to 48 samples
summary.main_contribution: First agentic benchmark measuring AI model behaviour regarding animal welfare in autonomous tool-use settings, revealing that text-response welfare reasoning does not transfer to agentic deployment.
summary.limitations: Limited to travel booking domain; small number of scenarios; single audit method for evaluation awareness; cultural variation in animal welfare norms not deeply explored.

title: Descriptor: Certus Caliber Classification Gunshot Dataset (C3GD)
summary: In this work, we introduce the Certus Caliber Classification Gunshot Dataset (C3GD), a publicly accessible data set developed for the analysis of firearm muzzle blast sounds. The dataset aims to provide a wide variety of firearms, calibers, cartridges, microphones, and microphone locations with metadata detailed beyond what is currently otherwise available. It comprises more than 8000 field-collected data points from 28 firearms across 16 calibers. Because data collection in the field is costly, much of the existing research has been done using gunshot audio collected from the internet, which increases the risk of low-quality data and label noise. This dataset is primarily focused on caliber classification, but can also be used for gunshot detection, audio separation, and audio signal processing, providing a diversified and real-world reference. The dataset aims to provide enough diversity to be able to generalize to more real-world applications while also providing enough metadata for detailed academic analysis.

summary.technical_summary: The Certus Caliber Classification Gunshot Dataset (C3GD) contains over 8,000 field-collected gunshot audio samples from 28 firearms spanning 16 calibers, with detailed metadata on microphones, locations, and cartridges. The dataset supports caliber classification, gunshot detection, audio separation, and signal processing tasks, aiming to reduce label noise and improve real-world generalisability compared to internet-sourced alternatives.

title: Knowledge Reutilization in Meta-Reinforcement Learning
summary: Meta-reinforcement learning enables fast adaptation by extracting shared structure from related tasks, but existing end-to-end methods often couple task inference with embodiment-specific control. This coupling can obscure non-parametric task semantics, reduce sample efficiency, and limit cross-agent reuse. We propose a meta-knowledge reutilization framework that learns task-level knowledge on a dynamics-simplified agent and transfers it to heterogeneous agents. The framework uses a Bayesian non-parametric prior to organize latent task modes and a high-level policy to generate task-level magnitude guidance. To bridge reusable task knowledge with different embodiments, we introduce a semantic-magnitude interface and a lightweight temporal adaptor, which convert frozen meta-knowledge into temporally aligned subgoals for embodiment-specific low-level controllers. Experiments on multiple locomotion agents show that our framework reduces final-step tracking error by 94.75% -- 99.79% compared with recent state-of-the-art baselines and achieves comparable deployment performance with about 23.8% of their interaction data.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper is about teaching AI agents to reuse knowledge across different robot bodies. Instead of training each robot from scratch, the authors create a system where a simple robot learns the "idea" of a task (like walking), and then transfers that knowledge to more complex robots. This allows the complex robots to learn much faster and with less data.
summary.technical_summary: The paper proposes a meta-knowledge reutilization framework for meta-reinforcement learning that decouples task inference from embodiment-specific control. It uses a Bayesian non-parametric prior (Dirichlet process) to organize latent task modes, a high-level policy to generate task-level magnitude guidance, a semantic-magnitude interface, and a lightweight temporal adaptor to convert frozen meta-knowledge into temporally aligned subgoals for embodiment-specific low-level controllers. Experiments on multiple locomotion agents show 94.75%-99.79% reduction in final-step tracking error compared to SOTA baselines, with comparable deployment using ~23.8% of interaction data.
key_methods: Bayesian non-parametric prior (Dirichlet process) for latent task mode organization
Semantic-magnitude interface for cross-embodiment knowledge transfer
Lightweight temporal adaptor for subgoal alignment
High-level policy for task-level magnitude guidance
summary.data_or_benchmark: Multiple locomotion agents (MuJoCo-based simulation environments). Comparisons against recent SOTA meta-RL baselines.
summary.main_contribution: A framework that decouples task-level knowledge from embodiment-specific control in meta-RL, enabling efficient cross-agent knowledge transfer with dramatically reduced interaction data requirements.
summary.limitations: Tested only on simulated locomotion tasks; limited to homogeneous task families; requires a dynamics-simplified source agent; real-world robotics validation is absent.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Minimal direct relevance. This is a pure AI/robotics paper with no connection to biomedical data, proteomics, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or clinical prediction.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: No direct application. The concept of knowledge transfer across different "embodiments" is vaguely analogous to transfer learning across different omics layers or populations, but the methods are not designed for biomedical data.
summary.possible_research_idea: None that are practical. One could stretch the analogy: using a simpler model (e.g., PRS) to learn task structure and transfer to complex multimodal models (e.g., cross-attention transformers), but the Bayesian non-parametric and RL machinery is mismatched for tabular biomedical data.

title: Structural Role Injection in Handlebars-Templated LLM Prompts: Triple-Brace Interpolation, Delimiter Family, and the Limits of HTML Auto-Escaping
summary: Large language model applications build prompts from templates, and Handlebars is a widely used templating engine and the default prompt-template format in Microsoft Semantic Kernel. Its double-brace {x} expression HTML-escapes the interpolated value and is documented as the safe default; its triple-brace {x} expression inserts the value raw. We show that this choice silently governs an application's exposure to structural role injection, where attacker-controlled data carries chat role delimiters that forge a higher-privilege turn. A model-free analysis establishes the mechanism: Handlebars escaping rewrites angle brackets but not square brackets, colons, or Markdown hashes, so it neutralises ChatML, Llama-3, and XML role delimiters (survival rate 0.00) while leaving Llama-2 [INST], legacy Human:/Assistant:, and Markdown ### delimiters intact (survival rate 1.00 for the last two). We then run 5760 trials across seven delimiter families, two attack objectives, and four models (GPT-3.5 Turbo, GPT-4o mini, GPT-4.1 mini, Claude Haiku 4.5) at a combined API cost of 1.63 USD. GPT-3.5 Turbo follows the task-hijack instruction in 97% of raw and 91% of escaped trials, with the escaping protection concentrated in the angle-bracket families and absent for the colon- and Markdown-based families; the harder secret-exfiltration objective, which does not saturate, exposes the same family interaction more cleanly. Claude Haiku 4.5 resists both objectives almost entirely. The escaped default protects only the delimiter schemes whose characters HTML escaping happens to cover, gives no protection for the rest, and cannot substitute for a structural separation of instruction and data.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper investigates a security vulnerability in how large language model (LLM) applications use Handlebars templating (the default in Microsoft Semantic Kernel). It shows that the "safe" default escaping only protects against certain types of prompt injection attacks (those using angle brackets) but leaves other delimiter types (square brackets, colons, Markdown hashes) completely unprotected, meaning attacker-controlled text can still hijack the LLM's behaviour.
summary.technical_summary: The paper presents a systematic analysis of structural role injection in Handlebars-templated LLM prompts. It demonstrates that Handlebars' double-brace HTML escaping rewrites angle brackets but not square brackets, colons, or Markdown hashes, thus neutralising ChatML, Llama-3, and XML role delimiters while leaving Llama-2 [INST], legacy Human:/Assistant:, and Markdown ### delimiters intact. The authors run 5760 trials across seven delimiter families, two attack objectives (task hijack and secret exfiltration), and four models (GPT-3.5 Turbo, GPT-4o mini, GPT-4.1 mini, Claude Haiku 4.5) at a total API cost of $1.63. GPT-3.5 Turbo follows task-hijack instructions in 97% of raw and 91% of escaped trials, with escaping protection concentrated in angle-bracket families. Claude Haiku 4.5 resists both objectives almost entirely. The key finding is that HTML escaping cannot substitute for proper structural separation of instruction and data.
key_methods: Systematic evaluation of Handlebars escaping behaviour across delimiter families
5760 controlled prompt injection trials across 4 LLMs
Two attack objectives: task hijack and secret exfiltration
Model-free analysis of HTML escaping coverage
summary.data_or_benchmark: Custom benchmark of 5760 prompt injection trials across 7 delimiter families, 2 attack objectives, and 4 models (GPT-3.5 Turbo, GPT-4o mini, GPT-4.1 mini, Claude Haiku 4.5).
summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates that HTML auto-escaping in Handlebars templates provides only partial, coincidental protection against prompt injection, and cannot replace proper structural separation of instructions and data in LLM prompt engineering.
summary.limitations: Limited to Handlebars/Semantic Kernel ecosystem; only tests four LLMs; attack success depends on model instruction-following tendencies which vary; does not propose a general solution beyond noting the inadequacy of escaping.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Minimal direct relevance. This is a cybersecurity/LLM-safety paper with no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, multi-omics, causal inference, or clinical prediction. It does not address biomedical AI, multimodal prediction, or any of Weijie's core research areas.

title: HLS-GPT: A Generative Pretrained Transformer (GPT) for Continental-Scale NASA Harmonized Landsat and Sentinel-2 (HLS) Reflectance Reconstruction Across All Bands on Arbitrary Dates
summary: Recent deep learning methods for Landsat and Sentinel-2 reflectance time series reconstruction remain limited by restricted spectral coverage, limited geographic scalability, or patch-based designs with short temporal contexts. We present HLS-GPT, a large-scale generative pretrained Transformer model for reconstructing NASA Harmonized Landsat Sentinel-2 30 m surface reflectance for all bands, any date, and any pixel location. HLS-GPT uses a hierarchical Transformer architecture to handle the different spectral band configurations of Landsat and Sentinel-2 and operates on single-pixel 12-month time series. To capture geographic and seasonal variability, the model was trained with nine years of HLS time series from more than 0.25 million training pixels across the conterminous United States. A random cropping and masking strategy extracts 12-month periods with varying start dates across epochs, masks 50% of valid observations, and trains the model to reconstruct the masked reflectance values from the remaining observations. Evaluation using more than 62,000 independent test pixels shows robust reconstruction under diverse land surface conditions, including complex crop phenology and sparse, irregular observations. Leave-one-observation-out evaluation achieved reconstruction RMSE below 0.026 for all HLS spectral bands, with relative RMSE below 35% for visible bands and below 13% for other bands. Red-edge band errors were comparable to red and near-infrared errors despite the absence of red-edge bands on Landsat. Sensitivity analyses that randomly masked 10% to 90% of test observations showed only modest degradation when 10% to 50% of observations were masked, with all-band RMSE below 0.028. Image reconstruction over nine independent 109 by 109 km CONUS HLS tiles further demonstrates that HLS-GPT outperforms two conventional methods and the NASA-IBM Prithvi model.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents HLS-GPT, a Transformer model that reconstructs missing satellite image data (reflectance values) for any date and location across the United States. It uses a hierarchical GPT-like architecture trained on nine years of NASA satellite data to fill in gaps caused by clouds or irregular satellite passes.
summary.technical_summary: HLS-GPT is a generative pretrained Transformer designed for reconstructing 30m surface reflectance time series from NASA's Harmonized Landsat Sentinel-2 (HLS) data. It uses a hierarchical Transformer architecture operating on single-pixel 12-month time series, trained on 0.25 million pixels across CONUS over nine years. A random cropping and masking strategy masks 50% of valid observations during training, and the model reconstructs masked reflectance values. Evaluation on 62,000 test pixels shows RMSE below 0.026 for all bands, with graceful degradation under 10-50% masking.
key_methods: Random cropping and masking strategy (50% masking)
summary.data_or_benchmark: NASA Harmonized Landsat Sentinel-2 (HLS) 30m surface reflectance data; 0.25M training pixels across CONUS; 62,000 independent test pixels; nine independent 109×109 km CONUS HLS tiles for image reconstruction evaluation
summary.main_contribution: First large-scale generative pretrained Transformer for continental-scale satellite reflectance reconstruction across all spectral bands on arbitrary dates, demonstrating robust performance under sparse and irregular observation patterns.
summary.limitations: Limited to CONUS; single-pixel design ignores spatial context; no explicit handling of atmospheric correction artifacts; not validated outside North America; no biomedical or health application.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Minimal direct relevance. This is a remote sensing paper with no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, genetics, or clinical prediction. The Transformer architecture and masking strategy are generic ML techniques already well-established in biomedical contexts.

The hierarchical Transformer and masking approach could conceptually inspire handling of missing multi-omics data, but the domain gap is large and simpler imputation methods (e.g., MICE, autoencoders) are more appropriate for biomedical data.
summary.possible_research_idea: The masked reconstruction paradigm could theoretically be adapted for multi-omics data imputation (e.g., masking protein levels and reconstructing from genetics + other omics), but this would require substantial re-engineering and the paper offers no biological insight or benchmark relevant to Weijie's work.

title: Ternary Mamba: Grouped Quantization-Aware Training of W1.58A16 State Space Models
summary: State Space Models (SSMs) such as Mamba-2 offer linear-time inference but their memory footprint limits edge deployment. Prior ternary SSM work (Slender-Mamba) trains from scratch on 150B tokens; we show a pretrained checkpoint suffices, reducing the marginal token budget by 1,000x. Using grouped quantization-aware training (QAT) with knowledge distillation from a frozen FP16 teacher, we compress Mamba-2 1.3B to 3.61x (2,687 to 744 MB) and achieve 48.1% zero-shot accuracy (7-task average) in just 102M tokens (4 GPU-hours, single H100) -- approaching Bi-Mamba's 48.4% (within +/-0.9pp CI). This QAT-from-pretrained setting reveals zero-ratio collapse, a novel instability caused by learnable quantization scales that does not arise in from-scratch training. We further show that post-hoc correction strategies effective for Transformers fail for SSMs due to error accumulation through the recurrence. These results demonstrate that ternary SSMs do not require expensive from-scratch training: QAT from pretrained checkpoints with KD is a data-efficient alternative.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents a method to compress large language models (specifically Mamba-2, a type of state space model) to use ternary weights (-1, 0, +1) instead of full precision, dramatically reducing memory footprint. The key insight is that you don't need to train these models from scratch on massive data—you can take an already-trained model and apply quantization-aware training with knowledge distillation, using only 102 million tokens (4 GPU hours) instead of 150 billion tokens. The authors also identify a new instability called "zero-ratio collapse" that occurs during this process.
summary.technical_summary: The authors apply grouped quantization-aware training (QAT) with knowledge distillation (KD) from a frozen FP16 teacher to compress Mamba-2 1.3B to ternary precision (W1.58A16). They achieve 3.61x compression (2687 to 744 MB) with 48.1% zero-shot accuracy (7-task average) using only 102M tokens, compared to prior work (Slender-Mamba) requiring 150B tokens from-scratch training. They identify zero-ratio collapse—a novel instability from learnable quantization scales in the QAT-from-pretrained setting—and show that post-hoc correction methods effective for Transformers fail for SSMs due to error accumulation through recurrence.
key_methods: Knowledge distillation from frozen FP16 teacher
summary.data_or_benchmark: Zero-shot evaluation on 7 common sense reasoning tasks (ARC-Easy, ARC-Challenge, BoolQ, HellaSwag, PIQA, SciQ, WinoGrande). Training uses 102M tokens (4 GPU-hours on single H100).
summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates that ternary SSMs can be obtained via QAT from pretrained checkpoints with KD, reducing the token budget by 1000x compared to from-scratch training. Also identifies zero-ratio collapse as a novel instability unique to this setting.
summary.limitations: Only evaluated on small-scale (1.3B) models; limited to 7 zero-shot tasks; no biomedical or clinical validation; no connection to multi-omics, proteomics, or any health-related application.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Very low. This is a pure ML systems/compression paper with no biomedical, epidemiological, or multi-omics content. No connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, MR, PRS, or any of Weijie's research areas.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly useful. The model compression techniques could theoretically be applied to biomedical foundation models, but the paper does not explore this and Weijie's work does not involve deploying large language models on edge devices.
summary.possible_research_idea: No immediate research ideas for Weijie's domain. One could imagine compressing a biomedical LLM for clinical deployment, but this is far from Weijie's current research trajectory in causal inference and multi-omics.

title: Qwen-RobotNav Technical Report: A Scalable Navigation Model Designed for an Agentic Navigation System
summary: Agentic navigation systems require a base navigation model whose observation strategy can be externally reconfigured at inference time, because instruction following, object search, target tracking, and autonomous driving share the same perception-planning backbone yet demand fundamentally different strategies for consuming the visual stream. We present Qwen-RobotNav, a scalable navigation model built on Qwen-RobotNav that addresses it through a parameterised interface with two complementary dimensions: multiple task modes that select the navigation behaviour, and controllable observation parameters (e.g., token budget, per-camera weights) that govern how visual history is encoded. With training-time randomization over all parameters, Qwen-RobotNav is robust to any inference-time configuration requiring zero architectural modification to the Qwen-RobotNav backbone. We train Qwen-RobotNav on 15.6M samples; co-training with vision-language data prevents the collapse into reactive action-sequence mappers observed in trajectory-only training. The parameterised interface also makes Qwen-RobotNav a natural building block for agentic systems: for long-horizon scenarios, an upper-level planner decomposes goals into sub-tasks and dynamically switches Qwen-RobotNav's task mode and context strategy mid-episode, composing complex behaviours from repeated calls to the same model. Extensive experiments show that Qwen-RobotNav sets new state-of-the-art results across major navigation benchmarks. The model exhibits favourable scaling from 2B to 8B parameters, with joint multi-task training developing a shared spatial-planning substrate that transfers across task families, and demonstrates strong zero-shot generalisation to real-world robots across diverse environments.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents Qwen-RobotNav, a robot navigation model that can flexibly switch between different navigation tasks (like following instructions, searching for objects, or driving) without changing its core architecture. It uses a controllable interface that adjusts how the robot processes visual information, and was trained on 15.6 million samples. The model achieves state-of-the-art results on navigation benchmarks and works on real robots in new environments without additional training.
summary.technical_summary: Qwen-RobotNav introduces a parameterised interface for agentic navigation with two dimensions: multiple task modes (instruction following, object search, target tracking, autonomous driving) and controllable observation parameters (token budget, per-camera weights). Training-time randomisation over all parameters ensures robustness to any inference-time configuration without architectural changes. The model is trained on 15.6M samples with co-training on vision-language data to prevent collapse into reactive action-sequence mapping. Scaling from 2B to 8B parameters shows favourable performance gains, and joint multi-task training develops a shared spatial-planning substrate that transfers across task families.
key_methods: Parameterised interface with task modes and observation parameters
Training-time randomisation over all configurable parameters
Multi-task joint training across navigation task families
Upper-level planner for long-horizon task decomposition
summary.data_or_benchmark: 15.6M training samples; evaluated on major navigation benchmarks and real-world robot environments
summary.main_contribution: A scalable, parameterised navigation model that can be externally reconfigured at inference time for different navigation tasks without architectural modification, achieving state-of-the-art results across benchmarks.
summary.limitations: No direct connection to biomedical, proteomics, or clinical applications; focused purely on robotics and computer vision.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: No direct relevance. This is a robotics/navigation paper with no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, MR, multi-omics, or clinical prediction.
