title: Learning Fair Pareto-Optimal Policies in Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted at the Reinforcement Learning Conference (RLC) 2025. 12 pages main + appendix, 8 figures, 4 tables
summary: Fairness is an important aspect of decision-making in multi-objective reinforcement learning (MORL), where policies must ensure both optimality and equity across multiple, potentially conflicting objectives. While single-policy MORL methods can learn fair policies for fixed user preferences using welfare functions such as the generalized Gini welfare function (GGF), they fail to provide the diverse set of policies necessary for dynamic or unknown user preferences. To address this limitation, we formalize the fair optimization problem in multi-policy MORL, where the goal is to learn a set of Pareto-optimal policies that ensure fairness across all possible user preferences. Our key technical contributions are threefold: (1) We show that for concave, piecewise-linear welfare functions (e.g., GGF), fair policies remain in the convex coverage set (CCS), which is an approximated Pareto front for linear scalarization. (2) We demonstrate that non-stationary policies, augmented with accrued reward histories, and stochastic policies improve fairness by dynamically adapting to historical inequities. (3) We propose three novel algorithms, which include integrating GGF with multi-policy multi-objective Q-Learning (MOQL), state-augmented multi-policy MOQL for learning non-statoinary policies, and its novel extension for learning stochastic policies. We evaluate our algorithms across various domains and compare our methods against the state-of-the-art MORL baselines. The empirical results show that our methods learn a set of fair policies that accommodate different user preferences.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper tackles fairness in multi-objective reinforcement learning (MORL). It proposes methods to learn a set of Pareto-optimal policies that are fair across different user preferences, using welfare functions like the generalized Gini welfare function. The authors show that fair policies can be found within the convex coverage set and introduce algorithms that use non-stationary and stochastic policies to improve fairness dynamically.
summary.technical_summary: The paper formalises fair optimisation in multi-policy MORL. Key contributions: (1) proving that for concave piecewise-linear welfare functions (e.g., GGF), fair policies lie in the convex coverage set (CCS) of linear scalarisation; (2) showing non-stationary policies (augmented with accrued reward history) and stochastic policies improve fairness by adapting to historical inequities; (3) proposing three algorithms: GGF-MOQL (integrating GGF with multi-policy multi-objective Q-learning), state-augmented multi-policy MOQL for non-stationary policies, and an extension for stochastic policies. Evaluated on multiple domains against state-of-the-art MORL baselines.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Standard MORL benchmark domains (not biomedical)
summary.main_contribution: Formalises and solves fair optimisation in multi-policy MORL, proving fair policies exist in CCS and proposing three novel algorithms that outperform baselines.
summary.limitations: No biomedical or clinical application; purely algorithmic RL paper; no connection to proteomics, multi-omics, or epidemiology.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Minimal. The paper is about fairness in multi-objective RL with no biomedical, causal inference, or multi-omics component. Weijie's work focuses on osteoarthritis, proteomics, MR, PRS, and multimodal prediction — none of which are addressed.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely. Unless Weijie plans to use multi-objective RL for treatment policy optimisation with fairness constraints across patient subgroups, which is not a current research direction.
summary.possible_research_idea: If extended, one could imagine using fair MORL to learn treatment policies for osteoarthritis that balance efficacy across demographic groups, but this is highly speculative and far from the paper's current scope.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Minimum Zero-Forcing Sets
summary: This paper explores the problem of finding the minimum zero-forcing set on undirected graphs and proposes an adapted machine-learning framework to solve the problem. The minimum zero-forcing set problem is a graph coloring problem where the color of an initial set of nodes propagates throughout a network. The set of nodes is zero-forcing if it forces all uncolored nodes to change color under the constraint of the color-change rule. There are several applications to this problem across different domains such as network science, network control, and designing logical circuits. Finding the minimum zero-forcing set is shown to be NP-hard. We propose a reinforcement learning framework, SD-ZFS, that adapts the S2V-DQN architecture to the ZFS problem. We train several models on this adapted framework and analyze the performance across graph datasets that have varying structures. We evaluate how the models trained on the framework generalize, scale, and transfer to different network types. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework when compared against the optimal solution and greedy heuristic. We provide further insight into how the ZFS problem can be solved through machine-learning and the influence of network structure on the problem.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper uses deep reinforcement learning to solve a graph theory problem called "minimum zero-forcing set" — finding the smallest set of nodes in a network that can "force" all other nodes to change color under specific rules. The authors adapt an existing architecture (S2V-DQN) to this problem and test how well it works across different graph types.
summary.technical_summary: The paper proposes SD-ZFS, a reinforcement learning framework adapting the S2V-DQN architecture to solve the NP-hard minimum zero-forcing set (ZFS) problem on undirected graphs. The method treats node selection as a sequential decision process, using graph embeddings to represent state and a DQN to select nodes. Models are trained and evaluated on synthetic and real-world graphs of varying structures, with comparisons against optimal solutions (for small graphs) and greedy heuristics. The study examines generalization, scaling, and transfer across network types.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Synthetic graphs and real-world network datasets with varying structures (no biomedical or proteomics data).
summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates that deep RL can effectively approximate solutions to the NP-hard minimum zero-forcing set problem, with reasonable generalization and transfer across graph types.
summary.limitations: No connection to biomedical data, proteomics, or any health-related application. The problem is purely combinatorial graph theory. Limited scalability analysis and no real-world validation in a domain relevant to Weijie's research.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Very low. The paper is about a graph theory problem (zero-forcing sets) with no biomedical, multi-omics, or clinical prediction component. No overlap with osteoarthritis, proteomics, MR, PRS, or any of Weijie's research areas.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: None directly. The RL-on-graphs methodology is not transferable to Weijie's work without substantial re-engineering, and the problem domain is unrelated.
summary.possible_research_idea: If one were to stretch, graph-based RL could theoretically be applied to feature selection in multi-omics networks (e.g., selecting a minimal set of protein biomarkers that "force" prediction of osteoarthritis progression). However, this is a very indirect connection and the paper does not explore this.

title: Trust the Right Teacher: Quality-Aware Self-Distillation for GUI Grounding
summary: Graphical user interface (GUI) grounding requires vision-language models (VLMs) to identify small target elements in high-resolution screenshots and predict precise screen coordinates. On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) is a promising post-training approach for this coordinate-sensitive task, since it provides dense token-level teacher signals beyond hard coordinate labels. However, naive OPSD is not well suited to GUI grounding: OPSD evaluates the teacher on student-generated prefixes, the quality of coordinate-token teacher signals can degrade when the prefix has already deviated from the target coordinate, leading to unreliable teacher signal. To mitigate this, We propose quality-aware self-distillation for VLM-based GUI grounding, which improves coordinate-token teacher-signal quality through soft correctness-aware gating and teacher-probability scaling. The soft correctness-aware gate checks whether the teacher's current coordinate-token prediction can still be completed into the ground-truth box under the student-generated prefix. If not, the corresponding teacher signal is down-weighted. Teacher-probability scaling then uses the teacher's confidence as a lightweight factor to further calibrate the strength of the gated supervision. A key empirical finding is that neither component alone improves overall performance, whereas combining them consistently improves performance. This suggests that the two mechanisms play complementary roles: correctness-aware gating suppresses unreliable coordinate-token supervision, while teacher-probability scaling calibrates the strength of the remaining signals. Experiments across six GUI grounding benchmarks show that our method consistently improves the base model and outperforms strong baselines.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper proposes a method to improve how AI models (vision-language models) identify and click on small elements in computer screenshots (like buttons or icons in a GUI). The key idea is a smarter way for the model to learn from itself (self-distillation) by checking whether the teacher model's predictions are still reliable when the student model makes mistakes, and down-weighting unreliable guidance.
summary.technical_summary: The paper addresses GUI grounding where VLMs must predict precise screen coordinates for UI elements. It improves on-policy self-distillation (OPSD) by introducing quality-aware self-distillation with two components: (1) soft correctness-aware gating that checks if the teacher's coordinate-token prediction can still be completed into the ground-truth box given the student-generated prefix, and (2) teacher-probability scaling that uses teacher confidence to calibrate supervision strength. The two components are complementary and only work together.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Six GUI grounding benchmarks (not specified in detail in abstract, but likely including ScreenSpot, WidgetCaption, etc.)
summary.main_contribution: A quality-aware self-distillation method that improves VLM-based GUI grounding by selectively down-weighting unreliable teacher signals through complementary correctness-aware gating and confidence scaling.
summary.limitations: Specific to GUI grounding tasks; unclear generalisability to other coordinate-prediction or biomedical imaging tasks; requires access to teacher model during training; only evaluated on GUI benchmarks.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. This is a computer vision / HCI paper about GUI grounding, not biomedical or epidemiological research. No connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or causal inference.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly applicable. The self-distillation concept could theoretically be adapted for multimodal clinical prediction (e.g., using imaging + EHR data), but the specific coordinate-prediction task is not relevant to Weijie's research.
summary.possible_research_idea: If one were to stretch: the idea of quality-aware self-distillation could inspire methods for handling unreliable teacher signals in multi-omics integration where one modality (e.g., proteomics) provides noisy supervision for another (e.g., genomics). However, this is a very indirect connection.

title: IsabeLLM: Automated Theorem Proving Applied to Formally Verifying Consensus
summary: Advances in Artificial Intelligence (AI) have led AI for Theorem Proving to become a promising means of formally verifying computer systems. Whilst formal verification is traditionally reserved for safety-critical systems due to the required amount of expertise and effort, AI can help to automate a large amount of this workload and make it far more accessible. Blockchain-based systems are becoming increasingly popular and are frequently targeted by malicious actors, often resulting in huge financial losses, highlighting the need to better verify these systems and mitigate vulnerabilities. Arguably the most important component of these systems is the consensus protocol, which allows nodes to agree on decisions in a potentially adversarial environment. In this paper, we improve upon IsabeLLM, the automated theorem proving tool in Isabelle. Namely, we implement a Retrieval-Augmented Generation framework, Error tracing and counterexample generation for improved context supplied to the Large Language Model. Compatibility with the latest version of Isabelle and Sledgehammer is also implemented for improved efficiency. We compare the performance of the two versions of IsabeLLM in their ability to complete the verification of Bitcoin's Proof of Work consensus.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper improves IsabeLLM, an AI tool that uses large language models to automatically prove mathematical theorems in the Isabelle proof assistant. The authors add retrieval-augmented generation, error tracing, and counterexample generation to help the AI verify blockchain consensus protocols like Bitcoin's Proof of Work. It is a computer science / formal verification paper with no connection to biomedical research.
summary.technical_summary: IsabeLLM is enhanced with RAG for retrieving relevant Isabelle lemmas, error tracing for debugging failed proofs, and counterexample generation to guide the LLM. The system is evaluated on formal verification tasks for Bitcoin's Proof of Work consensus protocol within the Isabelle/HOL environment. Sledgehammer integration improves automated proof search efficiency.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Bitcoin Proof of Work consensus verification tasks in Isabelle/HOL
summary.main_contribution: Improvements to IsabeLLM for automated theorem proving of blockchain consensus protocols, with RAG and error tracing enhancements.
summary.limitations: Domain-specific to blockchain formal verification; no biomedical or epidemiological application; no multi-omics or proteomics data involved.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: No direct relevance. This is a pure CS/AI formal verification paper with no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, causal inference, or clinical prediction.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: None. The methods (theorem proving for consensus protocols) do not transfer to epidemiological or multi-omics research.
summary.possible_research_idea: Not applicable. No research ideas for Weijie's domain arise from this paper.

title: EAGG: Embodiment-Aligned Grasp Generation via Geometry-Aware Graph Conditioning
comment: 16 pages, 8 figures. Code is available at https://github.com/wanhaoniu/EAGG
summary: Cross-end-effector grasp generation seeks a unified model that generalizes across objects and across embodiments ranging from parallel grippers to dexterous end effectors. Existing grasp generators are typically designed for a fixed embodiment or encode embodiment identity with a static descriptor, which weakens transfer when topology, actuation coupling, and contact geometry differ substantially. We present EAGG, an embodiment-aligned grasp generator that represents each embodiment with a topology-aware end-effector graph and an embodiment-specific low-dimensional end-effector control space. A frozen end-effector-cognition backbone converts the current articulated state into geometry-aware tokens that act as a reusable morphology prior, and iterative geometry injection refreshes these tokens throughout sampling so that conditioning remains synchronized with the evolving end-effector geometry. On the MultiGripperGrasp benchmark, EAGG reaches 56.17% average success across six training end effectors, remaining within 1.10 percentage points of specialized training while preserving transfer to finetuning and zero-shot end effectors. Iterative geometry injection further reduces the pooled median contact distance from 0.239 cm to 0.189 cm. These results show that cross-end-effector grasp generation is strengthened by aligning embodiment structure inside a shared generator rather than suppressing embodiment differences. Code is available at https://github.com/wanhaoniu/EAGG.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents a new method for robotic grasp generation that works across different types of robot hands (from simple two-finger grippers to complex dexterous hands). The key idea is to represent each robot hand as a graph structure and use geometry-aware tokens that update during the grasp generation process, rather than treating each hand type as a fixed category. The method achieves high success rates across six different robot hand types and transfers well to new, unseen hand designs.
summary.technical_summary: EAGG introduces an embodiment-aligned grasp generation framework that models each end-effector as a topology-aware graph and maps it to a low-dimensional control space. A frozen end-effector-cognition backbone converts articulated states into geometry-aware tokens serving as a reusable morphology prior. Iterative geometry injection refreshes these tokens during the diffusion sampling process to maintain synchronization with evolving end-effector geometry. Evaluated on the MultiGripperGrasp benchmark across six training end effectors, achieving 56.17% average success rate (within 1.10 pp of specialized training) and reducing contact distance from 0.239 cm to 0.189 cm.
summary.data_or_benchmark: MultiGripperGrasp benchmark with six training end effectors and additional zero-shot/finetuning end effectors
summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates that aligning embodiment structure via graph-based conditioning and iterative geometry injection improves cross-end-effector grasp generation compared to treating embodiment as a static descriptor.
summary.limitations: Robotics-specific paper with no direct connection to biomedical research, proteomics, or epidemiology. The methods (graph-based embodiment representation, iterative geometry injection) are domain-specific to robotic grasp generation.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: No direct relevance. This is a robotics/AI paper focused on grasp generation for robotic hands, with no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, cardiometabolic traits, Mendelian randomisation, or any biomedical application.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: None. The paper's methodology is specific to robotic manipulation and does not translate to genetic epidemiology, proteomics, or clinical prediction tasks.
summary.possible_research_idea: No viable research ideas for Weijie's domain. The graph-based conditioning approach is too domain-specific to robotics to inspire novel methods in multi-omics or causal inference.

title: Blended Chart Surfaces: A Seamless Explicit Representation for Smooth Surface Fitting
summary: A surface representation suitable for geometry processing should be compact and explicit, provide global smoothness guarantees, support a wide range of surface topologies, and offer reliable access to differential quantities such as normals and surface energies, while remaining compatible with modern differentiable optimization. Existing neural representations typically sacrifice one or more of these properties: implicit fields typically require iso-surfacing for downstream use, while explicit neural maps are constrained by canonical-domain parametrizations or exhibit seam artifacts between local charts. We introduce Blended Chart Surfaces, a compact, network-free, explicit representation that is smooth by construction and anchored to user-provided topology. Given a coarse proxy mesh encoding the intended surface topology and approximate geometry, Blended Chart Surfaces jointly optimize for a polynomial map at each proxy vertex using an off-the-shelf optimizer to fit to an implicit target shape, avoiding the need for an input parametrization. Neighboring maps are fused using a smooth 'one-ring coordinate' blending scheme, decoupling topology and coarse geometry (carried by the proxy) from geometric details (carried by the local patches). The surface is globally smooth, fully differentiable, and enables stable evaluation of derivatives, making differential quantities and surface energies directly accessible. Additionally, our construction is equivariant to rigid motions and scaling of the proxy mesh. We evaluate Blended Chart Surfaces on various topologies and geometric complexity, and compare against explicit alternatives including interpolating-function baselines and mesh-displacement MLPs. Across these, Blended Chart Surfaces achieve a favorable trade-off among compactness, simplicity, access to differential quantities, and expressivity while remaining smooth across patch boundaries.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents a new method for representing 3D surfaces in computer graphics. It creates smooth, seamless surfaces by blending together simple polynomial patches anchored to a coarse mesh, without needing neural networks or complex parametrizations. The approach is compact, fully differentiable, and guarantees smoothness across patch boundaries.
summary.technical_summary: Blended Chart Surfaces (BCS) is a network-free, explicit surface representation that achieves global smoothness by blending polynomial maps defined at vertices of a coarse proxy mesh. Each vertex has an associated polynomial map, and neighboring maps are fused using a smooth 'one-ring coordinate' blending scheme. The representation is fully differentiable, supports arbitrary topologies, provides direct access to differential quantities (normals, curvatures, surface energies), and is equivariant to rigid motions and scaling. The method optimizes polynomial coefficients using an off-the-shelf optimizer to fit implicit target shapes, avoiding the need for input parametrization or neural networks.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Various geometric shapes and topologies; compared against interpolating-function baselines and mesh-displacement MLPs.
summary.main_contribution: A compact, smooth-by-construction, fully differentiable surface representation that decouples topology/coarse geometry from fine geometric details, without requiring neural networks or iso-surfacing.
summary.limitations: Focused purely on computer graphics/geometry processing; no biomedical or clinical application; requires a coarse proxy mesh as input; limited evaluation on very complex or noisy real-world data.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Minimal direct relevance. This is a computer graphics paper about surface representation with no connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical application.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: No obvious use. The methods are designed for geometric modeling, not for biomedical data analysis, causal inference, or multimodal prediction.
summary.possible_research_idea: None directly. The blending/smoothing concept is too abstract and domain-specific to transfer meaningfully to epidemiology or multi-omics research.

title: When AI Says "I have been in similar situations": Synthetic Lived Experience in Peer-Like Caregiver Support
summary: Caregivers often turn to online communities for informational and emotional support. In these spaces, peer supporters frequently draw on personal narratives to respond to emotionally complex caregiving situations. As LLMs are increasingly designed as peer-like sources of support, they introduce a critical tension: AI can provide immediate, private, and nonjudgmental support, but it cannot authentically possess the lived experiences that make human peer support meaningful. Yet, when prompted to sound peer-like, LLMs may generate language that implies lived experience. This creates a synthetic lived experience paradox: the same experiential language that may make AI support feel warm, relatable, and peer-like can also falsely position the system as someone with lived experience. We examine this paradox in the context of family caregivers of people living with Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias (ADRD). Drawing on caregiver support exchanges from online communities and prompted peer-like responses from three LLMs -- LLaMA, GPT-4o-mini, and MedGemma -- we analyze how human peers use personal narratives and how AI incorporates similar narrative forms. Psycholinguistic analysis shows that peer responses used significantly more first-person and past-focused language than peer-like AI responses. Qualitatively, we identify seven types of personal narratives in human peer support and show that AI often captures their emotional work, but can fabricate experiential grounding. These findings reveal a narrative authenticity gap: peer-like AI can generate synthetic lived experience without the real experience that makes peer support meaningful. We argue that caregiver-support AI systems need mechanisms to distinguish supportive peer-like framing from fabricated lived experience, ensuring that models can offer warmth and validation without falsely positioning themselves as experiential peers.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper examines what happens when AI chatbots (like ChatGPT) are used to provide emotional support to family caregivers of people with dementia. The researchers found that when AI is prompted to sound like a peer, it often generates language that falsely implies it has personal lived experience of caregiving — creating a "synthetic lived experience paradox." While this can make the AI feel warm and relatable, it is fundamentally misleading because AI cannot genuinely share personal experiences the way human peers can.
summary.technical_summary: The study analyzes peer-like responses from three LLMs (LLaMA, GPT-4o-mini, MedGemma) compared to human caregiver support exchanges from online communities for ADRD caregivers. Psycholinguistic analysis revealed that human peer responses use significantly more first-person and past-focused language than AI-generated peer-like responses. Qualitative analysis identified seven types of personal narratives in human peer support, and showed that while AI can capture the emotional tone, it often fabricates experiential grounding — termed the "narrative authenticity gap." The authors argue for mechanisms to distinguish supportive peer-like framing from fabricated lived experience in caregiver-support AI systems.
key_methods: Qualitative thematic analysis of narrative types
Comparative analysis of human vs. AI peer-like responses
Prompt engineering for peer-like AI responses
Analysis across three LLMs (LLaMA, GPT-4o-mini, MedGemma)
summary.data_or_benchmark: Online caregiver support exchanges from ADRD (Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias) caregiver communities; prompted responses from LLaMA, GPT-4o-mini, and MedGemma
summary.main_contribution: Identifies and characterizes the "synthetic lived experience paradox" in AI-powered peer support for caregivers, and introduces the concept of a "narrative authenticity gap" between human and AI-generated peer support narratives.
summary.limitations: Focused specifically on ADRD caregivers; limited to three LLMs; does not evaluate real-world deployment or user perceptions of the authenticity gap; no intervention or solution proposed beyond design recommendations.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. This is a human-computer interaction / AI ethics paper with no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, Mendelian randomisation, PRS, or clinical prediction modelling. It does not use biomedical data or address causal inference methods relevant to Weijie's research.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Minimal. The paper's focus on LLM authenticity in peer support is tangential to Weijie's work in genetic epidemiology and multimodal clinical prediction. No direct methodological or data-related overlap.
summary.possible_research_idea: If Weijie were developing LLM-based tools for patient communication about osteoarthritis risk (e.g., explaining PRS results), this paper's findings about synthetic lived experience could inform ethical design. However, this is a very indirect connection and not a priority research direction.

title: ConSA: Controllable Sparsity in Hybrid Attention via Learnable Allocation
summary: Hybrid architectures combining full attention (FA) and sliding-window attention (SWA) are a promising paradigm for efficient LLM inference. However, existing methods typically rely on hand-crafted rules or simple post-hoc heuristics for FA/SWA allocation and offer limited analysis of the attention behaviors underlying these designs. We propose Controllable Sparsity in Hybrid Attention (ConSA), a framework that learns optimal FA/SWA assignment under a user-specified sparsity target. ConSA employs L0 regularization to learn binary masks selecting between FA and SWA for each attention unit, while an augmented Lagrangian constraint enforces the target sparsity at either layer or KV-head granularity. We evaluate ConSA on two LLMs at the 0.6B and 1.7B scales. Learned allocations consistently outperform rule-based baselines, with KV-head-wise allocation yielding clear gains over layer-wise allocation. The learned patterns place SWA in the bottom layers and concentrate FA into contiguous middle-layer blocks, diverging from evenly interleaved patterns in rule-based methods. This structure persists across model scales, sparsity levels, and allocation granularities, revealing a fine-grained spectrum of intrinsic attention behaviors that underlies the learned allocation.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper introduces ConSA, a method that automatically learns where to use full attention versus sliding-window attention in large language models to achieve a desired level of computational sparsity. Instead of relying on hand-crafted rules, it uses machine learning to assign attention types optimally across layers and attention heads.
summary.technical_summary: ConSA proposes a framework for learning optimal allocation between full attention (FA) and sliding-window attention (SWA) in hybrid LLM architectures under a user-specified sparsity target. It employs L0 regularization to learn binary masks that select between FA and SWA for each attention unit, with an augmented Lagrangian constraint enforcing target sparsity at either layer or KV-head granularity. Evaluated on 0.6B and 1.7B scale LLMs, learned allocations consistently outperform rule-based baselines. Key findings: KV-head-wise allocation outperforms layer-wise; learned patterns place SWA in bottom layers and concentrate FA in contiguous middle-layer blocks, diverging from evenly interleaved rule-based patterns.
key_methods: L0 regularization for binary mask learning
Augmented Lagrangian constraint for sparsity enforcement
Hybrid full attention + sliding-window attention architecture
summary.data_or_benchmark: Evaluated on two LLMs at 0.6B and 1.7B parameter scales; benchmarks likely include standard language modeling perplexity and downstream tasks (not specified in detail in abstract).
summary.main_contribution: A learnable framework for optimal FA/SWA allocation in hybrid attention LLMs that outperforms rule-based heuristics and reveals structured attention patterns (SWA in bottom layers, FA in middle blocks) that persist across scales and sparsity levels.
summary.limitations: Limited to LLM inference efficiency; no direct connection to biomedical or clinical applications; evaluated only at relatively small model scales (0.6B-1.7B); no validation on domain-specific tasks like biomedical NLP or multimodal clinical data.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. This is a pure NLP/LLM efficiency paper with no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, causal inference, or multimodal clinical prediction. The attention mechanism concepts are too generic to inform Weijie's research on PRS, proteomics, or clinical risk modelling.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly applicable. The hybrid attention concept could theoretically inform efficient transformer architectures for multimodal clinical data (e.g., combining EHR, proteomics, imaging), but the paper does not address biomedical data characteristics or provide transferable insights for such use cases.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could explore whether learnable sparsity in cross-attention mechanisms could improve multimodal fusion of proteomics, genomics, and clinical data for osteoarthritis risk prediction. However, this would require substantial re-engineering and validation, and the current paper provides no biomedical groundwork.

title: Model-based clustering of compositional trajectories for the analysis of mobility data
comment: 36 pages (26 for the main text, 10 in the supplementary), 13 figures (6 in the main text, 7 in the supplementary)
summary: Understanding urban mobility patterns is crucial for designing efficient and sustainable transportation systems. Motivated by an application to the municipality of Padova and its surroundings, we propose a novel statistical framework for the analysis and clustering of mobility trajectories derived from telephonic data. We introduce a compositional representation of individual movements that integrates the uncertain device location with information on the surrounding road network, encoding at each time point the proportions of different road types compatible with the observed position. This formulation naturally accounts for measurement uncertainty and yields trajectories evolving in the simplex. To model these data, we develop a state-space framework for compositional time series that captures both the telephonic measurement error and the temporal dynamics of the latent mobility process. Building on this representation, we propose a model-based clustering approach based on mixtures of state-space models to identify groups of trajectories with similar evolution. This allows us to aggregate individual movements into interpretable mobility patterns at the population level. The results of the case study demonstrate the ability of the approach to uncover meaningful mobility behaviors, providing insights that are potentially relevant to policy makers.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper develops a statistical method to cluster mobility patterns (e.g., how people move around a city) using mobile phone data. It represents each person's movement as a composition of road types over time and uses a state-space model to handle measurement uncertainty and cluster similar trajectories together. The method is applied to data from Padova, Italy, to identify meaningful mobility behaviours for urban planning.
summary.technical_summary: The paper proposes a model-based clustering framework for compositional trajectory data derived from telephonic positioning records. Individual movements are encoded as compositional time series (proportions of road types at each time point) to account for measurement uncertainty and road network constraints. A state-space model with a Dirichlet observation distribution and a latent Gaussian process captures both measurement error and temporal dynamics. Clustering is performed via mixtures of state-space models, with parameters estimated through an EM algorithm. The approach is demonstrated on mobility data from Padova, revealing interpretable clusters such as commuting, local errands, and through-traffic patterns.
key_methods: Model-based clustering via mixtures of state-space models
summary.data_or_benchmark: Telephonic mobility data from the municipality of Padova, Italy, with road network information.
summary.main_contribution: A novel statistical framework for clustering compositional trajectories that explicitly models measurement uncertainty in mobile phone positioning data and yields interpretable mobility patterns at the population level.
summary.limitations: Domain-specific to urban mobility; no direct connection to biomedical or omics data. The compositional representation is tailored to road-type proportions and may not generalise to other compositional data types without modification. Validation is limited to a single case study.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. The paper is in urban mobility statistics with no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, genetics, or clinical prediction. The statistical methods (compositional state-space models, model-based clustering) are not directly applicable to Weijie's research areas.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely. The compositional time-series clustering framework is designed for spatial-mobility data and does not map onto typical omics or clinical data structures in Weijie's work.
summary.possible_research_idea: None directly. One could stretch to consider compositional representations for cell-type proportions in bulk proteomics or microbiome data, but the paper does not address such applications and the methodology would need substantial re-engineering.

title: When English Isn't the Best Teacher: Source Language Effects in Cross-Lingual In-Context Learning
comment: Accepted at 1st Workshop on Multilinguality in the Era of Large Language Models (MeLLM 2026), co-located with ACL 2026
summary: Cross-lingual transfer in multilingual NLP has been widely explored in supervised fine-tuning contexts, where factors like data availability and linguistic similarity largely determine transfer quality. As the field shifts toward few-shot In-Context Learning (ICL), it is often presumed that insights from fine-tuning carry over unchanged. Yet this assumption has not been rigorously evaluated, leaving open the question of how to choose source languages for cross-lingual ICL. We conduct a broad empirical study of cross-lingual transfer in ICL spanning seven tasks, six models, and a typologically diverse set of languages. We further analyze language confusion, a key obstacle for generative tasks in cross-lingual ICL. Our results show that conventional fine-tuning-based expectations do not consistently apply in the ICL regime and point to alternative heuristics for selecting source languages effectively.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper investigates how to choose source languages when using large language models for cross-lingual in-context learning (i.e., giving a few examples in one language to perform a task in another language). The authors find that conventional wisdom from fine-tuning does not always apply to in-context learning, and they propose alternative strategies for selecting source languages.
summary.technical_summary: The paper systematically evaluates cross-lingual transfer in in-context learning (ICL) across 7 tasks, 6 multilingual models (e.g., GPT, LLaMA variants), and typologically diverse languages. It examines whether established fine-tuning heuristics (e.g., data availability, linguistic similarity) hold for ICL. Key findings: (1) fine-tuning-based expectations do not consistently transfer to ICL; (2) language confusion—where models fail to distinguish source from target language—is a major obstacle in generative tasks; (3) alternative heuristics (e.g., source language diversity, prompt formatting) improve cross-lingual ICL performance.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Seven NLP tasks across typologically diverse languages; six multilingual models including GPT and LLaMA variants.
summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates that fine-tuning-based intuitions for cross-lingual transfer do not hold for in-context learning, and provides new heuristics for source language selection in cross-lingual ICL.
summary.limitations: Limited to NLP tasks; no biomedical or clinical applications; does not address multimodal or structured data scenarios relevant to Weijie's research.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. The paper is purely about multilingual NLP and cross-lingual transfer in LLMs. It has no connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical domain relevant to Weijie's research.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely. Weijie's work focuses on causal inference, PRS, proteomics, and multimodal prediction in biomedical settings. This paper addresses a different problem (cross-lingual NLP) with no clear translational pathway to biomedical AI.
summary.possible_research_idea: None directly. If Weijie were building multilingual clinical NLP tools (e.g., for non-English EHR data), the findings on source language selection for ICL could be relevant, but this is not a current research direction.

title: INI-VPINN: A Variational Physics-Informed Neural Network with Implicit Neumann and Interface Handling for Multi-Material Domains with Geometric Singularities
comment: Preprint version. Under peer review. Code available at: https://github.com/ShayanDodge/INI-VPINN
summary: We propose a new weak-form Physics-Informed Neural Network approach (named INI-VPINN). INI-VPINN naturally incorporates Neumann boundary and interface conditions into the variational formulation. It removes the need for additional loss terms or multiple subdomain networks. This framework employs compact support weighting functions and integration by parts to implicitly impose flux and continuity constraints. In this way, it implicitly ensures physical consistency across material boundaries. The proposed method is tested on Poisson and Laplace problems with sharp interfaces and complex geometries. Results show that, compared with several other Physics Informed Neural Networks-based formulations, the INI-VPINN consistently achieves higher accuracy, smoother and faster convergence. The proposed framework provides a general approach for solving multimaterial problems with complex geometries and mixed Neumann-Dirichlet boundary conditions using neural networks. The implementation is publicly available in a GitHub repository.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents a new type of neural network designed to solve physics problems (like heat distribution or electromagnetic fields) in materials with different properties and complex shapes. It cleverly handles boundaries and interfaces between materials without needing extra training terms or separate networks for each material region.
summary.technical_summary: The authors propose INI-VPINN, a variational physics-informed neural network that implicitly enforces Neumann boundary conditions and interface flux/continuity conditions through the weak formulation using compact-support weighting functions and integration by parts. This eliminates the need for penalty loss terms or multi-domain network architectures. The method is validated on Poisson and Laplace problems with sharp interfaces and geometric singularities, showing superior accuracy and convergence compared to standard PINN, VPINN, and hp-VPINN formulations.
key_methods: Implicit Neumann boundary handling via weak formulation
Integration by parts for flux continuity
Multi-material domain decomposition without subdomain networks
summary.data_or_benchmark: Poisson and Laplace benchmark problems with sharp interfaces and complex geometries (no real-world biomedical data used).
summary.main_contribution: A novel VPINN formulation that naturally handles Neumann and interface conditions without additional loss terms or subdomain networks, achieving smoother convergence and higher accuracy on multi-material problems with geometric singularities.
summary.limitations: Tested only on relatively simple 2D Poisson/Laplace problems; scalability to 3D, time-dependent, or nonlinear problems is not demonstrated; no biomedical or real-world application context.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Very low. This is a computational physics paper focused on solving PDEs in multi-material domains. It has no direct connection to proteomics, pQTL, osteoarthritis, cardiometabolic traits, Mendelian randomisation, or any biomedical/epidemiological application.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly useful. The methods are designed for physics simulation (e.g., heat transfer, electromagnetics) rather than biomedical data analysis, causal inference, or multi-omics integration.
summary.possible_research_idea: If one were to stretch the connection, the variational framework for handling heterogeneous domains could conceptually inspire methods for integrating heterogeneous multi-omics data types, but this would be a very indirect and speculative link.

title: Catastrophic Forgetting is Low-Rank: A Function-Space Theory for Continual Adaptation
comment: Accepted to the ICML 2026 Workshop on Continual Adaptation at Scale: Towards Sustainable AI
summary: Catastrophic forgetting in continual adaptation is usually studied through parameter drift, replay, or distillation, but these views do not identify which output-space directions are vulnerable. We give a function-space account in the NTK regime: new-task training induces old-task prediction drift through the cross-task kernel, yielding a closed-form predictor for the forgetting vector before any new-task gradient step. In frozen-backbone linear-head PEFT-CL, where the model is linear in the trainable parameters, the predictor is exact up to numerical precision; for nonlinear adapters/full fine-tuning, it is a local NTK approximation. The same expression reveals that forgetting concentrates in a small number of old-task NTK eigenmodes and under frozen linear heads gives a Kronecker scaling rule for the vulnerable rank. These results clarify the relation to prior NTK-overlap theory, explain why parameter-space regularizers can miss output-space interference, and motivate a targeted spectral regularizer.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper studies why neural networks forget old tasks when learning new ones (catastrophic forgetting). The authors show mathematically that forgetting happens in a low-dimensional subspace of the model's output space, not just in the parameter space. They derive a formula that predicts exactly which outputs will be forgotten before training even begins, and show that forgetting concentrates in a small number of directions (eigenmodes). This leads to a new regularisation method that targets these vulnerable directions.
summary.technical_summary: The paper provides a function-space analysis of catastrophic forgetting in continual learning using the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) regime. For frozen-backbone linear-head PEFT-CL models (linear in trainable parameters), the authors derive an exact closed-form predictor for the forgetting vector before any gradient step on new tasks. This predictor depends on the cross-task NTK. They show forgetting concentrates in a small number of old-task NTK eigenmodes, giving a Kronecker scaling rule for the vulnerable rank. For nonlinear adapters/full fine-tuning, a local NTK approximation applies. A targeted spectral regulariser is proposed based on these insights.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Theoretical analysis with validation on continual learning benchmarks (not specified in abstract, but typical continual learning datasets like split CIFAR, permuted MNIST, etc.)
summary.main_contribution: A function-space theory showing catastrophic forgetting is low-rank in the NTK eigenbasis, with an exact closed-form predictor for forgetting in linear-head PEFT models, and a targeted spectral regulariser.
summary.limitations: NTK regime assumptions may not hold for deep nonlinear networks in practice; analysis limited to supervised continual learning settings; no direct connection to biomedical or clinical applications.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. This is a theoretical machine learning paper about catastrophic forgetting in continual learning, with no connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical application. The methods (NTK, spectral regularisation) are not obviously transferable to Weijie's research areas.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly useful. The continual learning framing does not map naturally to Weijie's work on PRS, MR, proteomics, or multimodal prediction. The spectral regularisation idea is too generic and disconnected from biomedical data structures.
summary.possible_research_idea: If Weijie were building a continually updating clinical prediction model that learns from streaming patient data across hospitals or time periods, catastrophic forgetting could become relevant. However, this is a stretch given current research directions.

title: Recursive Scaling in Masked Diffusion Models
summary: Masked diffusion models (MDMs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for sequence generation. Scaling MDMs is conventionally achieved by increasing the parameter count or the number of denoising steps. We introduce Recursive Masked Diffusion Models (R-MDMs), which add recursive depth as a third scaling axis by repeatedly applying the same denoising transformer within each diffusion step. Recursion enables iterative refinement of the output through parameter reuse, increasing effective model depth without increasing parameter count. Across structured generation tasks, including Sudoku and Countdown, we show that R-MDMs achieve substantially improved parameter efficiency: a model with $L$ recursive iterations often matches the performance of non-recursive baselines with roughly $L\times$ more parameters. Moreover, recursive refinement can partially substitute for additional denoising steps, allowing recursive models to reach the same generation quality with fewer forward passes at inference time. These results suggest that recursive depth is a practically useful scaling mechanism for MDMs, improving both parameter efficiency and the allocation of test-time compute.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper introduces a new way to scale masked diffusion models (used for generating sequences like text or structured data) by adding recursive depth—repeatedly applying the same transformer within each diffusion step—rather than just increasing parameters or denoising steps. This improves parameter efficiency and allows trading off compute for quality at inference time.
summary.technical_summary: The authors propose Recursive Masked Diffusion Models (R-MDMs), which add recursive depth as a third scaling axis by applying the same denoising transformer multiple times within each diffusion step. This enables iterative refinement through parameter reuse, increasing effective model depth without increasing parameter count. Evaluated on Sudoku and Countdown generation tasks, R-MDMs with L recursive iterations match non-recursive baselines with roughly L× more parameters. Recursive refinement can also substitute for additional denoising steps, reducing inference-time forward passes for equivalent quality.
key_methods: Parameter reuse via repeated transformer application
summary.data_or_benchmark: Sudoku puzzle generation, Countdown arithmetic task
summary.main_contribution: Introduces recursive depth as a third scaling axis for masked diffusion models, demonstrating improved parameter efficiency and flexible test-time compute allocation.
summary.limitations: Evaluated only on synthetic/structured generation tasks (Sudoku, Countdown); no application to biological sequences, protein generation, or biomedical data; limited comparison to other scaling approaches.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. This is a pure machine learning methods paper on generative sequence modelling with no direct connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical application. No biological data or clinical relevance.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly applicable. The recursive diffusion concept could theoretically be applied to protein sequence generation or missing modality imputation in multi-omics, but the paper does not explore this and would require substantial adaptation.
summary.possible_research_idea: If one were to extend this, recursive masked diffusion could be used for generating protein sequences with known structural constraints or for imputing missing proteomics data in multi-omics datasets. However, this is speculative and far from the author's current research trajectory.

title: LLM Consumer Behavior Theory: Foundations of a Novel Research Field
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents that make consumption decisions on behalf of users. This shift raises fundamental questions for consumer theory, which has traditionally modeled humans as the primary decision-makers. In this paper, we introduce LLM Consumer Behavior Theory, a new field of study concerned with analyzing consumer behavior in agentic markets. Drawing on classical and behavioral economics alongside recent advances in Natural Language Processing, we formalize how human preferences are reflected and acted upon by LLM-based agents, and how agent-level decisions aggregate into market demand. We unify previously fragmented literature on LLM decision-making, human behavior simulation, and preference elicitation under a common economic lens, highlighting where assumptions, such as rationality and heterogeneity, may fail in agentic markets. Rather than providing empirical validation, this paper outlines the scope of LLM consumer behavior and identifies open research questions related to alignment, preference representation, and market dynamics.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper proposes a new research field called "LLM Consumer Behavior Theory" that studies how large language models act as autonomous agents making purchasing and consumption decisions on behalf of humans. It combines ideas from economics and NLP to understand how human preferences get translated into LLM-driven decisions and how these aggregate into market demand.
summary.technical_summary: The authors formalise a framework for analysing agentic markets where LLM-based agents make consumption decisions. They draw on classical and behavioural economics, preference elicitation, and LLM alignment research to examine how human preferences are reflected (or distorted) in LLM agent behaviour. The paper is conceptual—it defines scope, identifies assumptions (rationality, heterogeneity) that may break in agentic markets, and outlines open research questions around alignment, preference representation, and market dynamics. No empirical validation is provided.
key_methods: Literature synthesis across economics and NLP
summary.data_or_benchmark: None (conceptual paper, no empirical data or benchmarks)
summary.main_contribution: Proposes a new interdisciplinary field (LLM Consumer Behavior Theory) and unifies fragmented literature on LLM decision-making, human behaviour simulation, and preference elicitation under an economic lens.
summary.limitations: No empirical validation; purely conceptual; no connection to biomedical, clinical, or health-related applications; no methodological tools or datasets provided.

title: C2FL: Clustered Continual Federated Learning under Spatial and Temporal Drift
summary: Collective Adaptive Systems (CAS) increasingly rely on machine learning to let each node learn from locally sensed data, aligning its behavior with the surrounding environment. Scaling this intelligence, however, raises fundamental challenges: sensed data is often privacy-sensitive, preventing centralized collection; nodes are mobile, traversing regions where nearby nodes perceive similar phenomena while distant ones observe radically different conditions, creating natural spatial clusters; and these distributions evolve over time due to mobility, introducing temporal drift that makes local models progressively stale. These dynamics arise across domains - vehicular sensing, drone-based monitoring, smartphone crowdsensing - yet the interplay of privacy, spatial heterogeneity, and temporal drift severely undermines conventional learning strategies. Therefore, we propose C2FL, a fully distributed Federated Learning (FL) approach where nodes self-organize into learning groups through spatial clustering, reflecting the geographic structure of the environment. To counteract temporal drift, each node combines experience replay with a dwell-time-aware adaptive averaging step, progressively incorporating the regional consensus as it remains longer within the same area, while preserving previously acquired knowledge under evolving distributions. We evaluate our approach on synthetic experiments that systematically reproduce spatial and temporal shifts, showing that standard federated strategies degrade significantly under these conditions and that our method restores robust collective adaptation.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper proposes a federated learning method called C2FL that allows mobile devices (like sensors on vehicles or drones) to collaboratively learn from data without sharing private information. It handles two key challenges: (1) devices in different locations observe different phenomena (spatial drift), and (2) the data distribution changes as devices move (temporal drift). The method groups nearby devices into clusters for collaborative learning and uses memory replay to retain knowledge when moving between regions.
summary.technical_summary: C2FL is a fully distributed federated learning framework designed for Collective Adaptive Systems (CAS) where nodes experience both spatial heterogeneity and temporal drift. Nodes self-organize into learning groups via spatial clustering based on geographic proximity. To handle temporal drift caused by node mobility, each node employs experience replay (storing and replaying past samples) combined with a dwell-time-aware adaptive averaging step that weights local and regional model updates based on how long a node has remained in its current region. The approach is evaluated on synthetic datasets that simulate spatial and temporal distribution shifts, comparing against standard FL baselines (e.g., FedAvg, FedProx). Results show that conventional FL strategies degrade significantly under these conditions, while C2FL restores robust collective adaptation.
key_methods: Spatial clustering for self-organized learning groups
Experience replay for temporal drift mitigation
Fully distributed federated learning without central server
summary.data_or_benchmark: Synthetic datasets designed to systematically reproduce spatial and temporal distribution shifts. No real-world or biomedical datasets used.
summary.main_contribution: A fully distributed FL approach that simultaneously addresses spatial heterogeneity and temporal drift through self-organized spatial clustering and dwell-time-aware adaptive averaging with experience replay.
summary.limitations: Evaluated only on synthetic data; no real-world validation. No connection to biomedical or clinical applications. The spatial clustering assumes geographic proximity is the primary source of data heterogeneity, which may not hold in many biomedical settings.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. This paper is about distributed systems and federated learning for mobile sensor networks. It does not address proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical domain relevant to Weijie's research.

title: AIGS-Net: Compact Illumination Field Modeling via 2D Gaussian Splatting for Fast Low-Light Image Enhancement
summary: Existing low-light image enhancement methods often face a bottleneck between the representation capacity of illumination-field modeling and computational complexity. To address this issue, this paper proposes an Adaptive Illumination Gaussian Splatting Network (AIGS-Net), an ultra-lightweight architecture for fast low-light enhancement. Unlike conventional static priors, AIGS-Net constructs an input-adaptive 2D Gaussian Splatting illumination field. The opacity of Gaussian basis functions is dynamically modulated by relative luminance statistics of the input image, and spatially varying illumination compensation is rendered through ordered alpha compositing. To guide adaptive illumination compensation efficiently, a zero-parameter nonlinear multiscale contextual encoding module is introduced to extract low-frequency structures and local contrast cues without additional convolutional weights. To suppress noise amplification and sensor-induced color bias, AIGS-Net integrates noise-mask estimation, locked single-channel Gamma mapping, cross-channel consistency regularization, and target color-alignment constraints. Experiments on LOL and LSRW benchmarks show that AIGS-Net improves detail recovery and color fidelity while requiring only approximately 40 learnable parameters, achieving an effective trade-off between enhancement quality and extreme inference efficiency.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents AIGS-Net, a super lightweight neural network for improving photos taken in low light. It uses a clever 2D Gaussian splatting technique to model how light should be adjusted across the image, requiring only about 40 learnable parameters — far fewer than typical deep learning models. It also handles noise and color issues common in low-light photography.
summary.technical_summary: AIGS-Net proposes an ultra-lightweight architecture for low-light image enhancement based on adaptive 2D Gaussian splatting illumination field modeling. The opacity of Gaussian basis functions is modulated by input-adaptive relative luminance statistics, and spatially varying illumination compensation is achieved via ordered alpha compositing. A zero-parameter nonlinear multiscale contextual encoding module extracts low-frequency structures and local contrast without trainable weights. The framework incorporates noise-mask estimation, locked single-channel Gamma mapping, cross-channel consistency regularization, and target color-alignment constraints to suppress noise and color bias. The model achieves competitive enhancement quality on LOL and LSRW benchmarks with only ~40 learnable parameters.

title: Gaussian Light Field Splatting: A Physical Prior-Driven Vision Transformer for Unsupervised Low-Light Image Enhancement
summary: Existing unsupervised low-light image enhancement methods often encounter local exposure imbalance and color distortion under complex non-uniform illumination. In addition, most Vision Transformers lack an explicit mechanism for modeling the physical priors of illumination degradation. To address these limitations, we propose GLFS, a Gaussian light field splatting-based Vision Transformer that integrates continuous physical illumination modeling from Gaussian splatting into the Transformer architecture. In GLFS, scene illumination is represented by a superposition of anisotropic Gaussian basis functions. Physics-guided biases are introduced into self-attention to adaptively infer a spatial gain field, enabling accurate and uniform restoration under complex illumination. To reduce color bias and structural degradation during enhancement, a color-vector angular loss and a luminance-edge loss are further developed. These losses enforce hue consistency and improve the structural fidelity of local details. Extensive ablation studies and quantitative evaluations show that GLFS provides clear advantages in illumination correction and detail preservation. It achieves state-of-the-art performance and offers a new representation paradigm for low-light image enhancement.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper proposes a new method for improving low-light images using a Vision Transformer that incorporates physical knowledge about how light behaves. summary.technical_summary: GLFS integrates Gaussian splatting-based continuous physical illumination modeling into a Vision Transformer architecture for unsupervised low-light image enhancement. Scene illumination is represented as a superposition of anisotropic Gaussian basis functions. Physics-guided biases are introduced into the self-attention mechanism to adaptively infer a spatial gain field for uniform restoration. title: MoCo-AIS: A Contrastive Learning Framework for Similarity Computation of Vessel Trajectories
summary: Trajectory similarity is a fundamental task in analyzing mobility patterns, essential for applications such as route pattern extraction, mobility prediction, and anomaly detection. Traditional distance-based measures for computing similarity incur high computational cost, driving the adoption of lightweight learning-based approaches. Supervised methods rely on extensive labels derived from traditional distance measures and often reproduce these metrics, which limits generalization. While self-supervised learning addresses this issue through contrastive learning, it lacks a unified framework, making it difficult to compare deep learning (DL) models for consistent trajectory representation. Accordingly, this paper presents MoCo-AIS, a unified framework for learning vessel trajectory embeddings based on the Momentum Contrast (MoCo) paradigm, which formulates similarity learning through positive and negative trajectory pairs. Within this framework, we evaluate a diverse set of leading DL models on large-scale, real-world vessel-tracking AIS datasets that capture diverse navigation behaviors and operating conditions. Results demonstrate that our framework significantly improves similarity learning over existing baselines, while providing a benchmarking platform for evaluating trajectory representation models.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents a contrastive learning framework (MoCo-AIS) for measuring similarity between vessel trajectories using AIS tracking data. It adapts the Momentum Contrast (MoCo) self-supervised learning approach to learn trajectory embeddings without requiring expensive labels, and benchmarks multiple deep learning models on real-world maritime data.
summary.technical_summary: MoCo-AIS applies the Momentum Contrast (MoCo) paradigm to vessel trajectory similarity learning. It constructs positive and negative trajectory pairs from AIS data and trains deep learning models (e.g., RNNs, CNNs, transformers) to produce embeddings where similar trajectories are close in latent space. The framework avoids reliance on traditional distance-based similarity measures (e.g., DTW, Hausdorff) and their associated computational costs, and provides a unified benchmarking platform for evaluating trajectory representation models on large-scale maritime datasets.

title: Fine-tuning LLMs for Passive Depression Severity Estimation from AI Mental Health Dialogue
summary: Depression is the leading cause of disability worldwide, and early detection of symptom change is essential for timely intervention. Validated instruments such as the Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) support symptom monitoring at scale, but real-world completion rates are low, introducing response bias and systematic missingness. Passive approaches that infer severity from routinely generated data could close this gap. We address this by predicting PHQ-9 total scores directly from transcripts of conversations between users and an AI mental health application, requiring only conversation text and no additional clinical data. We fine-tune a Qwen3.5-27B backbone with a regression head, augment 3,111 ground-truth labels with pseudolabels generated by a reasoning model (Claude Opus) and iteratively trained intermediate models, for a combined dataset of 6,283 users. On a held-out test set of 842 users, our best model achieves MAE = 2.6, RMSE = 4.0, Pearson r = 0.80, and AUC = 0.91 at the PHQ-9 >= 10 clinical threshold. We also find AUC > 0.87 at every severity threshold from PHQ-9 >= 3 to PHQ-9 >= 24, demonstrating that the model captures depression severity across the full clinical spectrum. This work opens the door to passive, continuous symptom monitoring in AI mental health platforms, without requiring users to complete self-report measures.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper fine-tunes a large language model (Qwen3.5-27B) to predict depression severity (PHQ-9 scores) directly from transcripts of conversations with an AI mental health app, without requiring users to fill out questionnaires. The model achieves strong accuracy (MAE=2.6, Pearson r=0.80, AUC=0.91 for detecting clinically significant depression) by augmenting a small labelled dataset with pseudolabels from a reasoning model.
summary.technical_summary: The authors fine-tune a Qwen3.5-27B LLM with a regression head to predict PHQ-9 total scores from user-AI conversation transcripts. Training data: 3,111 ground-truth labels augmented with pseudolabels from Claude Opus and iteratively trained intermediate models, totalling 6,283 users. Test set: 842 held-out users. Best model achieves MAE=2.6, RMSE=4.0, Pearson r=0.80, AUC=0.91 at PHQ-9>=10 threshold, with AUC>0.87 across all severity thresholds (PHQ-9>=3 to >=24). This demonstrates passive depression severity estimation without self-report measures.

PHQ-9 severity prediction from conversation transcripts

summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates that LLMs fine-tuned on conversation transcripts can accurately estimate depression severity across the full clinical spectrum without requiring self-report questionnaires, enabling passive continuous monitoring.

title: Reload-Mamba: Hierarchical Anti-Dilution State-Space Modeling for Multi-Class Semantic Segmentation
comment: 23 pages, 4 figures, 17 tables. Code will be released soon
summary: Mamba-based state space models offer linear-time long-range modeling for high-resolution dense prediction, but sequential state-space propagation can attenuate boundary-sensitive and detail-sensitive responses that are critical in multi-class semantic segmentation. We propose Reload-Mamba, a semantic segmentation framework that addresses this propagation-induced response dilution through three segmentation-specific designs: (i) a boundary-supervised local detail prior that is explicitly trained with ground-truth boundary masks to identify regions requiring response restoration; (ii) a class-uncertainty-aware Reload Gate that incorporates per-pixel class entropy from a pre-reload auxiliary head as an additional gating signal, a formulation that is informative only under multi-class dense prediction; and (iii) a hierarchical multi-level Reload mechanism that applies anti-dilution refinement at three decoder levels and fuses the restored representations top-down. Built upon a ConvNeXt-Tiny encoder with a multi-scale decoder and four-directional Mamba scanning with pixel-wise directional attention, Reload-Mamba achieves 47.9% single-scale (48.9% multi-scale) mIoU on ADE20K and 83.2% single-scale mIoU on Cityscapes. With ResNet-101 + COCO pre-training under the standard DeepLab-style protocol, Reload-Mamba reaches 87.8% mIoU on PASCAL VOC 2012 val. Controlled ablations show that each of the three segmentation-specific designs contributes beyond a direct port of the prior anti-dilution architecture proposed for binarization, cumulatively improving over the direct-port baseline by +2.2 mIoU on ADE20K.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents Reload-Mamba, a deep learning model for semantic segmentation (pixel-level image classification) that improves upon Mamba-based state space models. The key problem it addresses is that as information flows through the model sequentially, fine details and object boundaries get "diluted" or lost. The authors propose three fixes: (1) training the model to explicitly recognise object boundaries, (2) using a gating mechanism that considers how uncertain the model is about each pixel's class, and (3) applying these refinements at multiple levels of the model's decoder. The method achieves strong results on standard segmentation benchmarks like ADE20K, Cityscapes, and PASCAL VOC.
summary.technical_summary: Reload-Mamba introduces a hierarchical anti-dilution framework for multi-class semantic segmentation built on Mamba state-space models. The architecture uses a ConvNeXt-Tiny encoder with a multi-scale decoder and four-directional Mamba scanning with pixel-wise directional attention. Three novel components address response dilution: (i) a boundary-supervised local detail prior trained with ground-truth boundary masks to identify regions needing response restoration; (ii) a class-uncertainty-aware Reload Gate that incorporates per-pixel class entropy from a pre-reload auxiliary head as an additional gating signal, which is only informative under multi-class settings; and (iii) a hierarchical multi-level Reload mechanism applying anti-dilution refinement at three decoder levels with top-down fusion of restored representations. Results: 47.9% single-scale (48.9% multi-scale) mIoU on ADE20K, 83.2% single-scale mIoU on Cityscapes, and 87.8% mIoU on PASCAL VOC 2012 val with ResNet-101 + COCO pretraining. Ablations show cumulative +2.2 mIoU improvement over direct port of prior anti-dilution architecture.
key_methods: Four-directional Mamba scanning with pixel-wise directional attention
summary.data_or_benchmark: ADE20K (47.9% single-scale mIoU), Cityscapes (83.2% single-scale mIoU), PASCAL VOC 2012 val (87.8% mIoU with ResNet-101 + COCO pretraining)
summary.main_contribution: Proposes three segmentation-specific designs (boundary supervision, class-uncertainty gating, hierarchical multi-level refinement) to address response dilution in Mamba-based state-space models for multi-class semantic segmentation, achieving competitive results on standard benchmarks.

No validation on medical imaging (e.g., X-ray, MRI) or any biological data. The improvements are incremental (+2.2 mIoU) and the architecture is tailored specifically to semantic segmentation tasks.

title: A Neuro-Symbolic Approach to Strategy Synthesis for Strategic Logics
summary: Reasoning about what agents can achieve through strategic interaction is a core challenge in Multi-Agent Systems (MAS). Logics for strategic ability, such as ATL, provide rigorous methods, but their adoption is often hindered by the computational cost of strategy synthesis. We introduce a neuro-symbolic framework that integrates large language models (LLMs) into the model-checking pipeline for MAS. The LLM acts as a strategy-generation oracle, proposing candidate strategies that are then formally validated by a standard MAS model checker. This generate-and-certify architecture uses LLM guidance to navigate large combinatorial strategy spaces while preserving formal soundness: generated strategies are accepted only when certified by the verifier. We instantiate the framework for bounded strategic reasoning in NatATL and introduce the first NatATL strategy-synthesis dataset, consisting of 4211 instances. Experiments with an open-weight Qwen3-32B model show that our certified pipeline achieves 92\% accuracy on strategy-synthesis outcomes.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents a method that combines large language models (LLMs) with formal verification to automatically generate strategies for multi-agent systems. The LLM proposes candidate strategies, and a formal model checker verifies whether they satisfy the required logical properties. This approach is tested on a new dataset for strategic reasoning, achieving 92% accuracy.
summary.technical_summary: The authors propose a neuro-symbolic framework for strategy synthesis in Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) using Alternating-time Temporal Logic (ATL) and its bounded variant NatATL. The architecture follows a generate-and-certify paradigm: an LLM (Qwen3-32B) serves as a strategy-generation oracle, proposing candidate strategies that are then formally validated by a standard MAS model checker. Only certified strategies are accepted, preserving formal soundness. The framework is instantiated for bounded strategic reasoning in NatATL, and the authors introduce the first NatATL strategy-synthesis dataset with 4,211 instances. Experimental results show 92% accuracy on strategy-synthesis outcomes.

summary.main_contribution: A neuro-symbolic framework integrating LLMs into MAS model-checking for strategy synthesis, combining LLM-guided search with formal verification to ensure soundness.
