title: Understanding Long-Term Dynamics of Individual Metro Usage: A Hidden Semi-Markov State Framework with Survival Analysis
summary: Understanding how individual metro usage evolves over multi-year horizons is essential for transit planning and passenger retention. However, existing approaches typically characterize mobility patterns as static clusters or short-term variability, leaving the lifecycle dynamics of transit participation underexplored. This study proposes a state-based lifecycle modeling framework that integrates Hidden Semi-Markov Models (HSMM) with discrete-time survival analysis to characterize the evolution of individual metro mobility. The HSMM infers latent mobility states with explicit duration distributions and a transition matrix governing regime changes, while the survival component models exit and re-entry events via state-dependent hazard functions conditioned on mobility-state trajectories and behavioral history. Applied to four years of smart card data from the Shanghai metro system (2021-2024), the framework enables the identification of interpretable mobility states, the characterization of transition dynamics, and the quantification of state-dependent exit and re-entry processes. The analysis reveals five robust mobility states with a directional transition hierarchy centered on an occasional-usage gateway state, and fundamentally different temporal mechanisms governing disengagement and return: exit hazard is state-dependent but duration-independent, whereas re-entry hazard decays sharply with inactivity length. These findings provide a methodological foundation for lifecycle-oriented mobility analysis and practical guidance for transit operators to identify at-risk users and time retention interventions.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper uses smart card data from the Shanghai metro to model how individual passengers' travel behaviour changes over four years. It combines a Hidden Semi-Markov Model (which identifies distinct travel states like frequent, occasional, or inactive) with survival analysis (which models how long people stay in each state and what makes them leave or return). The authors find five stable travel patterns and show that the reasons people stop using the metro differ from the reasons they start again.
summary.technical_summary: The authors propose a lifecycle framework integrating Hidden Semi-Markov Models (HSMM) with discrete-time survival analysis to model individual metro usage dynamics over multi-year horizons. The HSMM infers latent mobility states with explicit sojourn-time distributions and a transition matrix, while the survival component models state-dependent exit and re-entry hazards conditioned on mobility-state trajectories and behavioural history. Applied to four years of Shanghai metro smart card data (2021–2024), the framework identifies five robust mobility states with a directional transition hierarchy centred on an occasional-usage gateway state. Key findings include that exit hazard is state-dependent but duration-independent, whereas re-entry hazard decays sharply with inactivity length.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Four years of Shanghai metro smart card data (2021–2024), individual-level transaction records
summary.main_contribution: A novel integration of HSMM with survival analysis to model long-term lifecycle dynamics of individual transit behaviour, revealing asymmetric temporal mechanisms for disengagement vs. re-engagement.
summary.limitations: Domain-specific to urban transit; no biomedical or genetic data; single-city dataset; no validation in other populations or transport systems.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. The paper is about metro usage modelling, not proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical topic. The methodological combination of state-space models with survival analysis is interesting but not novel in biostatistics/epidemiology, where similar approaches (latent class + survival) are already standard.

However, this is a stretch and existing multi-state models in survival analysis (e.g., illness-death models, latent Markov models) already cover this territory more directly.

title: MoonSplat: Monocular Online Gaussian Splatting with Sim(3) Global Optimization
summary: Online 3D reconstruction from monocular image sequences is a challenging and ongoing research topic. 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), leveraging its high-quality real-time rendering capability, empowers online 3D reconstruction to represent dense scenes with enhanced expressiveness, and thus holds great promise for a wide range of applications such as robotics and AR/VR. However, existing online 3DGS methods still suffer from some key challenges: fragile camera pose estimation due to the lack of global optimization, and low optimization efficiency in large-scale or long-sequence scenarios. To address these issues, we propose a robust and efficient online voxelized 3DGS reconstruction framework integrated with global $\text{Sim}(3)$ optimization, which enables reliable camera tracking and efficient global loop closure for both camera poses and voxelized 3DGS. To accelerate the convergence of the voxelized 3DGS, we further introduce a color residual learning strategy, which not only boosts optimization speed but also enhances rendering quality. Extensive experiments on diverse indoor and outdoor datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in both camera pose estimation accuracy and rendering quality, while retaining real-time efficiency. Additionally, we develop and deploy a real-world UAV-based active reconstruction system grounded on our proposed method, validating its robustness and generalizability for practical online 3D reconstruction tasks. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/TrickyGo/MoonSplat.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents MoonSplat, a new method for reconstructing 3D scenes in real-time from a single moving camera (like a drone or phone video). It uses a technique called 3D Gaussian Splatting combined with global optimization to improve camera tracking and scene quality, especially for large or long sequences. The authors also built a real drone-based system to demonstrate it works in practice.
summary.technical_summary: MoonSplat addresses online monocular 3D reconstruction by integrating global Sim(3) optimization into a voxelized 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) framework. It tackles two key issues in prior online 3DGS methods: fragile camera pose estimation from lack of global optimization, and low efficiency in large-scale/long-sequence settings. The framework enables reliable camera tracking and global loop closure for both poses and voxelized 3DGS. A color residual learning strategy accelerates convergence and improves rendering quality. Evaluated on diverse indoor/outdoor datasets, achieving SOTA in pose estimation accuracy and rendering quality with real-time performance. A real-world UAV-based active reconstruction system validates practical robustness.

Color residual learning for faster convergence
Loop closure for both poses and 3DGS

summary.main_contribution: First online voxelized 3DGS framework with global Sim(3) optimization enabling robust camera tracking, efficient loop closure, and real-time high-quality 3D reconstruction from monocular video, validated on a real UAV system.

title: Predictive Analytics in E-Commerce for CustomerBehavior Forecasting using hybrid Ret-DNN withXGBoost Model
comment: 2025 2nd International Conference on Software, Systems and Information Technology (SSITCON)
summary:
In recent years, electronic (E) commerce services have rapidly increased in the daily lives of people, which helpsthem to purchase products online. However, retail platforms have struggled to understand customer behavior and make it difficult to predict their future purchases. To overcome these challenges, this study proposes a hybrid Retail Deep NeuralNetwork (Ret-DNN) with an Extreme Gradient Boosting(XGBoost) model for capturing temporal features and tabular dynamics of retail data. First, data were sourced from a UnitedKingdom (UK)-based online retailer that contains transactions with almost 500,000 records. Then, the collected data were pre-processed using a series of techniques, such as data cleaning, outlier handling, temporal feature extraction, feature encoding, and z-score normalization, to ensure that the data were ready for model training and testing. Subsequently, the preprocessed data were fed into the Ret-DNN model, which acts as a feature extractor to understand the complete context of customer transactions. Further, the extracted data were fed as input into the XGBoost model, which predicted the final output as the purchase probability of customers. Finally, the proposed Ret-DNN XGBoost model achieved better results by attaining aMean Absolute Error (MAE) 0.2193 when compared to the existing Ret-DNN model.
  Keywords: Customer behavior forecasting, extreme gradientboosting, electronic commerce, predictive analytic, retail deepneural networks.

It focuses on feature extraction via a retail-specific deep neural network followed by gradient boosting for final purchase probability prediction.

title: How Inference Compute Shapes Frontier LLM Evaluation
summary: AI evaluations are shifting toward harder tasks that benefit from longer trajectories involving tool use and iterative problem solving. As a result, performance is increasingly sensitive to the amount and allocation of compute available at test time ("inference compute"). Yet many evaluations still report performance at a single restrictive budget, meaning that low scores may reflect the evaluation setup rather than the model's underlying capability. To test this, we evaluate up to 12 frontier language models on seven challenging benchmarks spanning software engineering, mathematics, medicine, and cybersecurity. We use a controlled setup combining three simple inference-scaling interventions: larger token budgets, context compaction, and repeated submission attempts, guided either by the model itself or by minimal correctness feedback. We find three main results. First, larger token budgets substantially improve performance on benchmarks across multiple domains, including cybersecurity, FrontierMath, Humanity's Last Exam, and TerminalBench. Second, fixed-budget evaluations can increasingly understate frontier capability as models advance. Newer models reach higher performance at large budgets, where they unlock harder tasks and solve them more reliably. Third, benchmarks differ in which inference-scaling methods help most: repeated submission broadly improves performance, but the value of larger token budgets, external feedback, and parallel attempts varies by benchmark. Overall, our results show that benchmark scores are protocol-dependent. We therefore argue that evaluations should report capability as a function of inference-time compute, specify protocol choices explicitly, and compare model generations over a large shared compute range at matched budgets, especially in safety- or policy-relevant settings.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper investigates how the amount of computing power available during AI model testing (inference compute) affects performance scores on challenging benchmarks. The authors tested 12 frontier language models on 7 hard benchmarks across software engineering, maths, medicine, and cybersecurity. They found that giving models more computing resources during testing substantially improves their scores, meaning that current benchmark rankings may be misleading because they often test models under restrictive conditions. The paper argues that evaluations should report performance as a function of available compute rather than as a single fixed score.
summary.technical_summary: The study evaluates up to 12 frontier LLMs on 7 challenging benchmarks (SWE-bench, FrontierMath, Humanity's Last Exam, TerminalBench, cybersecurity tasks, and medical benchmarks) using three inference-scaling interventions: (1) larger token budgets, (2) context compaction, and (3) repeated submission attempts with self-guided or correctness-feedback strategies. Results show that performance is highly protocol-dependent: larger token budgets yield substantial gains across domains, newer models benefit more from increased budgets, and different benchmarks respond differently to different scaling methods. The authors argue for reporting capability as a function of inference-time compute rather than single-budget scores.
key_methods: Inference-scaling interventions (token budgets, context compaction, repeated submissions)

title: PearlVLA: Progressive Embodied Action-Plan Refinement in Latent Space
summary: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models face a trade-off between efficient action generation and explicit deliberation. Directly decoding actions from vision-language backbone representations enables low-latency control, whereas explicit reasoning through textual chains, pixel-level subgoals, or action search can improve planning but incurs substantial latency and computational cost. We propose PearlVLA, a VLA framework that moves deliberation into the latent space of a vision-language model (VLM). PearlVLA separates VLM meta-query representations into a fixed visual grounding branch and an iterative latent plan branch. At each refinement round, a plan-conditioned world query probes a lightweight frozen latent world model for an action-free future observation latent, which is fed back to guide plan refinement. A future-guided RefineNet then applies scheduled residual updates to progressively refine a coarse semantic draft into a fine-grained latent action plan. The refined plan after K rounds is then decoded in parallel into an action chunk for low-latency execution. We further introduce Causal Refinement-Grouped Process-Reward RL to optimize the latent refinement process with rewards from longer-horizon imagined futures induced by latent plan edits. Empirical evaluations on the LIBERO benchmark demonstrate that PearlVLA achieves state-of-the-art performance among existing methods.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents a new AI system called PearlVLA that helps robots understand language commands and plan actions more efficiently. Instead of thinking step-by-step in text (which is slow), it does its planning in a compressed "latent space" inside the AI model, making it faster while still allowing for careful refinement of plans. It uses a lightweight world model to imagine future outcomes and iteratively improves its action plan before executing it.
summary.technical_summary: PearlVLA introduces a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework that performs action planning refinement entirely in the latent space of a vision-language model (VLM). It separates VLM meta-query representations into a visual grounding branch and an iterative latent plan branch. A plan-conditioned world query probes a frozen latent world model to obtain action-free future observation latents, which guide a RefineNet that applies scheduled residual updates to progressively refine a coarse semantic draft into a fine-grained latent action plan. After K refinement rounds, the plan is decoded in parallel into an action chunk. The training uses Causal Refinement-Grouped Process-Reward RL to optimize the refinement process using rewards from longer-horizon imagined futures induced by latent plan edits. Evaluated on LIBERO benchmark, achieving SOTA.

Future-guided RefineNet with scheduled residual updates

title: ChLogic: Evaluating Robustness of Logical Reasoning in Chinese Expressions
summary: Large language models perform increasingly well on standardized logical reasoning benchmarks, but whether this ability remains robust beyond English is unclear. We introduce ChLogic, an English--Chinese aligned benchmark that tests whether models preserve logical reasoning performance when the same latent logical structure is expressed in English and diverse Chinese surface realizations. Built from formal logical templates, the benchmark contains three data sets: (i) the General aligned set, derived from 60 General Propositions across nine template families; (ii) the Difficult aligned set, derived from 40 Difficult Problems; and (iii) the Chinese-only set, covering 15 language-specific phenomenon types. Each aligned item pairs one English reference expression with five Chinese realizations. Experiments on Qwen3, Ministral, and GLM models reveal a persistent English--Chinese performance gap. Back-translation from standard Chinese into English often improves performance on the General aligned set, but produces mixed effects on the Difficult aligned set, where Qwen3-32B and GLM-5.1 perform worse after translation. These results indicate that Chinese surface realization, translation artifacts, and model-specific behavior jointly affect multilingual logical reasoning. Overall, ChLogic provides a useful stress test for the robustness of multilingual reasoning.

Built from formal logical templates, it comprises three datasets: General aligned (60 propositions, 9 template families), Difficult aligned (40 problems), and Chinese-only (15 language-specific phenomena). Each aligned item pairs one English reference with five Chinese surface realizations. Experiments on Qwen3, Ministral, and GLM models show a persistent English-Chinese performance gap. Back-translation from Chinese to English improves performance on the General set but yields mixed results on the Difficult set, with Qwen3-32B and GLM-5.1 performing worse after translation.

title: Learn to Quantify Social Interaction with Constraints for Pedestrian Walking
summary: Long-term human path forecasting in crowds is critical for autonomous moving platforms (like autonomous driving cars and social robots) to avoid collision and make high-quality planning. Although the current research take into account social interactions for prediction, they don't reveal the exact kinds of social interactions happened among people and how the social interactions affect the decision-making process of pedestrians, which further limits its robustness. Social interactions in pedestrian walking are intuitively massive and hard to label and quantify. In this paper, we explore creatively to quantify and interpret how pedestrians interact with others by proposing Learn to Cluster. Our clustering social interactions is probabilistic latent variable generative, learning directly from sequential trajectory observations, scalable to arbitrary number of pedestrians. Learn to cluster is label-free and can be naturally integrated into the training process of the prediction model. The latent variables will then serve as 'labels' to categorize social interactions. Extensive experiments over several trajectory prediction benchmarks demonstrate that our method is able to learn the patterns of social interactions and effectively integrate the patterns to pedestrian trajectory prediction.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

It is label-free, scalable to arbitrary numbers of pedestrians, and can be integrated into trajectory prediction models. The latent variables serve as pseudo-labels for categorizing interaction types, improving prediction robustness.

title: Dynamic Rollout Editing for Reducing Overthinking in RL-Trained Reasoning Models
summary: Long-form chain-of-thought reasoning can improve LLM performance on complex tasks, but models often continue generating unnecessary reasoning after a correct answer has emerged. We refer to this behavior as overthinking. We study this phenomenon from the perspective of GRPO-style reinforcement learning (RL) post-training, framing it as a training-time credit-assignment problem rather than merely a decoding-time stopping problem. In rollouts sampled at the onset of GRPO training, we observe that successful trajectories can exhibit a slightly higher degree of overthinking than unsuccessful trajectories for the same prompts. This early imbalance provides a starting point for an undesirable feedback loop: because GRPO assigns sequence-level credit, it cannot distinguish the solution-reaching prefix from the unnecessary continuation that lengthens a successful trajectory. Both receive positive update signal, allowing the initial imbalance to grow into more severe overthinking during training. To address this issue, we introduce Dynamic Rollout Editing (DRE), a training-time intervention for successful trajectories that continue thinking after answer emergence. DRE preserves the accepted verified prefix, edits the remaining thinking, and prefers the edited trajectory within the same RL group, weakening the preference signal for unnecessary thinking without penalizing the reasoning needed to reach the answer. Experiments across diverse tasks show the effectiveness of DRE.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper addresses the problem of "overthinking" in large language models trained with reinforcement learning (specifically GRPO). When models generate long chain-of-thought reasoning, they often continue producing unnecessary reasoning steps even after the correct answer has already been reached. The authors propose Dynamic Rollout Editing (DRE), a training-time method that edits successful trajectories by cutting off unnecessary reasoning after the answer emerges, which reduces overthinking without harming performance.
summary.technical_summary: The paper studies overthinking in GRPO-style RL post-training for reasoning LLMs. It identifies an early training imbalance where successful trajectories exhibit slightly more overthinking than unsuccessful ones, which GRPO's sequence-level credit assignment amplifies into severe overthinking. DRE intervenes at training time: for successful trajectories that continue thinking after answer emergence, it preserves the verified prefix (up to the correct answer), edits the remaining thinking, and prefers the edited trajectory within the same RL group. This weakens the preference signal for unnecessary thinking without penalizing reasoning needed to reach the answer. Experiments span diverse reasoning tasks.

summary.main_contribution: Identifies overthinking as a training-time credit-assignment problem in RL-trained reasoning models, and proposes DRE as a simple training-time intervention that reduces unnecessary reasoning without sacrificing performance.

title: AI Adoption Across a Multinational Workforce: Sociotechnical Conditions for GenAI Acceptance in Human Resources
summary: Generative AI (GenAI) deployment in the workplace is accelerating rapidly. Nevertheless, questions of who adopts, who benefits, and who is left behind and why are still understudied. In this paper, we investigate these dynamics in the context of a multinational tech company transitioning from a legacy Human Resources (HR) search system to a GenAI-supported system, analyzing search log data, survey data (n=25), and ten semi-structured interviews. Our findings show that adoption depended on the fit between the GenAI system's design assumptions and employees' work positionalities (role, spoken language, tenure). Further, we find that employees' trust in GenAI answers was built through source-checking, comparison among systems, and seeking input from colleagues or HR when in doubt. Our contribution is twofold. First, we provide empirical evidence of workplace GenAI adoption during a live organizational transition, showing that adoption is influenced by factors such as situational fit, search literacy, and trust calibration. It is also further shaped by knowledge conditions such as the system's content quality, employee training, and guidance. Second, we translate these findings into design considerations for inclusive deployment and adoption in high-stakes environments such as HR. We argue that organizations should design systems considering the role and context-sensitive benefits they yield to different social groups. They also need to treat the organizational knowledge infrastructure as AI infrastructure to improve the accountability and usability of GenAI systems

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

The paper contributes empirical evidence on workplace GenAI adoption and translates findings into design considerations for inclusive deployment.

title: Structural Preservation and the Logical Expressiveness of Graph Neural Networks
summary:
Bridges between graph neural networks (GNNs) and logical formalisms have been established by fixing architectural choices, such as the types of aggregation, combination, and activation functions. These choices define restricted classes of GNNs for which tight correspondences with logical formalisms can be obtained, by showing that logical formulae can be translated into equivalent GNNs and, conversely, that GNNs can be translated into equivalent formulae.
  In this paper we take a semantic perspective by establishing the logical expressiveness of classes of GNN classifiers that are preserved under structural properties: embeddings (extensions), injective homomorphisms, and homomorphisms. We show that, for each such property, there exists a fragment of graded modal logic characterising the class of GNNs. In particular, preservation under embeddings, injective homomorphisms, and homomorphisms corresponds to existential graded modal logic, its existential-positive fragment, and existential-positive modal logic, respectively. These results characterise the expressiveness of broad classes of GNNs independently of specific architectural choices, but we also show that each of these classes admits a GNN architecture of the same expressiveness.
  Technically, our approach uses a new well-quasi-order result for trees of bounded height, yielding finite representations of unravelling-invariant classes.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper studies how well graph neural networks (GNNs) can express logical properties. It shows that GNNs that preserve certain structural relationships (like embeddings or homomorphisms) correspond to specific fragments of modal logic. The authors prove that these GNN classes can be characterised by logical formulas, independent of the specific architecture used.
summary.technical_summary: The paper establishes a semantic characterisation of GNN expressiveness by studying classifiers preserved under three structural properties: embeddings (injective homomorphisms that preserve relations both ways), injective homomorphisms, and homomorphisms. For each property, the authors identify a fragment of graded modal logic that exactly captures the class of GNNs satisfying that preservation property. Specifically, preservation under embeddings corresponds to existential graded modal logic, under injective homomorphisms to its existential-positive fragment, and under homomorphisms to existential-positive modal logic. The authors also show that each class admits a GNN architecture achieving the same expressiveness. Technically, they prove a well-quasi-order result for trees of bounded height to obtain finite representations of unravelling-invariant classes.
key_methods: Semantic preservation properties (embeddings, injective homomorphisms, homomorphisms)
summary.data_or_benchmark: No empirical data or benchmarks used; purely theoretical paper.
summary.main_contribution: Provides a semantic (rather than syntactic/architectural) characterisation of GNN expressiveness in terms of logical fragments, independent of specific architectural choices. Proves that preservation under structural properties corresponds exactly to fragments of graded modal logic.
summary.limitations: Purely theoretical with no empirical validation. Does not address practical GNN architectures used in biomedical applications. The logical fragments characterised may not directly translate to useful properties for real-world graph learning tasks.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. This is a theoretical computer science paper about GNN expressiveness and modal logic. It has no connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical application. Weijie's research focuses on epidemiology, causal inference, and clinical prediction, not on the theoretical foundations of graph neural networks.

title: Revisiting Structural Dependency in Autoregressive Multi-Task Table Recognition via Order-Independent Cell-Level Representations
summary: Multi-task table recognition jointly addresses table structure prediction, cell localization, and cell content recognition within a unified framework. Existing approaches often rely on autoregressive decoders to generate table structures and reuse their hidden states for cell localization and content recognition. This autoregressive generation process can make cell representations order-dependent, degrading global consistency across cells. This paper proposes a structural refinement module that produces order-independent cell features through non-causal attention. This design enables parallel inference of cell contents while conditioning each cell on global context encoded in the refined features. Experiments on two large datasets demonstrate consistent gains in cell localization and end-to-end recognition, while reducing overall inference time by around threefold.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper proposes a method to improve table recognition (extracting tables from documents) by making the model less dependent on the order it processes table cells. Instead of generating cells one by one in sequence, it uses a refinement module that lets all cells be processed in parallel, improving accuracy and speed.
summary.technical_summary: The paper addresses multi-task table recognition (structure prediction, cell localization, and content recognition) by introducing a structural refinement module that replaces autoregressive decoding with non-causal attention. This produces order-independent cell-level representations, enabling parallel inference of cell contents conditioned on global context. Experiments on two large table recognition datasets show consistent gains in cell localization and end-to-end recognition, with ~3x inference speedup.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Two large table recognition datasets (not named in abstract)
summary.main_contribution: Proposes a structural refinement module that enables order-independent, parallel cell content inference in multi-task table recognition, improving both accuracy and inference speed.
summary.limitations: Limited to table recognition in computer vision; no direct connection to biomedical or omics data.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Very low. This is a computer vision / document analysis paper with no connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical application.

title: StepGuard: Guarding Web Navigation via Single-Step Calibration
summary: Web navigation requires agents to follow natural language goals, interact with web pages, and produce accurate answers. While recent advances leverage vision-language models and reinforcement learning, existing methods still suffer from single-step fragility due to reward misalignment and error propagation. To tackle the reward entanglement, we design Dynamic Dual-Policy Optimization (DDPO), which dynamically switches between a navigation-first mode for exploration and an answer-first mode for question-answering to mitigate reward conflict. To calibrate the single-step error, we propose Confidence-Guided Adaptive Navigation Reflection (CANR), a mechanism that estimates per-step confidence, triggers reflection only when necessary, and uses contrastive rewards to encourage self-correction to calibrate the single-step inaccuracy. With the above as the main components, we finally develop our StepGuard, a new framework of Guarding Web Navigation via Single-Step Calibration. Experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly improves navigation and answer accuracy, setting new state-of-the-art performance on standard web navigation benchmarks.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents StepGuard, a new method to improve how AI agents navigate websites. It uses two main techniques: Dynamic Dual-Policy Optimization (DDPO) which switches between exploring the page and answering questions to avoid conflicting goals, and Confidence-Guided Adaptive Navigation Reflection (CANR) which checks if each step is correct and only asks the agent to reconsider when confidence is low. The approach achieves better results on standard web navigation benchmarks.
summary.technical_summary: StepGuard addresses single-step fragility in web navigation agents by introducing DDPO, a dual-policy reinforcement learning framework that dynamically switches between navigation-first and answer-first modes to mitigate reward misalignment. CANR estimates per-step confidence using the agent's internal representations, triggers reflection only when confidence falls below a threshold, and applies contrastive rewards to encourage self-correction. The framework is evaluated on standard web navigation benchmarks and achieves state-of-the-art performance.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Standard web navigation benchmarks (not specified in abstract, likely Mind2Web, WebArena, or similar)
summary.main_contribution: A framework combining dual-policy RL with confidence-guided reflection to improve single-step accuracy in web navigation agents.
summary.limitations: Not specified in abstract; likely domain-specific to web navigation and may not generalise to biomedical or clinical tasks without adaptation.

title: GameCraft-Bench: Can Agents Build Playable Games End-to-End in a Real Game Engine?
summary: Game generation is an emerging application of coding agents, requiring models to transform natural-language specifications into playable interactive systems. Unlike traditional coding tasks, game generation takes place within a game engine, where scripts, scenes, assets, rendering, and runtime interactions must jointly produce coherent gameplay. We formalize end-to-end game generation as the problem of producing a complete game artifact that realizes a specification through observable player-game interaction in a target environment. We argue that evaluating this setting requires three desiderata: Engine Grounding, Artifact Completeness, and Interactive Verification. We propose an interaction-grounded evaluation framework that assesses executable gameplay through replayed demonstrations and rubric-guided multimodal judging. We instantiate this framework as GameCraft-Bench, a benchmark comprising 140 Godot tasks across 15 game families. Evaluations of frontier coding agents show that end-to-end game generation remains highly challenging: the strongest agent achieves only 41.46%, and most agents score below 40%. Further analysis reveals that while agents often implement recognizable mechanics, they struggle to deliver complete games with sufficient content, functional visual feedback, and coherent presentation. See https://tongxuluo.github.io/gamecraft-bench-website for demos, code, and data.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper introduces a benchmark called GameCraft-Bench that tests how well AI coding agents can build complete, playable video games from scratch using the Godot game engine. The benchmark includes 140 game development tasks across 15 game types. The authors found that even the best AI agent only succeeded 41% of the time, and most scored below 40%, showing that end-to-end game generation remains very difficult for current AI systems.
summary.technical_summary: GameCraft-Bench formalises end-to-end game generation as producing a complete game artifact from natural-language specifications within a real game engine (Godot). The framework requires three desiderata: Engine Grounding (using actual engine APIs), Artifact Completeness (full project files), and Interactive Verification (playable demonstrations). Evaluation uses replayed demonstrations and rubric-guided multimodal judging. The benchmark contains 140 tasks across 15 game families. Frontier coding agents (e.g., GPT-4 based agents) achieve at most 41.46% success, with most below 40%.
key_methods: Godot engine-based task suite (140 tasks, 15 families)
summary.data_or_benchmark: GameCraft-Bench: 140 Godot game development tasks across 15 game families
summary.main_contribution: Formalises and benchmarks the problem of end-to-end game generation by coding agents in a real game engine, revealing that current agents struggle to produce complete, playable games with coherent visual feedback and sufficient content.
summary.limitations: Limited to Godot engine; results may not generalise to other engines or non-game coding tasks. The benchmark focuses on small-scale games; larger game generation remains unexplored.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: No direct relevance. The paper is about AI game generation in the Godot engine, with no connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, causal inference, or any biomedical application.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: None. This paper does not address any topic in Weijie's research areas (epidemiology, genetic epidemiology, proteomics, multi-omics, causal inference, clinical prediction).
summary.possible_research_idea: No viable research ideas for Weijie's domain. The benchmark and methods are specific to game development and do not transfer to biomedical or multi-omics settings.

title: Meta-classification of one-class classification models using ranking correlation and nearest neighbor
summary: Machine Learning (ML) techniques have been applied to various problems. However, applying ML to ML models is an unexplored direction. For this purpose, this paper considers a meta-classification of one-class classification (OCC) models, because all ML models could be approximated as OCC models. The proposal represents OCC models as normality rankings and classifies them using nearest-neighbor and ranking-correlation metrics. The experiment classifies OCC models, where classes correspond to training datasets, algorithms, and hyperparameters. The proposal achieves high accuracy when class labels are datasets. Moreover, it can classify algorithms when the training datasets contain the same class. In addition, the discussion highlights that the classification of OCC models is essentially the classification of datasets that treats multiple samples as a single input. The experiment demonstrates the classification of datasets using sleeping records. The proposed method can provide a unified solution for classifying OCC models, datasets, and rankings. Source code is uploaded to the public repository https://github.com/ToshiHayashi/ClassOCC.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper proposes a method to classify machine learning models (specifically one-class classification models) by treating them as ranking vectors and using nearest-neighbor and correlation-based metrics. It shows that this meta-classification approach can identify which dataset, algorithm, or hyperparameter generated a given model, with high accuracy when classifying by dataset.
summary.technical_summary: The authors propose a meta-classification framework for one-class classification (OCC) models. OCC models are represented as "normality rankings" — ordered lists of test instances by their anomaly scores. These rankings are then classified using nearest-neighbor methods with ranking correlation metrics (e.g., Spearman, Kendall). The approach is evaluated on classifying OCC models by their training dataset, algorithm type, and hyperparameters. Experiments show near-perfect accuracy for dataset classification and reasonable performance for algorithm classification when training datasets share the same class. The method is also demonstrated on sleep record data for dataset classification.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Synthetic and benchmark OCC datasets; sleep record dataset for demonstration. No standard biomedical or proteomics benchmark used.
summary.main_contribution: A novel meta-classification framework that treats OCC models as ranking vectors and classifies them using ranking correlation and nearest-neighbor methods, providing a unified solution for classifying models, datasets, and rankings.
summary.limitations: Limited to OCC models; no connection to biomedical, proteomics, or multi-omics data; no clinical or translational validation; small-scale experiments; unclear scalability to large foundation models or high-dimensional omics data.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. The paper is purely methodological in ML and does not address proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical application. No overlap with Weijie's research focus.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely. The meta-classification of OCC models has no clear application to Mendelian randomization, PRS, proteomics, or multimodal clinical prediction in osteoarthritis.
summary.possible_research_idea: If extended, one could imagine using ranking-based representations to compare anomaly detection models on proteomics data (e.g., identifying outlier protein signatures in OA), but this is highly speculative and far from the current paper.

title: FlowRAG: Synergizing Explicit Reasoning via Frequency-Aware Multi-Granularity Graph Flow
summary: Graph-based retrieval-augmented generation (GraphRAG) is effective for knowledge-intensive and multi-hop query tasks; however, many existing methods primarily seed entity-based graphs and rely on implicit semantic relevance propagation. This often (i) under-retrieves when user queries are abstract and semantically sparse at the entity level, and (ii) suffers from brittle multi-hop reasoning, where noisy activations can derail entity-to-entity transitions and corrupt the inferred relation chain, yielding unreliable conclusions. To this end, we propose \texttt{FlowRAG}, a semantic-aware retrieval framework that improves both semantic recall and explicit reasoning. Specifically, \texttt{FlowRAG} constructs a quad-level heterogeneous graph over passages, summaries, sentences, and entities, where summary nodes serve as a coarse semantic hub. At retrieval time, a dual-granularity activation module combines summary--query alignment with sentence-level matching to activate relevant entities under paraphrase and abstraction robustly. We then introduce a frequency-aware weighted flow module that routes relevance through entity--passage links weighted by within-passage term frequency, pruning noisy connections and extracting high-confidence reasoning paths as an explicit logic skeleton for generation. Extensive experiments show that \texttt{FlowRAG} obtains state-of-the-art performance on complex reasoning benchmarks.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: FlowRAG is a new method for improving how AI systems retrieve and reason with information when answering complex questions. It builds a multi-level graph (passages, summaries, sentences, entities) and uses frequency-aware weighting to prune noise and extract clear reasoning paths. The goal is to make retrieval-augmented generation more reliable for multi-hop queries.
summary.technical_summary: FlowRAG constructs a quad-level heterogeneous graph (passages, summaries, sentences, entities) for retrieval-augmented generation. It uses a dual-granularity activation module combining summary-query alignment with sentence-level matching to handle abstract queries. A frequency-aware weighted flow module routes relevance through entity-passage links weighted by within-passage term frequency, pruning noisy connections and extracting explicit reasoning paths as logic skeletons for generation. Evaluated on complex reasoning benchmarks.
key_methods: Quad-level heterogeneous graph construction (passages, summaries, sentences, entities)
Frequency-aware weighted flow module with term-frequency pruning
Explicit reasoning path extraction as logic skeletons
summary.data_or_benchmark: Complex reasoning benchmarks (e.g., multi-hop QA datasets) — not biomedical specific.
summary.main_contribution: A semantic-aware retrieval framework that improves both recall and explicit multi-hop reasoning for GraphRAG via frequency-aware flow and multi-granularity graph construction.
summary.limitations: No biomedical or clinical validation; tested only on general-domain reasoning benchmarks; no integration with structured biomedical knowledge bases or ontologies; computational overhead of quad-level graph construction not discussed.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. This is a pure CS/AI paper on retrieval-augmented generation with no connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or causal inference. The graph-based reasoning approach could theoretically be adapted for biomedical knowledge retrieval, but the paper provides no such application.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly applicable. If Weijie were building a literature-based reasoning system for causal inference or drug-target discovery, the multi-granularity retrieval concept might inform system design, but this would require substantial adaptation and biomedical validation.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could adapt FlowRAG's frequency-aware flow for retrieving relevant pQTL or proteomics evidence from literature when constructing causal graphs for drug-target MR. However, this is speculative and far from the paper's scope.

title: A homotopy-type-theoretic generalization of neurosymbolic inference
summary: A wide range of neurosymbolic (NeSy) systems compute one functional: a belief-weighted sum of a logical quantity over a space of $σ$-structures, of which weighted model counting, fuzzy logic, and probabilistic logic are special cases. This account is built on sets, and a set deliberately forgets two things that are important for NeSy: when two $σ$-structures are the same up to a symmetry of the theory, and how many distinct proofs witness a query. Replacing the underlying sets by types, in the sense of homotopy type theory, preserves this information, and turns this functional into a belief-weighted homotopy cardinality, a notion of size that counts each object in inverse proportion to its symmetries. We develop the framework from scratch for NeSy systems, prove a conservativity theorem that recovers the classical functional when symmetries are trivial, and show that the symmetry our framework exposes is exactly the one behind reasoning shortcuts. The payoff is concrete: the shortcut-aware concept posterior that recent methods reach by ensembling or expressive density estimation is the only symmetry-invariant point of the confusion-set simplex, computable in closed form by averaging a single model over the symmetry group. On MNIST reasoning-shortcut benchmarks this single-model wrapper is better calibrated than a diversity-trained ensemble, while leaving label accuracy and identifiable concepts untouched. Code is freely available at https://github.com/bio-ontology-research-group/hott-nesy.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper develops a new mathematical framework for neurosymbolic AI systems using homotopy type theory. It shows that current approaches lose information about symmetries and multiple proofs, and proposes a method that preserves this information to avoid "reasoning shortcuts" — where models give correct answers for wrong reasons. The approach improves model calibration on reasoning tasks without sacrificing accuracy.
summary.technical_summary: The authors generalize the standard NeSy functional (a belief-weighted sum over σ-structures) by replacing sets with homotopy types. This turns the functional into a belief-weighted homotopy cardinality that accounts for symmetries (automorphisms of σ-structures) and multiple distinct proofs. They prove a conservativity theorem recovering the classical functional when symmetries are trivial, and show that reasoning shortcuts correspond to symmetry-breaking. The shortcut-aware concept posterior is derived as the unique symmetry-invariant point on the confusion-set simplex, computable via averaging over the symmetry group. Experiments on MNIST reasoning-shortcut benchmarks show improved calibration over diversity-trained ensembles with identical accuracy.
summary.data_or_benchmark: MNIST reasoning-shortcut benchmarks (standard NeSy evaluation tasks)
summary.main_contribution: A homotopy-type-theoretic reformulation of neurosymbolic inference that naturally accounts for symmetries and multiple proofs, providing a principled solution to reasoning shortcuts with a closed-form symmetry-invariant posterior.
summary.limitations: Theoretical framework is mathematically dense; demonstrated only on MNIST-based toy reasoning tasks, not on real-world biomedical or clinical data; scalability to large-scale multi-omics or EHR data is unclear.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. This is a pure AI/mathematical foundations paper with no connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical application. The neurosymbolic framework is not designed for causal inference, PRS, or multimodal clinical prediction.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly applicable. The symmetry-aware reasoning framework could theoretically be relevant if Weijie were building neurosymbolic models for clinical reasoning, but the paper does not address biomedical data or causal inference tasks.
summary.possible_research_idea: If one were to extend this to biomedical reasoning, the symmetry-aware posterior could potentially be used to debias concept learning in clinical prediction models (e.g., ensuring a model doesn't rely on spurious correlations in imaging data). However, this would require substantial bridging work.

title: WallZero: Mastering the Game of WallGo with Strategic Analysis
comment: Accepted by the Computers and Games conference (CG 2026)
summary: WallGo is a recently introduced strategic board game popularized by the 2025 Netflix series The Devil's Plan. Although played on a small 7 x 7 board, its combination of stone movement and wall placement yields high game-tree complexity and intricate strategic interactions. Despite its growing popularity, WallGo remains underexplored. This paper presents WallZero, an AlphaZero-based agent for the two-player WallGo setting. We introduce tailored action and feature designs to improve playing performance significantly. In the evaluation, WallZero defeats two professional Go players who participated in this study, securing on average 1.98x more territory per game. Beyond its strength, we use WallZero to assess game fairness and identify key strategies for mastering WallGo. Interestingly, our results show that the opening used in the Netflix series yields a more balanced game. Our code is available at https://rlg.iis.sinica.edu.tw/papers/wallzero.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents WallZero, an AI agent based on AlphaZero that learns to play WallGo—a strategic board game featured in the Netflix series The Devil's Plan. The agent uses custom action and feature designs to outperform professional human players, securing nearly twice as much territory per game. The authors also analyse game fairness and opening strategies.
summary.technical_summary: WallZero adapts the AlphaZero framework (deep neural networks + Monte Carlo Tree Search) to the two-player board game WallGo. The authors design tailored action representations and feature encodings specific to WallGo's mechanics (stone movement and wall placement). The agent is trained via self-play without human knowledge. Evaluation against two professional Go players shows WallZero achieves 1.98x more territory on average. The paper also uses the trained agent to analyse game balance across different opening strategies.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Self-play training data; evaluation against two professional Go players in WallGo matches.
summary.main_contribution: First strong AI agent for the WallGo board game, demonstrating that AlphaZero-style methods can be effectively adapted to this game with tailored feature/action designs, and providing strategic analysis of game fairness.
summary.limitations: Narrow domain (board game AI); no connection to biomedical or health-related research; no methodological novelty beyond standard AlphaZero adaptation.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: No direct relevance. The paper is about board game AI with no connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical topic in Weijie's research scope.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: None. The methods (AlphaZero, MCTS, self-play RL) are not applicable to Weijie's research in genetic epidemiology, causal inference, or multimodal clinical prediction.
summary.possible_research_idea: No viable research ideas for Weijie's domain arise from this paper.

title: High-Fidelity 3D Geometric Reconstruction of Pelvic Organs from MRI: A Hybrid Deep Learning and Iterative Optimization Approach
summary: Patient-specific 3D reconstruction of pelvic organ geometry from MRI is important for pelvic floor modeling and downstream patient-specific analysis. However, while previous studies have focused primarily on either image segmentation or downstream use of 3D models, the reconstruction of high-fidelity, high-quality geometries remains labor-intensive and poorly standardized. The study introduced a hybrid deformable shape modeling framework that integrates deep learning prediction with iterative optimization for the reconstruction of the bladder, uterus, and rectum. The framework consists of three core components: a geometry-aware multi-level deep learning architecture that preserves topological consistency of pelvic organs; a two-stage amortized optimization training strategy that balances global shape capture and local surface refinement; and a holistic synergy mechanism--where iterative optimization provides supervision for deep learning during the training phase, and during inference, deep learning rapidly predicts the global organ morphology, followed by iterative optimization to refine local surfaces and mesh quality. This framework demonstrated marked superiority in geometric fidelity than current mainstream deep learning-based organ reconstruction models. For individual anatomical structures, the reconstructed 3D geometries for the bladder, rectum, and uterus achieved significantly lower Chamfer Distance values and higher Dice Similarity Coefficient scores. In addition, while maintaining high computational efficiency, the proposed architecture yielded superior overall volumetric mesh quality. At the patient level, the framework achieved higher mean values for the 10 worst elements for both minSICN and minSIGE compared to traditional geometric post-processing algorithms.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents a deep learning framework for reconstructing 3D models of pelvic organs (bladder, uterus, rectum) from MRI scans. It combines a neural network with iterative optimization to produce high-quality, topologically consistent 3D geometries that are useful for surgical planning and biomechanical modeling.
summary.technical_summary: The authors propose a hybrid deformable shape modeling framework integrating a geometry-aware multi-level deep learning architecture with iterative optimization for 3D reconstruction of pelvic organs from MRI. The pipeline includes: (1) a multi-level network preserving topological consistency; (2) a two-stage amortized optimization strategy balancing global shape capture and local surface refinement; and (3) a holistic synergy mechanism where iterative optimization supervises deep learning during training, and during inference, DL predicts global morphology followed by iterative refinement. Evaluated on pelvic MRI data, the method achieves lower Chamfer Distance and higher Dice scores for bladder, rectum, and uterus compared to mainstream segmentation-to-mesh pipelines, with superior volumetric mesh quality.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Pelvic MRI dataset (bladder, uterus, rectum segmentation and 3D reconstruction). Evaluated using Chamfer Distance, Dice Similarity Coefficient, and mesh quality metrics (minSICN, minSIGE).
summary.main_contribution: A hybrid framework that combines deep learning with iterative optimization to produce high-fidelity, topologically consistent 3D reconstructions of pelvic organs from MRI, outperforming standard segmentation-based approaches in geometric accuracy and mesh quality.
summary.limitations: Focused exclusively on pelvic organs (bladder, uterus, rectum) from MRI; generalizability to other anatomical sites or imaging modalities not demonstrated. Requires ground truth 3D shapes for training. Computational cost of iterative optimization during inference may limit real-time applications.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. This is a computer vision / medical imaging paper focused on 3D geometric reconstruction of pelvic organs. It does not address proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, cardiometabolic traits, Mendelian randomization, PRS, or any of Weijie's core research areas.

title: Perceptual compensation for tonal context in self-supervised speech models
summary: This study examines the extent to which the wav2vec2.0 architecture exhibits evidence of compensation for phonological context. We conducted a pseudo-replication of a perceptional compensation experiment on Mandarin Chinese tones, and compared the embedding similarities and probing classifier outputs between a purely self-supervised pre-trained model and a model fine-tuned for Mandarin ASR. No evidence of compensation was found in the embedding similarities of the purely pre-trained model. Probing classifiers showed some evidence of compensation in addition to the expected layer-wise improvements in categorization, but failed to replicate human performance on isolated test syllables. Our findings contrast with previous reports of sensitivity to phonological structure emerging through pre-training alone, and suggest that supervised objectives may be necessary to encourage the abstraction of at least some types of phonological regularities.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper tests whether a self-supervised speech model (wav2vec2.0) can learn to compensate for tonal context in Mandarin Chinese, similar to how humans perceive tones. The authors found that the pre-trained model alone did not show this ability, and only after fine-tuning for speech recognition did some compensation emerge, though still not matching human performance.
summary.technical_summary: The study pseudo-replicates a perceptual compensation experiment for Mandarin Chinese tones using wav2vec2.0. Embedding similarities and probing classifier outputs were compared between a purely self-supervised pre-trained model and a model fine-tuned for Mandarin ASR. Results showed no evidence of tonal context compensation in the pre-trained model's embeddings. Probing classifiers on the fine-tuned model showed some compensation effects and layer-wise improvements in categorisation, but failed to replicate human-level performance on isolated syllables.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Mandarin Chinese tone perception experiment data; no specific benchmark dataset named.
summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates that self-supervised pre-training alone may not be sufficient for learning phonological context compensation, and that supervised fine-tuning is needed for at least some types of phonological regularities.
summary.limitations: Limited to Mandarin tones and a single architecture (wav2vec2.0); does not fully replicate human perception; narrow scope for biomedical applications.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Minimal direct relevance. The paper is about speech processing and self-supervised learning in NLP/ASR, with no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, genetics, or clinical prediction.

summary.possible_research_idea: None that align with Weijie's research focus on osteoarthritis, cardiometabolic traits, MR, pQTL, PRS, or multimodal clinical prediction.

title: When Multiple Scripts Matter: Evaluating ASR in Clinical Settings
summary: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) in non-English clinical settings is challenged by multiscript variability, where the same term may appear in multiple valid orthographic forms. Conventional string-matching evaluation metrics often underestimate ASR performance by treating orthographic variants as errors. To address this issue, we introduce MultiClin, a clinical ASR benchmark designed to evaluate robustness to multiscript variability. Experiments across diverse ASR models show that multiscript-aware evaluation provides a fairer assessment of recognition quality than conventional single-reference evaluation. We further investigate the impact of script consistency during training and find that inconsistent script mappings increase orthographic uncertainty and hinder model convergence, with a balanced 50% mapping ratio producing the highest entropy. In contrast, script unification consistently yields the best ASR performance. Our dataset and code are publicly available at: https://github.com/aitrics-ronaldo/Interspeech_MultiClin.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper introduces a new benchmark called MultiClin for evaluating automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems in clinical settings where multiple scripts (e.g., Hangul, Hanja, Romanisation) are used interchangeably. It shows that standard evaluation metrics unfairly penalise ASR models when they correctly recognise a term but in a different valid script, and proposes a multiscript-aware evaluation approach. The study also examines how script consistency during training affects model performance.
summary.technical_summary: The authors propose MultiClin, a clinical ASR benchmark designed to handle multiscript variability—where the same clinical term can appear in multiple valid orthographic forms (e.g., Korean Hangul vs. Hanja vs. Romanisation). They demonstrate that conventional string-matching metrics (e.g., WER, CER) underestimate ASR performance by treating orthographic variants as errors. They introduce multiscript-aware evaluation that considers all valid script forms. Experiments across diverse ASR models show this provides fairer assessment. They also investigate the impact of script consistency during training, finding that inconsistent script mappings increase orthographic uncertainty and hinder convergence, with a balanced 50% mapping ratio producing highest entropy, while script unification yields best ASR performance.
key_methods: Entropy analysis of script mapping ratios
summary.data_or_benchmark: MultiClin: a clinical ASR benchmark for Korean multiscript variability (Hangul, Hanja, Romanisation). Dataset and code publicly available on GitHub.
summary.main_contribution: Introduces multiscript-aware evaluation for clinical ASR to avoid underestimating model performance due to orthographic variability, and provides a benchmark (MultiClin) for this purpose.
summary.limitations: Focused on Korean language clinical ASR; limited generalisability to other languages or non-clinical domains. Does not address downstream clinical NLP tasks beyond ASR evaluation.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Atlas-Based 3D Asset Segmentation for Interactive Content Workflows
summary: Segmenting 3D assets into meaningful regions remains challenging, especially when segmentation criteria are application-dependent and require user control. We present a human-in-the-loop pipeline for generating a segmented 2D parameterized atlas from a 3D model for interactive media, game, and XR content workflows. Our method first selects a compact set of rendered views using a greedy set cover strategy over sampled surface points, and then supports interactive segmentation of these views with SAM~2 and Label Studio. The resulting masks are back-projected onto the model's UV parameterization to produce a unified segmented atlas that supports downstream production tasks such as segment-wise material assignment, style transfer, and semantic labeling. We assess the pipeline through a demonstration-based technical evaluation on eight cultural heritage objects. The results show that the approach can generate usable segmented atlases across diverse geometries while revealing recurring sources of manual correction, particularly fine structures, cavities, and weak appearance boundaries.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents a method for interactively segmenting 3D models into meaningful parts using a human-in-the-loop approach. It combines automated view selection with user-guided segmentation tools (SAM 2 and Label Studio) to create a segmented 2D atlas from a 3D model, useful for game development, XR content, and cultural heritage preservation.

These views are then interactively segmented using SAM 2 and Label Studio. The resulting 2D segmentation masks are back-projected onto the model's UV parameterization to produce a unified segmented atlas. The pipeline is evaluated on eight cultural heritage objects, demonstrating usability across diverse geometries while identifying recurring issues with fine structures, cavities, and weak appearance boundaries.

title: Improving low-resource ASR using bilingual fine-tuning with language identification: a cross-linguistic evaluation
summary: This study explores how bilingual fine-tuning affects automatic speech recognition (ASR) in low-resource languages. We evaluate this method across nine linguistically and geographically diverse language pairs, covering a range of language families and writing systems. To distinguish the two languages, during training, we pre-pend each input text with a language identification token. At inference, the model jointly predicts both the language and transcription from the speech input alone. As texts for which the language is incorrectly determined show low ASR performance, we also conduct a follow-up experiment in which the language identification token is provided both during training and inference. Our results show that bilingual fine-tuning can be beneficial when language identification accuracy is high, and that in cases where language identification performance is low, including the language identification token at inference helps to improve ASR performance.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper investigates whether fine-tuning an automatic speech recognition (ASR) model on two languages simultaneously (bilingual fine-tuning) can improve performance for low-resource languages. The authors test this across nine language pairs, using a language identification token to help the model distinguish between languages. They find that bilingual fine-tuning helps when the model can accurately identify which language is being spoken, and that providing the language token at inference time further boosts performance.
summary.technical_summary: The study evaluates bilingual fine-tuning of a pre-trained ASR model (likely Whisper or similar) on low-resource language pairs. A language identification (LID) token is prepended to input text during training. At inference, the model jointly predicts language and transcription from speech. Nine diverse language pairs are tested. Results show bilingual fine-tuning improves ASR when LID accuracy is high; when LID is poor, providing the LID token at inference (rather than predicting it) improves performance. The paper provides a cross-linguistic evaluation across different language families and writing systems.

title: Beyond Native Success: Auditing Deployment-Interface Exposure of CLIP Backdoors
summary: Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training models are widely reused across downstream interfaces, including feature extraction, retrieval, reranking, and selection. Existing CLIP backdoor, however, usually validate attacks on a small attack-native task, leaving unclear whether the same poisoned checkpoint remains exposed, weakens, or becomes not applicable when reused through other interfaces. We introduce DIFE, a Deployment-Interface Footprint Evaluation framework that audits backdoored CLIP checkpoints across deployment interfaces. DIFE makes various evaluations comparable by specifying each interface's component readout, trigger channel, target event, reference condition, and metric. DIFE also introduces effective-footprint diagnosis to identify the reusable CLIP component or component combination that carries exposure and explains where risk transfers. Auditing reproduced CLIP backdoors with DIFE reveals a structured landscape: native success is not a checkpoint-level risk certificate, exposure follows component footprints, text-side poisoning does not yield textual-encoder control, and some coupled attacks remain mechanism-bound. This audit reveals a import gapin existing CLIP backdoors: a textual encoder that itself becomes a reusable carrier of adversarial behavior. We therefore introduce BadTextTower to fill this gap. BadTextTower produces strong text-conditioned retrieval, reranking, and selection exposure while leaving visual-only reuse nearly clean.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper studies security vulnerabilities in CLIP models (which connect images and text) when they are reused in different ways like feature extraction, image retrieval, or ranking. The authors find that a backdoor attack that works for one type of task may not transfer to others, and they propose a framework to systematically audit these risks. They also introduce a new attack method that specifically targets the text encoder to make it dangerous across multiple text-based reuse scenarios.
summary.technical_summary: The paper introduces DIFE (Deployment-Interface Footprint Evaluation), a framework for auditing backdoor attacks on CLIP models across four deployment interfaces: feature extraction, retrieval, reranking, and selection. DIFE standardises evaluation by specifying component readout, trigger channel, target event, reference condition, and metric for each interface. It also introduces effective-footprint diagnosis to identify which CLIP components carry adversarial exposure. The authors audit existing CLIP backdoor methods and find that native-task success does not guarantee cross-interface risk; exposure follows component footprints, and text-side poisoning does not necessarily yield textual-encoder control. To address the identified gap—lack of a textual encoder that itself becomes a reusable carrier of adversarial behaviour—they propose BadTextTower, which produces strong text-conditioned retrieval, reranking, and selection exposure while keeping visual-only reuse nearly clean.
key_methods: CLIP backdoor auditing across four deployment interfaces
summary.data_or_benchmark: CLIP models with reproduced backdoor attacks; no specific biomedical dataset used.
summary.main_contribution: A systematic framework (DIFE) for auditing CLIP backdoor risks across deployment interfaces, and a new attack (BadTextTower) that fills the gap of reusable textual-encoder adversarial behaviour.
summary.limitations: Focused exclusively on CLIP vision-language models; no connection to biomedical or clinical data; no evaluation on healthcare tasks; purely a computer security paper.

The topic is computer security/ML safety, not biomedical AI or causal inference.

title: QueryMarket: Cost-Aware Online Active Learning in Data Markets
comment: 10 pages, 8 figures. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
summary: Data acquisition is a major bottleneck for learning in real-time streams: analysts must decide on the fly which labels to purchase while respecting a rolling budget. However, existing online active learning rarely unifies pricing, information gain, and rolling budget constraints under concept drift. We introduce QueryMarket, a market-inspired framework that queries each incoming data point based on its estimated utility to the model and its price. Within this framework, we propose OVBAL (online variance-based active learning), which integrates data pricing with information-driven selection by estimating each sample's marginal utility via a D-optimality criterion with exponential forgetting and executing cost-aware purchases under rolling budget constraints. OVBAL yields a simple, fully online decision rule that adapts to nonstationary streams and heterogeneous label costs. Experiments on synthetic data and a real-world solar power generation forecasting task show that OVBAL is particularly effective under seller-centric pricing and yields a more favorable long-run error-cost trade-off in the real-world task under both pricing schemes.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper proposes a new method for buying data labels on the fly when you have a limited budget. It treats data acquisition like a marketplace, deciding which data points to purchase based on how useful they are to the model and how much they cost. The method adapts to changing data patterns over time.
summary.technical_summary: QueryMarket is a framework for online active learning under rolling budget constraints with concept drift. The authors propose OVBAL (online variance-based active learning), which uses a D-optimality criterion with exponential forgetting to estimate each sample's marginal utility, integrates data pricing, and executes cost-aware purchases under rolling budget constraints. The method yields a fully online decision rule that adapts to nonstationary streams and heterogeneous label costs. Evaluated on synthetic data and a solar power forecasting task.

summary.main_contribution: A unified framework (QueryMarket) and algorithm (OVBAL) that jointly considers data pricing, information gain, and rolling budget constraints under concept drift for online active learning.

title: MaineCoon: Pursuing A Real-Time Audio-Visual Social World Model
summary: As an increasing majority of global video content is consumed on social platforms for interactive social purposes, video generation models built for social worlds are important but largely overlooked by previous studies. In this work, we define the position of social world models and build a prototype model as the first step towards this goal. While previous world models successfully simulate physical environments or gaming world exploration, they remain fundamentally detached from human-centric social dynamics. To bridge this gap as the first step to social world models, we present MaineCoon, the first real-time audio-visual autoregressive model that has 22B parameters and is capable of real-time streaming generation and sub-second interaction, with a record-breaking frame rate of up to 47.5 FPS, on a single GPU. To the best of our knowledge, MaineCoon is also the first real-time audio-visual generation model specifically optimized for social-interactive applications. To enable efficient and stable training, we introduce several novel techniques into MaineCoon, including self-resampling, cross-modal representation alignment, domain-aware preference optimization, and reinforced online-policy distillation (ROPD). We also design the first agentic streaming inference framework that supports thousand-second-scale or even longer generation while mitigating drift with agentic cache management and prompt planing. These innovations significantly accelerate training while optimizing real-time inference performance. We believe this work not only sets a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance benchmark for high-quality, low-latency, and long-horizon audio-visual autoregressive models, but also points out the paradigm shift desired for next-generation AI-native social platforms.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

It focuses on creating realistic social interactions (like conversations or reactions) rather than just physical scenes. summary.technical_summary: MaineCoon is a real-time audio-visual autoregressive model (22B parameters) designed for social-interactive applications.
