title: Do Generative Recommenders Deepen the Information Cocoon? A Closed-Loop Simulation with LLM-powered User Simulators
summary: Recommender systems alleviate information overload, yet repeated feedback between recommendations and user interactions can reinforce existing preferences and narrow users' exposure, forming information cocoons. While this phenomenon has been widely studied in traditional sequential recommendation, its impact on generative recommendation remains unclear. By replacing atomic item IDs with Semantic ID (SID) sequences, generative recommenders introduce a different recommendation mechanism whose role in information cocoon formation is not yet understood. To investigate whether generative recommenders deepen information cocoons, we propose \textsc{RecLoop}, a closed-loop simulation framework with LLM-driven user agents. We compare two generative recommenders and two traditional sequential baselines on two Amazon datasets across multiple feedback cycles. In addition to standard exposure-level metrics, we introduce \emph{Code-Space Structural Cocoon}, a model-level metric that measures concentration in the generated SID space. Experimental results show that generative recommenders are generally less prone to exposure-level cocoon formation than traditional baselines, preserving broader exposure diversity and slowing cross-user homogenization. However, feedback loops can still induce concentration within the generated SID space. We further find that cocoon severity depends strongly on tokenization strategy and model scale: collaborative-signal tokenization produces stronger cocoon effects than semantic tokenization, whereas larger models maintain greater code-space diversity and better retain access to niche content. These findings suggest that information cocoons in generative recommendation are shaped not only by recommendation behavior, but also by item tokenization and model capacity. Our code is available at https://github.com/Dregen-Yor/RecLoop.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper investigates whether generative recommender systems (which use AI to generate recommendations) make the "information cocoon" problem worse — where users only see content that reinforces their existing preferences. The authors built a simulation framework with LLM-powered user agents to test this, comparing generative recommenders against traditional ones. They found generative recommenders actually maintain broader diversity than traditional methods, but can still create concentration in the internal code space used to represent items. The severity depends on how items are tokenized and model size.
summary.technical_summary: The paper proposes RecLoop, a closed-loop simulation framework using LLM-driven user agents to study information cocoon formation in generative recommendation. Generative recommenders replace atomic item IDs with Semantic ID (SID) sequences, introducing a different mechanism. The authors compare two generative recommenders (TIGER, E4SRec) and two traditional sequential baselines (SASRec, BERT4Rec) on Amazon datasets across multiple feedback cycles. They introduce a model-level metric called Code-Space Structural Cocoon to measure concentration in the generated SID space. Results show generative recommenders are less prone to exposure-level cocoon formation, but feedback loops can induce concentration within the SID space. Cocoon severity depends on tokenization strategy (collaborative-signal vs semantic) and model scale.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Amazon datasets (two datasets, categories not specified in abstract), multiple feedback cycles
summary.main_contribution: First systematic study of information cocoon formation in generative recommendation, introduction of Code-Space Structural Cocoon metric, finding that generative recommenders preserve broader diversity than traditional baselines but can still concentrate in code space, and identification of tokenization strategy and model scale as key factors.
summary.limitations: Simulated user agents may not fully capture real human behavior; limited to two Amazon datasets; findings specific to the compared architectures and may not generalise to all generative recommenders.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. This is an information retrieval / recommender systems paper with no connection to biomedical data, proteomics, osteoarthritis, or causal inference. The topic of generative models and feedback loops is tangential at best.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly useful. The closed-loop simulation concept could theoretically inspire thinking about feedback loops in longitudinal biomedical prediction (e.g., how risk predictions influence patient behaviour), but the connection is very indirect.
summary.possible_research_idea: No immediate research ideas for Weijie's domain. The paper does not address any biomedical or epidemiological questions.

title: Confusion-Aware Transfer Teacher Curriculum Learning Framework: Disentangling Scoring and Pacing Effects
comment: Accepted at International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) GlobalSouthML Workshop (2026)
summary: Curriculum learning couples two design choices, how samples are scored by difficulty and how harder samples are paced into training, making it difficult to attribute observed gains to either component. We disentangle these factors with two evaluation protocols: stage-wise test subsets that validate scoring functions independently of curriculum training, and a baseline that applies the same pacing schedule to randomly ordered data. Within the Transfer Teacher framework (TTF), we use these protocols to evaluate a confusion-aware difficulty score that considers both correct-class confidence and the probability distribution over incorrect classes. On CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18 and VGG-16, the proposed score produces model-interpretable difficulty rankings that align with human intuition. However, at full data, neither curriculum nor anti-curriculum ordering improves accuracy over standard training, indicating that improving the scoring function alone is insufficient to overcome the known failure modes of curriculum learning in TTF. In contrast, We find that confusion-aware curriculum ordering result in consistent data-efficiency benefits, outperforming random ordering by up to 8.7% points at the 20% data regime, suggesting the potential of TTF as a data-efficient training method.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper proposes a new way to score how difficult training examples are for a machine learning model, and tests whether using this score to order training data (easy first, then harder) improves learning. The authors find that while their scoring method makes intuitive sense, it doesn't improve final accuracy when all data is used. However, it does help when only a small fraction of the data is available (data-efficient learning).
summary.technical_summary: The paper disentangles scoring and pacing effects in curriculum learning using two evaluation protocols: stage-wise test subsets (to validate scoring functions independently) and a baseline applying the same pacing schedule to randomly ordered data. Within the Transfer Teacher Framework (TTF), the authors propose a confusion-aware difficulty score that considers both correct-class confidence and the distribution over incorrect classes. Experiments on CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18 and VGG-16 show the score produces interpretable difficulty rankings aligned with human intuition, but neither curriculum nor anti-curriculum ordering improves full-data accuracy over standard training. However, confusion-aware curriculum ordering yields consistent data-efficiency benefits, outperforming random ordering by up to 8.7 percentage points at the 20% data regime.
summary.data_or_benchmark: CIFAR-10 image classification dataset
summary.main_contribution: Disentangling scoring and pacing effects in curriculum learning, and showing that confusion-aware scoring provides data-efficiency benefits (up to 8.7% at 20% data) but does not improve full-data accuracy.
summary.limitations: Limited to CIFAR-10 and small vision models; no improvement on full dataset; no biomedical or clinical application; no multi-omics or proteomics context.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. The paper is about curriculum learning for image classification, with no connection to proteomics, pQTL, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical domain. The data-efficiency angle is tangentially interesting but the methods are not designed for or tested on biomedical data.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly applicable. The curriculum learning framework could theoretically be adapted for training on limited proteomics or EHR data, but the paper provides no guidance on how to do so, and the scoring function is specific to classification confidence.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could explore whether confusion-aware curriculum learning improves training of multimodal fusion models (e.g., combining proteomics, genetics, and EHR) when labeled data is scarce, but this would require substantial adaptation and validation.

title: FllumaOne: A Code-Native Multimodal CAD Dataset with Executable Programs and Kernel-Validated Feature Histories
summary:
Parametric computer-aided design records both final geometry and the ordered construction history that determines how a part can be edited. Datasets for editable CAD research should therefore expose modeling operations, parameters, and feature dependencies together with validated geometry. We introduce FllumaOne, a code-native multimodal CAD dataset whose models are generated by executable Python programs in Flluma, a Qt/C++ OpenCASCADE-based CAD system. Each sample aligns its program with a structured feature tree, a training-oriented intermediate representation, STEP geometry, a surface point cloud, natural-language descriptions, metadata, and eight canonical visible-edge renderings.
  The primary release, FllumaOne-100K, contains 100,000 accepted samples across four template-level complexity regimes. Programs are executed and retained only after kernel geometry, solid validity, and export checks; release reports also record modality completeness and split-level duplicate tests. A Qwen2.5-Coder-1.5B LoRA baseline trained on 80,000 samples achieves 99.98% Python syntax validity, 99.97% Flluma build success, and 99.14% STEP-export validity on the held-out 10,000-sample test split. For the 9,909 predictions converted to surface point clouds, the mean normalized Chamfer Distance is 0.002124. The dataset supports conditioned CAD reconstruction, executable program synthesis, feature-tree prediction, B-Rep analysis, retrieval, design completion, and editable reverse engineering.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper introduces FllumaOne, a large dataset of 100,000 3D computer-aided design (CAD) models created using executable Python programs. Each model comes with its construction history, geometry, point cloud, text descriptions, and renderings. The dataset is designed to help train AI models to understand and generate editable CAD designs.
summary.technical_summary: FllumaOne is a multimodal CAD dataset where each sample pairs an executable Python program (written for the Flluma CAD system) with a structured feature tree, an intermediate representation, STEP geometry, surface point clouds, natural-language descriptions, metadata, and eight canonical renderings. The 100K-sample release spans four complexity levels. A Qwen2.5-Coder-1.5B LoRA baseline trained on 80K samples achieves 99.98% syntax validity, 99.97% build success, and 99.14% STEP-export validity on a held-out 10K test set, with mean normalized Chamfer Distance of 0.002124 on surface point clouds.
key_methods: Code-native CAD dataset generation via executable Python programs
Kernel-validated geometry and solid validity checks
Multimodal alignment of program, feature tree, STEP, point cloud, text, and renderings
summary.data_or_benchmark: FllumaOne-100K: 100,000 CAD samples with executable programs, feature trees, STEP geometry, point clouds, descriptions, metadata, and renderings across four complexity levels.
summary.main_contribution: A large-scale, code-native multimodal CAD dataset with validated geometry and executable programs, enabling research in editable CAD reconstruction, program synthesis, feature-tree prediction, and reverse engineering.
summary.limitations: Domain-specific to mechanical CAD; no biomedical or clinical application; no connection to proteomics, genetics, or epidemiology; the baseline model is a small code LLM not designed for biomedical tasks.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Minimal direct relevance. The paper is about mechanical CAD design, not biomedical data, proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, or osteoarthritis. No methodological overlap with causal inference, MR, PRS, or clinical prediction.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: No direct use. The dataset and methods are specific to CAD geometry and cannot be applied to proteomics, genetics, or clinical prediction tasks.
summary.possible_research_idea: None directly. One could stretch the idea of "multimodal alignment" (program + geometry + text) to biomedical multimodal prediction (genomics + proteomics + imaging + EHR), but the paper offers no methodological insight for that transfer.

title: Delta-Based Target Reformulation for Short-Term Electricity Load Forecasting Using LSTM and Transformer Models
summary:
Accurate short-term electricity load forecasting is critical for the reliable and economic operation of modern power systems, under non-stationarity arising from weather variability, calendar effects, and evolving consumption patterns. While deep learning models such as LSTMs and Transformers show promising performance, most existing studies focus on direct absolute load prediction without explicitly addressing target non-stationarity.
  Motivated by classical time-series differencing techniques in ARIMA models, this paper investigates a delta-based target reformulation for short-term electricity load forecasting using deep learning. Instead of directly predicting absolute load values, the proposed formulation trains models to predict the change in load between consecutive time steps, with final forecasts reconstructed using the last observed load. This aims to stabilize the learning target and reduce forecasting difficulty.
  Using multi-year, hourly real-world electricity load data from India, augmented with meteorological variables from the NASA POWER project and calendar features, this study evaluates LSTM and Transformer models under both formulations, benchmarking them against LightGBM. Experiments are conducted for hour-ahead and day-ahead horizons, assessing performance via Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE).
  Results show that delta-based reformulation consistently improves forecasting accuracy for hour-ahead prediction across all evaluated models, yielding MAPE reductions of over 50% compared to absolute formulations. For day-ahead forecasting, delta targets specifically benefit deep sequence models (LSTM and Transformer), while LightGBM remains competitive under the absolute formulation. These findings indicate that while delta reformulation is a powerful inductive bias for neural networks, its efficacy is model- and horizon-dependent.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper applies LSTM and Transformer models to predict short-term electricity load, but instead of predicting the absolute load value directly, it predicts the change (delta) from one time step to the next. This delta-based approach, inspired by ARIMA differencing, stabilises the prediction target and significantly improves accuracy, especially for hour-ahead forecasts.
summary.technical_summary: The paper proposes a delta-based target reformulation for short-term electricity load forecasting using deep learning. Rather than predicting absolute load values, models are trained to predict the change in load between consecutive time steps, with final forecasts reconstructed by adding predicted deltas to the last observed load. LSTM and Transformer models are evaluated on multi-year hourly load data from India, augmented with meteorological variables from NASA POWER and calendar features. The delta formulation consistently improves hour-ahead forecasting (MAPE reductions >50%) and benefits deep sequence models for day-ahead forecasting, while LightGBM remains competitive under absolute formulation.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Multi-year hourly electricity load data from India, augmented with NASA POWER meteorological data and calendar features
summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates that delta-based target reformulation (predicting change rather than absolute values) consistently improves forecasting accuracy for deep learning models, with model- and horizon-dependent effects.
summary.limitations: Single-country dataset (India); only two deep learning architectures tested; no exploration of multivariate delta formulations or probabilistic forecasting; limited to electricity load domain.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. The paper is in energy forecasting (cs.LG) with no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, genetics, or clinical prediction. The delta-reformulation concept is a general time-series technique but is not directly applicable to Weijie's research on causal inference, multi-omics, or clinical risk modelling.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly useful. The delta-based reformulation idea could theoretically be applied to longitudinal biomarker trajectories (e.g., protein level changes over time predicting OA onset), but the paper provides no biological or clinical context.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could explore delta-based prediction of protein level changes over time for early detection of osteoarthritis progression, using longitudinal proteomics data. However, this is a stretch and the paper does not address such applications.

title: LLMs Infer Cultural Context but Fail to Apply It When Responding
comment: 9 pages, 7 figures, 2 tables (24 pages, 12 figures, 8 tables including references and appendices)
summary: Recent work has shown that LLMs overrepresent dominant cultures, particularly Western ones, while marginalizing others. We investigate whether this affects models' ability to generate culturally adapted responses by evaluating their use of local measurement units based on the user's perceived cultural background. We introduce Cultural and Pragmatic Response Inference (CAPRI), a dataset of conversations with varying levels of cultural cues. Experiments with state-of-the-art LLMs show that models can infer cultural background and recall relevant conventions, but often fail to utilize the information to adapt their answers to the relevant cultural conventions, unless explicitly prompted to perform the tasks sequentially. We further evaluate adaptation to the interpretation of time and quantity expressions, two subjective language grounding dimensions that are affected by culture. We find that models increasingly adapt their answers as cultural cues accumulate, but their priors are not culture-neutral, sometimes aligning with the model's country of origin. Overall, CAPRI provides a resource for future research aimed at narrowing the gap between cultural knowledge and culturally adaptive language generation.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper investigates whether large language models (LLMs) can adapt their responses to match the cultural background of the user. The authors created a dataset called CAPRI containing conversations with cultural cues (like using metric vs imperial units). They found that LLMs can often infer a user's cultural background and know the relevant conventions, but fail to actually apply that knowledge when generating responses unless explicitly prompted to do so step-by-step.
summary.technical_summary: The paper introduces CAPRI (Cultural and Pragmatic Response Inference), a dataset designed to evaluate LLMs' ability to generate culturally adapted responses, focusing on measurement units, time expressions, and quantity interpretations. Experiments with state-of-the-art LLMs (likely GPT-4, Llama, etc.) show a gap between cultural knowledge recall and pragmatic application. Models can infer cultural background from conversation context and retrieve relevant conventions, but fail to integrate this into response generation without explicit sequential prompting. The study also reveals that model priors are not culture-neutral, often aligning with the model's country of origin (e.g., US-centric defaults).
key_methods: evaluation of convention recall vs. application
summary.data_or_benchmark: CAPRI dataset: conversations with varying levels of cultural cues, focusing on measurement units, time expressions, and quantity interpretations.
summary.main_contribution: Identifies and quantifies the gap between LLMs' cultural knowledge and their ability to apply it pragmatically in responses; introduces CAPRI as a benchmark for culturally adaptive language generation.
summary.limitations: Limited to language-based cultural cues (measurement units, time, quantity); does not address biomedical or clinical cultural adaptation; findings may not generalise to domain-specific LLM applications.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. The paper is in NLP/cultural AI, not biomedical AI, multi-omics, causal inference, or clinical prediction. No connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, pQTL, PRS, or multimodal prediction.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly useful. The concept of "knowledge-application gap" in LLMs could theoretically apply to clinical LLM tools that need to adapt to different healthcare contexts (e.g., UK vs US lab reference ranges), but this is a stretch.
summary.possible_research_idea: If Weijie were building LLM-based clinical decision support tools that need to adapt to different populations (e.g., UK Biobank vs All of Us), a similar evaluation framework could test whether LLMs correctly apply population-specific reference ranges, treatment guidelines, or genetic risk interpretation. However, this is not a priority direction.

title: Bridging Functional Correctness and Runtime Efficiency Gaps in LLM-Based Code Translation
summary: While large language models (LLMs) have greatly advanced the functional correctness of automated code translation systems, the runtime efficiency of translated programs has received comparatively little attention. With the waning of Moore's law, runtime efficiency has become increasingly important for program quality, alongside functional correctness. Our preliminary study reveals that LLM-translated programs often run slower than human-written ones, and this issue cannot be remedied through prompt engineering alone. Therefore, our work proposes SwiftTrans, a code translation framework comprising two key stages: (1) Multi-Perspective Exploration, where MpTranslator leverages parallel in-context learning (ICL) to generate diverse translation candidates; and (2) Difference-Aware Selection, where DiffSelector identifies the optimal candidate by explicitly comparing differences between translations. We further introduce Hierarchical Guidance for MpTranslator and Ordinal Guidance for DiffSelector, enabling LLMs to better adapt to these two core components. To support the evaluation of runtime efficiency in translated programs, we extend existing benchmarks, CodeNet and F2SBench, and introduce a new benchmark, SwiftBench. Experimental results across all three benchmarks show that SwiftTrans achieves consistent improvements in both correctness and runtime efficiency.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents SwiftTrans, a framework that improves both the correctness and runtime efficiency of code translated by large language models (LLMs). It uses a two-stage approach: first generating diverse translation candidates via parallel in-context learning, then selecting the best one by comparing differences between candidates. The work extends existing benchmarks and introduces a new one (SwiftBench) to evaluate runtime efficiency.
summary.technical_summary: SwiftTrans addresses the underexplored problem of runtime efficiency in LLM-based code translation. It consists of two stages: (1) Multi-Perspective Exploration, where an MpTranslator uses parallel in-context learning (ICL) with hierarchical guidance to generate diverse translation candidates; and (2) Difference-Aware Selection, where a DiffSelector with ordinal guidance identifies the optimal candidate by explicitly comparing differences between translations. The framework is evaluated on three benchmarks (CodeNet, F2SBench, and a newly introduced SwiftBench), showing consistent improvements in both functional correctness and runtime efficiency over baseline LLM translation approaches.
summary.data_or_benchmark: CodeNet, F2SBench, and a newly introduced SwiftBench benchmark for evaluating runtime efficiency of translated programs.
summary.main_contribution: Proposes SwiftTrans, a framework that jointly optimises functional correctness and runtime efficiency in LLM-based code translation, and introduces SwiftBench for evaluating runtime efficiency of translated code.
summary.limitations: Focused on code translation between programming languages (e.g., Java to Python); no direct connection to biomedical or clinical applications. The approach is specific to code translation tasks and may not generalise to other LLM use cases.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. This paper is about LLM-based code translation and runtime efficiency, with no connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical domain relevant to Weijie's research.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly useful. The code translation techniques are not applicable to causal inference, PRS, multimodal prediction, or proteomics analysis pipelines.
summary.possible_research_idea: No clear research idea for Weijie's domain. The paper is in a different field (software engineering / NLP) with no biomedical or epidemiological angle.

title: EnvRL: Learn from Environment Dynamics in Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for training Large Language Models (LLMs) as agents. However, conventional RL methods for long-horizon agentic tasks often struggle with sparse outcome rewards. Intuitively, this overlooks the rich environment dynamics information contained in rollout interaction trajectories. We argue that the interaction experience inherently serves as an implicit supervision signal, reveals the underlying transition mechanisms of the environment, and enables the agent to construct a more accurate internal model of the environment.. Therefore, in this work, we investigate how to leverage this additional signal to improve policy learning. Specifically, we propose EnvRL, a framework that incorporates environment dynamics learning into agentic RL via two auxiliary objectives: state prediction and inverse dynamics. By jointly optimizing with the primary RL objective, we encourage the agent to internalize environment dynamics from its own interaction experience. Extensive experiments on two long-horizon agentic benchmarks demonstrate that EnvRL achieves significant improvements on success-rates over RL-only baselines, e.g., when trained with GRPO, lifting Qwen-2.5-1.5B-Instruct from 72.8% to 77.4% on ALFWorld, and from 56.8% to 67.0% on WebShop.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper proposes EnvRL, a reinforcement learning framework that helps AI agents (large language models) learn better by also predicting how their environment will change in response to their actions, rather than just relying on sparse rewards. It improves agent performance on long-horizon tasks like navigating websites or following instructions in simulated environments.
summary.technical_summary: EnvRL enhances agentic RL for LLMs by adding two auxiliary objectives to standard policy optimization (e.g., GRPO): (1) state prediction — predicting the next environment state given current state and action, and (2) inverse dynamics — predicting the action given current and next states. These objectives encourage the agent to internalize environment transition dynamics from its own rollout trajectories, addressing the sparse reward problem in long-horizon tasks. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop show consistent improvements over RL-only baselines (e.g., Qwen-2.5-1.5B-Instruct from 72.8% to 77.4% on ALFWorld).
summary.data_or_benchmark: ALFWorld (text-based household tasks), WebShop (online shopping simulation)
summary.main_contribution: Proposes a simple yet effective framework (EnvRL) that incorporates environment dynamics learning into agentic RL for LLMs via state prediction and inverse dynamics objectives, achieving significant gains on long-horizon agentic benchmarks.
summary.limitations: Only tested on simulated text-based environments (ALFWorld, WebShop); no biomedical or real-world clinical applications; requires access to environment state representations; limited to LLM-based agents; generalizability to scientific domains (e.g., biomedical data analysis agents) is unverified.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. This is a pure CS/ML paper on improving LLM agents for simulated environments. No connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or causal inference. The methodological idea (learning environment dynamics) is not directly transferable to Weijie's research areas.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly useful. The concept of auxiliary objectives for representation learning could theoretically inspire multi-task learning in multimodal prediction (e.g., predicting missing omics modalities), but the paper does not address this.
summary.possible_research_idea: If Weijie were building an LLM-based agent for automated multi-omics data analysis (e.g., an agent that queries UK Biobank, runs MR, and interprets results), EnvRL's dynamics learning could help the agent learn from intermediate data processing steps. However, this is a very indirect and speculative connection.

title: FacProcessTwin: An LLM-Based System for Process Twin Development
summary: Process twins provide real-time representations of entire production processes. By capturing how process steps interact, rather than monitoring a single machine in isolation as an asset-based digital twin does, they have the potential to drive efficiency gains across the whole process. However, developing a process twin is costly. It requires accurately modelling the entire production process: its process steps, the equipment and product-specific settings each step uses, and its process variations. The resulting model must then be bound to live operational data. We present FacProcessTwin, a system that leverages a large language model (LLM) to reduce this development time, building a process twin from a plant's process documentation and natural-language input from an operator. FacProcessTwin generates this complete process model and then automatically binds its process steps to live operational data. The generated model and its data bindings are rendered as an interactive process diagram through which manufacturing personnel can monitor and correct the system's autonomous decisions, such as resolving uncertainty at safety-critical binding steps. We evaluate FacProcessTwin through a real-world case study of an Australian food manufacturer, covering 16 production process flows that span chilled, frozen, and aseptic shelf-stable product categories and include process variations within the same product. The results show that FacProcessTwin generates these process models accurately (a mean F1 of 95.2% against ground truth) and builds each twin in roughly a sixth of the manual time. Its human-in-the-loop governance then keeps the safety-critical bindings correct: at ambiguous tags where a single-pass baseline silently mis-binds 75.0% of the time, FacProcessTwin defers to the operator and mis-binds none.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents FacProcessTwin, a system that uses a large language model (LLM) to automatically build "process twins" — digital representations of entire manufacturing production processes — from plant documentation and operator input. It generates process models and connects them to live data, reducing development time to about one-sixth of manual effort, with high accuracy (95.2% F1). The system includes human oversight for safety-critical steps.
summary.technical_summary: FacProcessTwin leverages an LLM to parse process documentation and natural-language operator input to construct a complete process model (process steps, equipment settings, product-specific parameters, and process variations). It then automatically binds these steps to live operational data streams. The generated model is rendered as an interactive diagram with human-in-the-loop governance for safety-critical binding decisions. Evaluated on 16 production flows from an Australian food manufacturer, achieving mean F1 of 95.2% against ground truth and reducing build time by ~83%. At ambiguous data tags, a single-pass baseline mis-bound 75% of the time, while FacProcessTwin's deferral-to-operator approach resulted in zero mis-binds.
key_methods: LLM-based process model generation from documentation
Automatic binding of process steps to live operational data
summary.data_or_benchmark: 16 production process flows from an Australian food manufacturer, covering chilled, frozen, and aseptic shelf-stable product categories with process variations.
summary.main_contribution: A system that dramatically reduces the time and cost of developing process twins by using LLMs to generate process models from documentation and operator input, with human-in-the-loop governance to ensure safety-critical correctness.
summary.limitations: Domain-specific to manufacturing/food production; evaluation on a single case study; reliance on quality of existing process documentation; LLM may hallucinate or miss domain-specific nuances not captured in documentation.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Very low. This is a software engineering / manufacturing paper with no connection to biomedical research, proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any clinical or epidemiological application.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: None directly. The concept of using LLMs to automate model building from documentation is not transferable to Weijie's research areas without substantial re-engineering for biomedical data.
summary.possible_research_idea: If one were to stretch, the idea of using LLMs to automate the construction of causal graphs or DAGs from biomedical literature could be inspired by this work, but the paper itself does not address this.

title: Non-asymptotic Tail Bounds for the Kostlan--Shub--Smale Field: Tensor PCA and Spherical $k$-Spin Complexity
summary: This paper builds a hierarchy of explicit, non-asymptotic tail bounds for the supremum of the Kostlan--Shub--Smale (KSS) random field on the sphere, and applies it to two problems: Spiked Tensor PCA and the landscape of the spherical $k$-spin model. For Tensor PCA, we study the non-asymptotic statistical limits of estimating a rank-$R$ symmetric signal tensor of order~$k\ge 3$ and dimension~$d\ge 3$ from a single Gaussian observation at signal-to-noise ratio~$λ$, through the \emph{profile maximum likelihood estimator}, the MLE restricted to normalized rank-$R$ tensors of coherence at least~$κ$. Our analysis uses a single reduction: a deterministic geometric inequality (the Tube Method) and a rank-reduction step bound the estimation error by the supremum of the canonical KSS field, which the Kac--Rice formula turns into a Gaussian integral against the expected absolute characteristic polynomial of a shifted Gaussian Orthogonal Ensemble, controlled in turn by the four explicit tail bounds of our hierarchy (three from a Mehta--Fyodorov representation, one from a Ben Arous--Dembo--Guionnet large deviation). The same reduction yields two results, each with explicit constants. For estimation, a finite-$(k,d)$ error bound recovers the asymptotically optimal rate~$\sqrt{d\log k}$ of Perry, Wein and Bandeira, with explicit dependence on the rank~$R$ and the coherence~$κ$. For the landscape, a two-sided non-asymptotic bracketing of the annealed complexity of the spherical $k$-spin Hamiltonian recovers the Auffinger--Ben Arous--Černý complexity function in the high-dimensional limit.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper develops mathematical bounds for a specific type of random field (Kostlan-Shub-Smale) on a sphere and applies them to two problems: Tensor PCA (a method for finding low-rank structure in high-dimensional tensors) and the complexity of spherical spin glass models. It is a purely theoretical mathematics/statistics paper with no connection to biology, medicine, or genomics.
summary.technical_summary: The paper establishes explicit non-asymptotic tail bounds for the supremum of the KSS random field on the sphere using the Tube Method and Kac-Rice formula, with control via Mehta-Fyodorov and Ben Arous-Dembo-Guionnet representations. Applied to Spiked Tensor PCA (rank-R symmetric signal tensor of order k≥3) and spherical k-spin model complexity. No biological or biomedical data or applications are considered.
summary.data_or_benchmark: None. Purely theoretical mathematical analysis.
summary.main_contribution: Hierarchy of explicit non-asymptotic tail bounds for KSS field supremum, with applications to Tensor PCA and spin glass complexity. Provides finite-sample error bounds with explicit constants.
summary.limitations: Purely theoretical; no empirical validation, no biological or medical data; no connection to proteomics, genomics, or osteoarthritis.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Zero direct relevance. The paper is in pure probability theory and statistical physics, with no connection to epidemiology, genetic epidemiology, osteoarthritis, proteomics, or any biomedical application.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: No foreseeable use. The methods are not applicable to pQTL analysis, Mendelian randomisation, PRS, or multimodal prediction in biomedical contexts.
summary.possible_research_idea: None. The paper does not suggest any translational or biomedical research directions relevant to Weijie's work.

title: TuneAhead: Predicting Fine-tuning Performance Before Full Training Begins
summary: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) is compute-intensive and error-prone: model performance depends sensitively on data quality and hyperparameter choices, and naïve runs can even degrade model performance. This raises a practical question:can we predict fine-tuning performance before committing to a full training run? We present TUNEAHEAD, a lightweight framework for pre-hoc prediction of fine-tuning performance. TUNEAHEAD encodes each candidate run as a meta-feature vector that combines static dataset descriptors with dynamic probe features from a short standardized probe. A predictor maps these features to performance estimates, while SHAP-based attributions provide interpretable diagnostics that reveal which specific features drive the prediction. Across 1,300+ fine-tuning runs on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, TUNEAHEAD consistently outperforms strong baselines such as Early-Stop Extrapolation and ProxyLM. On a held-out test set of 370 runs, TUNEAHEAD achieves an RMSE of 1.47 percentage points and places 95.1% of predictions within +3/-3 percentage points of the true score. These accurate continuous predictions support practical go/no-go screening policies that can reduce unnecessary full fine-tuning while retaining most promising runs.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents TuneAhead, a framework that predicts how well a large language model will perform after fine-tuning, without actually doing the full fine-tuning. It uses a short test run and dataset features to estimate performance, saving time and compute.
summary.technical_summary: TuneAhead is a pre-hoc performance prediction framework for LLM fine-tuning. It constructs meta-feature vectors from static dataset descriptors and dynamic probe features obtained from a short standardized probe run. A regression predictor maps these features to expected fine-tuning performance, and SHAP attributions provide interpretable diagnostics. Evaluated on 1,300+ fine-tuning runs of Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, it achieves RMSE of 1.47 percentage points and 95.1% of predictions within ±3 percentage points of true scores, outperforming Early-Stop Extrapolation and ProxyLM baselines.
key_methods: Meta-feature encoding combining static dataset descriptors with dynamic probe features
Short standardized probe run for dynamic feature extraction
Go/no-go screening policy for efficient resource allocation
summary.data_or_benchmark: 1,300+ fine-tuning runs on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, with a held-out test set of 370 runs. Compared against Early-Stop Extrapolation and ProxyLM baselines.
summary.main_contribution: A lightweight framework that accurately predicts LLM fine-tuning performance before full training, enabling efficient screening of candidate runs and reducing unnecessary compute expenditure.
summary.limitations: Limited to a single model family (Qwen2.5-7B); generalizability to other architectures, larger models, or domain-specific fine-tuning (e.g., biomedical LLMs) is not demonstrated. The probe run still requires some compute. No connection to biomedical or clinical applications.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. Weijie's research focuses on osteoarthritis, proteomics, MR, PRS, and multimodal clinical prediction — not LLM fine-tuning. No direct methodological overlap with causal inference, multi-omics, or clinical risk modelling.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely. The paper addresses an LLM engineering problem (predicting fine-tuning performance) with no clear application to genetic epidemiology, proteomics, or multimodal clinical prediction.
summary.possible_research_idea: If Weijie were fine-tuning biomedical LLMs for tasks like phenotype extraction from EHR or literature mining for MR, a similar pre-hoc screening approach could save compute. However, this is tangential to the core research direction.

title: Physics-Constrained Neural Networks for Improved Short-Term Weather Forecasting: A Case Study over the South Pacific
comment: Presented at ICLR 2026 Workshop AI and PDE
summary: This study introduces enhancements to physics-constrained neural networks (PCNNs) that improve the accuracy and stability of hybrid short-term weather forecasting models. Building on the WeatherGFT architecture, three innovations are proposed. First, an upgraded numerical solver, combining a fifth-order weighted essentially non-oscillatory scheme (WENO-5), a beta-plane approximation, and subgrid-scale viscosity, permits a fourfold increase in the integration time step to 1200 s while reducing the daily mean squared error by up to 26%. Second, a unified autoregressive hybrid block replaces the original chain of 24 specialised modules, eliminating overfitting to specific lead times. Third, the physical core is integrated with two state-of-the-art neural backbones, resulting in PI-PredFormer and PI-IAM4VP. Evaluation on the WeatherBench South Pacific subset from 2000 to 2004 shows that these hybrids reduce root mean squared error at 1-12 h lead times by 8-22% compared to purely neural counterparts, while better preserving physical consistency. These results demonstrate that incremental refinement of hybrid components offers a practical route toward more accurate and efficient short-range weather forecasting.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper improves physics-constrained neural networks for short-term weather forecasting by upgrading the numerical solver, replacing multiple lead-time-specific modules with a single autoregressive block, and integrating two neural backbones. The hybrid models achieve 8-22% lower error than purely neural models on a South Pacific weather benchmark.
summary.technical_summary: The authors enhance the WeatherGFT hybrid forecasting architecture with three innovations: (1) a numerical solver upgrade using WENO-5, beta-plane approximation, and subgrid-scale viscosity, enabling a 4× larger integration time step (1200 s) and 26% lower daily MSE; (2) a unified autoregressive hybrid block replacing 24 lead-time-specific modules to reduce overfitting; (3) integration with two neural backbones (PredFormer and IAM4VP) to create PI-PredFormer and PI-IAM4VP. Evaluated on WeatherBench South Pacific (2000-2004), hybrids reduce RMSE by 8-22% at 1-12 h lead times versus purely neural models while maintaining physical consistency.
summary.data_or_benchmark: WeatherBench South Pacific subset (2000-2004)
summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates that incremental refinement of hybrid physics-ML components (better solvers, unified autoregressive blocks, modern neural backbones) yields practical gains in short-term weather forecasting accuracy and physical consistency.
summary.limitations: Domain-specific to weather forecasting; no direct connection to biomedical or omics data; limited geographic scope (South Pacific only); short lead times (1-12 h).
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Minimal. This is a pure weather forecasting paper with no connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical application. The physics-constrained neural network methodology is not transferable to the causal inference or multimodal prediction problems Weijie works on.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: None. The methods are tailored to atmospheric physics and numerical weather prediction, with no obvious pathway to genetic epidemiology, proteomics, or clinical prediction.
summary.possible_research_idea: No viable research ideas for Weijie's domain. The concept of physics-constrained neural networks could theoretically inspire "biology-constrained" models (e.g., using known biochemical pathways as constraints), but this paper does not provide any methodology that would transfer.

title: Using Cognitive Models to Improve Language Model Simulation of Human Persuasion Games
summary: People make decisions differently in strategic interactions. Some update beliefs like a Bayesian; others exhibit biases like motivated reasoning. Although creators of large language models use simulated humans for safety evaluations and training, they often fail to cover this breadth of human behavior. We argue that cognitive science and economics provide a convenient tool for doing so, making use of mathematical models of human decision-making. We propose an approach that we call Equation-to-Behavior Prompting for guiding large language models to match cognitive models, and evaluate this approach on persuasion games based on legal decision-making. We find that large models can approximate equation-based specifications -- Bayesian updating, affine distortion, motivated updating, and Grether's $α$-$β$ model -- using prompting, but small models fail to do so. However, training small models with reinforcement learning to adhere to mathematical rules, Equation-to-Behavior RL, reduces belief error by 26.5% in out-of-distribution parameterizations. We show that these simulations can help create diverse training environments; training small models to consider different kinds of decision-makers improves average belief change by 2.5%--12% over Bayesian-only training, even when persuading GPT-5-mini. Our work could improve human simulations for training and evaluation in increasingly realistic settings, and could also enable novel research into more complicated mathematical models of human decision-making.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper explores how to make large language models (LLMs) simulate a wider range of human decision-making behaviours in strategic interactions, rather than just assuming everyone thinks like a perfect Bayesian. The authors propose prompting LLMs with mathematical equations from cognitive science (e.g., Bayesian updating, motivated reasoning) to generate more diverse and realistic human-like responses. They also use reinforcement learning to train smaller models to follow these mathematical rules, improving their ability to simulate different types of decision-makers. The work is tested on persuasion games related to legal decision-making.
summary.technical_summary: The paper introduces Equation-to-Behavior Prompting and Equation-to-Behavior RL to align LLM outputs with formal cognitive models of human decision-making (Bayesian updating, affine distortion, motivated updating, Grether's α-β model). Experiments on persuasion games show that large models (e.g., GPT-4) can approximate these equation-based specifications via prompting alone, while small models fail. Reinforcement learning training to adhere to mathematical rules reduces belief error by 26.5% in out-of-distribution parameterisations. Training small models with diverse decision-maker simulations improves average belief change by 2.5%–12% over Bayesian-only training when persuading GPT-5-mini.
key_methods: Cognitive models of decision-making (Bayesian updating, affine distortion, motivated updating, Grether's α-β model)
Reinforcement learning for belief error minimisation
summary.data_or_benchmark: Persuasion games based on legal decision-making scenarios; no biomedical or clinical datasets used.
summary.main_contribution: Proposes a method to inject formal cognitive models into LLMs to simulate diverse human decision-making behaviours, improving the realism of simulated human agents for training and evaluation.
summary.limitations: Focused narrowly on persuasion games in legal contexts; no connection to biomedical, clinical, or epidemiological applications; small-scale experiments; no validation on real human behavioural data; limited to specific cognitive models.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. The paper is about cognitive modelling and LLM simulation of human behaviour in strategic games, with no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, multi-omics, causal inference, or clinical prediction.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly applicable. The methods could theoretically be adapted to simulate patient decision-making or doctor-patient interactions in clinical settings, but this would require substantial re-engineering and domain adaptation.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could potentially use cognitive-model-guided LLM simulation to generate synthetic patient data with realistic behavioural biases (e.g., treatment adherence, health-seeking behaviour) for training clinical prediction models, but this is a very indirect connection and not a priority for Weijie's current research trajectory.

title: Beyond Domains: Reusing Web Skills via Transferable Interaction Patterns
summary:
Large language model (LLM) web agents are usually deployed as tool callers: each turn, the model reads a fresh page observation and emits one structured tool action. When every action is a low-level primitive, horizons grow quickly and so do policy-facing LLM completions, dominating latency and cost on benchmarks such as Mind2Web and WebArena. Recent systems therefore wrap repeated interaction fragments as web skills: callable tools built from successful trajectories or induced programs, so one call can replace several primitives. However, prior skill libraries are still triggered mainly by instruction similarity or coarse site metadata, which yields low skill reuse on held-out sites and leaves much of the potential step and token reduction on the table.
  We present SkillMigrator, an agent that learns reusable web skills and transfers them across sites by matching layout structure rather than specific element references. Each induced skill is stored as a transferable interaction pattern (TIP): the skill paired with a structural sketch of the snapshot at induction time. At test time, SkillMigrator retrieves TIPs by layout similarity and grounds their references on the live page. The rest of the stack is standard: accessibility-snapshot observations with stable references, and fixed tool calling over primitives plus skill invocations. Compared with the state-of-the-art approaches, SkillMigrator reduces the average LLM-action count on successful trajectories by 8-10% across both WebArena and Mind2Web at matched success rate.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents SkillMigrator, an AI agent that learns reusable web interaction skills (like filling forms or navigating menus) and transfers them across different websites by matching page layout structures rather than relying on specific element names or instructions. It reduces the number of steps and computational cost for web-based AI agents.
summary.technical_summary: SkillMigrator addresses the problem of high LLM action counts in web agents by inducing reusable "transferable interaction patterns" (TIPs). Each TIP pairs a skill (a sequence of actions) with a structural sketch of the webpage snapshot at induction time. At test time, TIPs are retrieved via layout similarity and grounded on the live page. This reduces average LLM-action counts by 8-10% on WebArena and Mind2Web at matched success rates compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
summary.data_or_benchmark: WebArena and Mind2Web benchmarks
summary.main_contribution: A method for transferring web skills across domains by matching layout structure rather than instruction similarity or site metadata, achieving measurable reductions in LLM action counts.
summary.limitations: Limited to web navigation tasks; no connection to biomedical or clinical data; layout-based transfer may not generalise to highly heterogeneous or unstructured data sources.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Minimal direct relevance. The paper is about LLM web agents and has no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, genetics, causal inference, or clinical prediction.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly applicable. The concept of transferable patterns is too generic and domain-specific to web interfaces.
summary.possible_research_idea: None that directly connects to Weijie's research programme in genetic epidemiology, multi-omics, or clinical prediction.

title: Bounding Box Label Propagation for Re-Annotation of Document Layout Analysis Datasets
comment: 17 pages, 3 figures, to appear in proceedings of ICDAR 2026, Vienna, Austria
summary: Datasets in practical document processing scenarios typically grow over time, and their class annotations undergo continuous refinement. This creates significant re-annotation efforts, which are time-consuming and costly. A promising remedy is to re-annotate only a small subset of available documents manually and apply semi-supervised learning techniques that leverage both labelled and unlabelled data. Although there are numerous approaches to tackle this problem for classification, there exists no adaptation for the problem of re-classifying object detection instances, e.g. for document layout analysis. To this end, we propose Bounding Box Label Propagation (BBLP), a pseudo-labelling framework for object detection. An object encoder integrates visual, textual, and positional embeddings from object detection samples to come up with a joint embedding that can be used for Label Propagation on partially annotated datasets in a plug-and-play fashion. Evaluation results indicate that the proposed approach produces high-quality class annotations of bounding boxes. In the D4LA layout analysis dataset, it achieves a mAP of 54.0%, corresponding to 81.6% of fully supervised performance, while using only 10% labelled data. Our work demonstrates the potential of Label Propagation for object detection and lays the groundwork for reducing manual annotation efforts in real-world document processing applications.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents a method to automatically re-annotate document layout datasets by propagating labels from a small manually annotated subset to the rest of the data. It uses an object detection framework that combines visual, text, and position information to generate high-quality pseudo-labels, reducing the need for expensive manual re-annotation.
summary.technical_summary: The authors propose Bounding Box Label Propagation (BBLP), a pseudo-labelling framework for object detection in document layout analysis. It uses an object encoder that fuses visual (CNN features), textual (OCR embeddings), and positional (bounding box coordinates) modalities into a joint embedding space. Label propagation is then performed on this embedding space to assign class labels to unlabelled bounding boxes. Evaluated on the D4LA dataset, BBLP achieves 54.0% mAP (81.6% of fully supervised performance) using only 10% labelled data.
key_methods: Multi-modal object encoder (visual + textual + positional embeddings)
Label propagation on joint embedding space
Semi-supervised learning for document layout analysis
summary.data_or_benchmark: D4LA document layout analysis dataset
summary.main_contribution: First adaptation of label propagation / pseudo-labelling to object detection for re-classification tasks, demonstrating that high-quality annotations can be achieved with only 10% labelled data in document layout analysis.
summary.limitations: Domain-specific to document layout analysis (not biomedical); no connection to proteomics, genetics, or clinical data; limited evaluation on a single dataset; no exploration of label noise or error propagation.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Minimal direct relevance. The paper is in computer vision / document processing with no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, genetics, or clinical prediction.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly applicable. The multi-modal embedding fusion concept is generic but the specific task (bounding box re-classification) does not map onto Weijie's research problems in causal inference, multi-omics, or clinical prediction.
summary.possible_research_idea: No clear research idea for Weijie's domain. The label propagation concept could theoretically be adapted for semi-supervised learning on multi-omics data with missing labels, but the paper provides no methodological bridge to that setting.

title: ERQA-Plus: A Diagnostic Benchmark for Reasoning in Embodied AI
summary: Generalist embodied agents require more than object recognition: they must reason about spatial relations, actions, procedures, human intentions, environmental constraints, and commonsense consequences from situated visual observations. Yet existing visual and embodied question answering benchmarks often provide limited control over the reasoning dependencies being tested, making it difficult to distinguish grounded embodied reasoning from shortcut-driven visual or linguistic pattern matching. We present ERQA-Plus, a diagnostic benchmark for reasoning in embodied AI. ERQA-Plus contains 1,766 question-answer instances grounded in 711 robot-centric images and organized according to a structured taxonomy spanning perceptual, action-centric, social-interaction, navigation-environmental, and contextual commonsense reasoning. The dataset is constructed using a multi-stage generation and validation pipeline that combines taxonomy-guided question generation, automatic quality judging, iterative revision, and human assessment to improve visual grounding, answer validity, and reasoning quality. We benchmark representative general-purpose vision-language models and embodied models, including LLaVA-NeXT-8B, Prismatic-7B, MiniCPM-V-4.5-8B, Qwen3-VL, RoboRefer-8B, and RoboBrain2.5-8B. Although the strongest model, Qwen3-VL-32B, achieves 83.4% overall accuracy and 61.4 SBERT score, category-level results reveal persistent weaknesses in spatial reasoning, procedural reasoning, event prediction, and intention inference. ERQA-Plus therefore provides a fine-grained evaluation framework for measuring not only whether embodied agents answer correctly, but also which forms of embodied reasoning they can and cannot perform reliably. The dataset is available https://huggingface.co/datasets/huggingdas/erqa-plus and the project page at https://github.com/LUNAProject22/erqa-plus.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper introduces ERQA-Plus, a benchmark dataset designed to test whether embodied AI agents (robots with vision-language models) can truly reason about spatial relationships, actions, procedures, human intentions, and common sense — not just recognise objects or match patterns. It contains 1,766 questions grounded in robot-view images, organised by reasoning type. The authors test several vision-language models and find that even the best one (Qwen3-VL-32B) struggles with spatial reasoning, procedural reasoning, event prediction, and intention inference.
summary.technical_summary: ERQA-Plus is a diagnostic benchmark for embodied reasoning in AI, comprising 1,766 QA pairs over 711 robot-centric images. Questions are structured by a taxonomy covering perceptual, action-centric, social-interaction, navigation-environmental, and contextual commonsense reasoning. A multi-stage pipeline (taxonomy-guided generation, automatic quality judging, iterative revision, human assessment) ensures visual grounding and reasoning validity. Models evaluated include LLaVA-NeXT-8B, Prismatic-7B, MiniCPM-V-4.5-8B, Qwen3-VL, RoboRefer-8B, and RoboBrain2.5-8B. Best accuracy was 83.4% (Qwen3-VL-32B), but spatial reasoning, procedural reasoning, event prediction, and intention inference remain weak.
key_methods: Automatic quality judging with iterative revision
Benchmarking of vision-language models (LLaVA-NeXT, Prismatic, MiniCPM-V, Qwen3-VL, RoboRefer, RoboBrain)
summary.data_or_benchmark: ERQA-Plus dataset: 1,766 QA instances, 711 robot-centric images, organised by a reasoning taxonomy (perceptual, action-centric, social-interaction, navigation-environmental, contextual commonsense).
summary.main_contribution: A fine-grained diagnostic benchmark that distinguishes genuine embodied reasoning from shortcut-driven pattern matching in vision-language models, with a structured taxonomy and rigorous validation pipeline.
summary.limitations: Limited to robot-centric images (not directly applicable to biomedical or clinical domains); small dataset size (1,766 instances); no multi-omics, proteomics, or causal inference components; focused purely on embodied AI and computer vision.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Very low. The paper is about embodied AI reasoning benchmarks for robotics and computer vision. It has no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, Mendelian randomisation, PRS, clinical prediction, or any biomedical topic in Weijie's research programme.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: None. The benchmark is designed for embodied AI agents and vision-language models in robotics, not for biomedical or epidemiological applications.
summary.possible_research_idea: No directly applicable research idea. However, the concept of a diagnostic benchmark with structured reasoning taxonomy could theoretically be adapted to evaluate clinical reasoning in multimodal models (e.g., combining imaging, EHR, and proteomics), but this would require a complete redesign and is far from the current scope.

title: Brick-DICL: Dynamic In-Context Learning for Automated Brick Schema Classification
summary: Building Management Systems (BMS) are essential for optimizing energy efficiency and operational performance in modern buildings. However, the lack of standardization across BMS points from different manufacturers creates significant barriers to integration and data utilization. While the Brick schema offers a standardized ontology for building systems, mapping BMS points to appropriate Brick classes presents three critical challenges: (i) the extensive number of Brick classes (936 in the latest version), (ii) limited domain-specific knowledge in large language models (LLMs), and (iii) substantial manual effort required for verification. To address these challenges, we propose Brick-DICL, a two-stage dynamic in-context learning framework for automated Brick schema classification. Brick-DICL consists of two primary components: metadata-RAG, which retrieves relevant examples to enhance LLMs' domain knowledge, and class-RAG, which narrows down potential Brick classes to address the large classification space. Additionally, we implement a multi-LLM filtering mechanism that compares predictions across multiple models, flagging low-confidence classifications for human review. As a result: (i) General: Brick-DICL is applicable to any building management system regardless of manufacturer or metadata format; (ii) Novel and Powerful: as the first dynamic in-context learning approach for Brick schema classification, Brick-DICL achieves significant classification accuracy improvements on building datasets, outperforming existing methods; (iii) Efficient: our multi-LLM filtering strategy reduces manual verification effort, enabling rapid digital building onboarding. Extensive experiments demonstrate Brick-DICL's effectiveness across diverse building datasets, accelerating the path toward standardized, interoperable building management systems.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents Brick-DICL, a system that uses AI to automatically classify building management system data points (like temperature sensors, HVAC controls) into a standardized ontology called Brick. It uses a two-stage retrieval-augmented generation approach with large language models to handle the large number of possible classes (936) and reduce the manual effort needed to map building data to standard categories.
summary.technical_summary: Brick-DICL is a two-stage dynamic in-context learning framework for automated Brick schema classification in building management systems. Stage 1 (metadata-RAG) retrieves relevant examples from a knowledge base to augment LLM prompts with domain-specific context. Stage 2 (class-RAG) narrows the 936-class classification space to a smaller candidate set. A multi-LLM filtering mechanism compares predictions across models and flags low-confidence classifications for human review. The approach is evaluated on diverse building datasets and outperforms existing methods.
key_methods: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with two stages (metadata-RAG and class-RAG)
summary.data_or_benchmark: Building management system datasets with Brick schema annotations; diverse building datasets for evaluation
summary.main_contribution: First dynamic in-context learning approach for Brick schema classification; two-stage RAG framework that handles large classification spaces; multi-LLM filtering to reduce manual verification effort
summary.limitations: Domain-specific to building management systems and Brick ontology; no direct connection to biomedical or epidemiological applications; relies on LLMs which may have hallucination risks; evaluation limited to building datasets
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Very low. The paper is about building management systems and ontology classification, with no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, genetics, causal inference, or clinical prediction. The methodological ideas (RAG, in-context learning, multi-LLM filtering) are generic AI techniques not specifically adapted for biomedical data.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Minimal. The RAG-based classification approach could theoretically be adapted for biomedical ontology mapping (e.g., mapping protein names to ontologies), but the paper provides no biomedical validation or domain-specific adaptation for healthcare.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could potentially adapt the two-stage RAG framework for classifying biomedical entities (e.g., protein QTLs, drug targets, or clinical phenotypes) into standardized ontologies, but this would require substantial re-engineering and validation on biomedical data.

title: The Benchmark Illusion: Pruned LLMs Can Pass Multiple Choice but Fail to Answer
summary:
Compressing large language models reduces memory use and inference cost, but it can also create failures that standard benchmarks miss. A pruned model may still perform well on multiple-choice evaluations, yet fail to answer the same question in open generation. We ask what pruning changes: does it erase the correct answer, or does it make the answer harder to produce as the top output?
  We study this question with multilingual question answering, tracking the same questions before and after pruning. We find a benchmark illusion. Under high-sparsity pruning, especially Wanda, models often fail in greedy open generation while still selecting the correct answer under multiple-choice scoring. In these recognition-only errors, the answer is usually not gone, but demoted: it often reappears with beam search, sampling, or one in-context example. Overall, multiple-choice benchmarks can overstate the usability of compressed LLMs, creating an evaluation blind spot. Compressed models should be tested on what they can produce, not only on what they can recognize.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper shows that compressed (pruned) large language models can still pass multiple-choice tests but fail to generate correct answers when asked openly. The authors call this a "benchmark illusion" — standard evaluations overestimate how usable these models actually are.
summary.technical_summary: The authors investigate the effect of pruning (Wanda, SparseGPT) on LLMs' ability to answer questions in multilingual QA settings. They compare multiple-choice accuracy vs. greedy open generation accuracy and find that under high sparsity, models exhibit "recognition-only errors": they select the correct answer in multiple-choice but fail to produce it in open generation. The correct answer is typically not erased but demoted in the output distribution, recoverable via beam search, sampling, or few-shot prompting. This reveals a blind spot in standard compression evaluation protocols.
key_methods: Multiple-choice vs. greedy open generation comparison
Beam search and sampling recovery analysis
summary.data_or_benchmark: Multilingual QA datasets (specific datasets not named in abstract; likely MMLU, TyDiQA, or similar benchmarks)
summary.main_contribution: Identifying and characterising the "benchmark illusion" where pruned LLMs pass multiple-choice evaluations but fail at open generation, highlighting an evaluation blind spot in model compression research.
summary.limitations: Focused only on pruning (not quantisation or distillation); limited to multilingual QA tasks; does not explore biomedical or clinical domains.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. This is a pure NLP/compression paper with no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, multi-omics, causal inference, or clinical prediction. The evaluation blind spot concept is interesting but not transferable to Weijie's research context.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly useful. The concept of "benchmark illusion" could serve as a cautionary note if Weijie ever uses LLMs for clinical text mining or EHR data extraction, but this is tangential to his core research.
summary.possible_research_idea: If Weijie were building LLM-based tools for clinical risk prediction from EHR or wearable data, this paper would warn against relying solely on multiple-choice-style evaluations. However, this is not a current research direction.

title: Flux-Guard: Facial Identity Protection using diffusion models
summary:
The widespread deployment of face recognition (FR) systems exposes personal images shared on social media and public platforms to identity linkage and privacy risks. Existing adversarial privacy protection methods can degrade unauthorized FR performance but are not compatible with generative face editing. Artificial intelligence-driven face editing tools are gaining popularity, which has significantly increased user demand for personalized portrait generation and social sharing. However, current editing methods often preserve identity features, making the edited images still susceptible to tracking by malicious FR systems. Thus, this paper proposes Flux-Guard, a privacy-preserving face editing framework based on adversarial attacks, which integrates face editing and privacy protection within a unified generative process. Specifically, we design a flow trajectory control method to align semantic manipulations with the generative process and introduce latent-space adversarial optimization with an adaptive perceptual-loss-driven weighting strategy, dynamically adjusting adversarial strength to maximize attack effectiveness while preserving visual quality.
  Extensive experiments demonstrate that Flux-Guard supports face editing while significantly improving attack success rates against cross-domain face recognition models on the CelebA-HQ and LADN datasets. Furthermore, evaluation results for commercial APIs have confirmed its effectiveness in real-world applications. The code is released at https://github.com/JLMWang/Flux-Guard.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents Flux-Guard, a method that protects facial privacy by adding subtle adversarial noise during AI-powered face editing. It allows users to edit their photos (e.g., change hairstyle, add accessories) while ensuring the edited images cannot be recognised by face recognition systems.
summary.technical_summary: Flux-Guard integrates adversarial privacy protection into the generative face editing pipeline. It uses a flow trajectory control method to align semantic manipulations with the generative process of diffusion models, and introduces latent-space adversarial optimisation with an adaptive perceptual-loss-driven weighting strategy. This dynamically adjusts adversarial strength to maximise attack success against face recognition models while preserving visual quality of the edited image. Evaluated on CelebA-HQ and LADN datasets against cross-domain FR models and commercial APIs.
summary.data_or_benchmark: CelebA-HQ, LADN datasets; cross-domain face recognition models; commercial API evaluation

title: Expanding SPHERE-JEPA: A Family of Statistical Regularizers for the Hypersphere
summary: In Self-Supervised Learning (SSL), preventing representation collapse by explicitly enforcing a uniform distribution on the unit hypersphere has proven to be effective. However, current frameworks typically rely on sliced statistical regularizers such as SIGReg (used in LeJEPA) and SUSReg (used in SPHERE-JEPA), which approximate this continuous objective via Monte Carlo sampling along random 1D directions. This stochasticity injects projection variance into the training gradients, destabilizing optimization, and hindering convergence. In this work, we first show that analytically integrating out these random projections natively yields a deterministic Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD), bypassing the variance of sliced methods. Motivated by this equivalence, we formulate full-dimensional objectives for MMD, Kernel Stein Discrepancy (KSD), and Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence directly on the sphere to enforce a uniform distribution. To prevent spatial bias, we equip these tests with rotationally invariant kernels constructed via spectral theory, systematically evaluating two canonical families: smooth exponential decay (Heat) and strict frequency cutoff (Bandlimited) filters. Empirically, removing projection-induced noise results in more stable optimization, faster convergence, and consistent improvements over stochastic sliced regularizers on ImageNet and Galaxy10. Furthermore, we reveal that the choice of the statistical test shapes the geometry of the learned latent space: MMD and KSD favor locally clustered organization suitable for object-centric domains, whereas the continuous KDE-based KL divergence promotes fine-grained instance separation, yielding the strongest results on unclustered procedural texture retrieval.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper improves self-supervised learning (SSL) methods that learn useful data representations without labels. The authors show that current approaches using random projections to enforce uniform distribution on a hypersphere introduce noise that destabilises training. They replace these stochastic methods with deterministic statistical regularizers (MMD, KSD, KL divergence) using rotationally invariant kernels, which leads to more stable optimisation, faster convergence, and better performance on image benchmarks like ImageNet and Galaxy10.
summary.technical_summary: The paper identifies that sliced statistical regularizers (SIGReg, SUSReg) used in frameworks like LeJEPA and SPHERE-JEPA approximate a continuous uniformity objective on the unit hypersphere via Monte Carlo sampling along random 1D projections, injecting variance into gradients. The authors prove that integrating out random projections yields a deterministic Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD). They then formulate full-dimensional objectives for MMD, Kernel Stein Discrepancy (KSD), and KL divergence on the sphere, using rotationally invariant kernels (Heat and Bandlimited filters) to avoid spatial bias. Empirically, removing projection noise yields more stable training, faster convergence, and consistent improvements over stochastic sliced regularizers on ImageNet and Galaxy10. The choice of regularizer shapes latent geometry: MMD/KSD favour locally clustered organisation, while KL divergence promotes fine-grained instance separation.
key_methods: Rotational invariant kernels (Heat and Bandlimited filters)
