title: Test-Time Training for Robust Text-Guided Open-Vocabulary Object Counting
summary: Text-guided Open-vocabulary Object Counting (TOOC) enables counting arbitrary object categories specified by text prompts, offering substantially greater flexibility than conventional closed-set counting. However, existing TOOC methods are developed and evaluated primarily on ideal images, while real-world scenes often suffer from adverse conditions such as rain, fog, darkness, and sensor noise, which severely degrade visual quality and impair vision-language alignment. To bridge this gap, we introduce Robust-TOOC, the first benchmark for evaluating TOOC under diverse corruption conditions, which covers six representative degradation types: rain, fog, darkness, Gaussian noise, salt-and-pepper noise, and mixed corruption. To improve robustness while preserving the original counting architecture, we propose Dual-TTT, a dual-architecture test-time training framework for TOOC. Specifically, during test-time training, Dual-TTT updates only the Text-guided Lightweight Denoising module (TL-Denoiser), while keeping the original counting network frozen. Inspired by diffusion models, the TL-Denoiser is optimized to remove corruption-aware noise from image representations under degraded conditions. Since only the TL-Denoiser is trained at test time, Dual-TTT is annotation-free and can be seamlessly integrated into existing TOOC models without modifying their original architecture. Extensive experiments on multiple recent TOOC baselines demonstrate the effectiveness of our method.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper introduces a new benchmark (Robust-TOOC) for testing how well text-guided object counting models work under real-world image corruptions like rain, fog, darkness, and noise. It also proposes a method called Dual-TTT that adds a lightweight denoising module trained at test time to make existing counting models more robust without changing their original architecture.
summary.technical_summary: The paper addresses Text-guided Open-vocabulary Object Counting (TOOC) under adverse conditions. It first constructs Robust-TOOC, a benchmark applying six corruption types (rain, fog, darkness, Gaussian noise, salt-and-pepper noise, mixed corruption) to existing counting datasets. The proposed Dual-TTT framework uses a dual-architecture test-time training approach: a Text-guided Lightweight Denoising module (TL-Denoiser) is trained at test time to remove corruption-aware noise from image representations, while the original frozen counting network remains unchanged. The TL-Denoiser is inspired by diffusion model principles and is optimized per test sample without requiring annotations.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Robust-TOOC benchmark covering 6 corruption types applied to existing TOOC datasets; evaluation on multiple recent TOOC baselines
summary.main_contribution: First benchmark for evaluating TOOC under diverse corruption conditions, plus a plug-and-play test-time training method that improves robustness without modifying original model architectures.

title: MuseVLA: An Adaptive Multimodal Sensing Vision-Language-Action Model for Robotic Manipulation
summary: Humans naturally leverage diverse sensing modalities to interact with the physical world, while most Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robotics rely solely on RGB observations. This limits their ability to perceive physical properties that are difficult or impossible to infer from RGB cameras, such as temperature, sound, or radar response. We present MuseVLA, an adaptive multimodal sensing VLA model that integrates novel sensors as on-demand tools for robotic manipulation. Given a task instruction and visual context, MuseVLA first generates a sensor token and target description that select the sensing modality to invoke and what to attend to, analogous to a tool call with arguments. It then converts the selected sensor measurement into a grounded sensor image, a unified intermediate representation that encodes heterogeneous readings for multimodal fusion and action generation. This design decouples sensor-specific processing from the VLA backbone, enabling efficient integration of diverse modalities. To reduce the need for expensive multisensory robot datasets, we further introduce a data synthesis pipeline that augments existing RGB video datasets with grounded sensor images, enabling generalization to unseen sensor-guided tasks. We evaluate MuseVLA on a real-world robot across challenging dexterous hand manipulation tasks that require multimodal sensing inputs, including temperature-guided pick-and-place, audio-driven object search, and radar-assisted hidden object retrieval. MuseVLA achieves 80.6% success rate on average, outperforming RGB-only and multisensory VLA baselines significantly, and exhibits strong zero-shot capabilities on unseen tasks.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents MuseVLA, a robotic system that combines vision, language, and action with additional sensors (temperature, audio, radar) to help robots manipulate objects more intelligently. It generates a sensor token and target description to select which sensing modality to invoke, converts sensor readings into a unified "grounded sensor image" representation, and fuses this with RGB data for action generation. A data synthesis pipeline augments existing RGB video datasets with simulated sensor images to reduce the need for expensive multisensory robot data collection.

title: Closing the Feedback Loop: From Experience Extraction to Insight Governance in Verbal Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted to the ICML 2026 RLxF: Reinforcement Learning from World Feedback Workshop, RLxF@ICML 2026, Seoul, South Korea
summary: Training-free verbal reinforcement learning enables LLM agents to learn from world feedback -- objective signals such as dynamic task outcomes, market returns, or demand forecasts -- by extracting verbal rules from experience and injecting them as context, updating the agent's behavior without parameter changes. However, in non-stationary environments these agents face a retention-forgetting dilemma: retaining stale insights causes negative transfer, while discarding them causes catastrophic forgetting when conditions recur. We identify four requirements for navigating this dilemma -- outcome-driven evaluation, persistent structured evidence, non-monotonic knowledge lifecycle, and compositional governance -- and show that existing methods invest heavily in experience extraction while underinvesting in insight governance. We propose a three-layer architecture -- rules, evidence, and skills -- connected by a feedback-driven curation loop that closes the governance gap. Rules capture distilled experience from world outcomes; evidence logs track each rule's reliability across episodes; skills govern which rules to apply, how to resolve conflicts, and when to abstain. On financial forecasting as a case study, where world feedback is naturally abundant, noisy, and non-stationary, we show that the same accumulated experience either degrades performance below the zero-shot baseline or dramatically improves accuracy and risk-adjusted returns, depending on whether the curation loop is present.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper proposes a method to help LLM agents learn from experience without retraining, by extracting verbal rules from feedback and managing them with a structured system. It addresses the problem of when to keep or discard learned rules in changing environments, using a three-layer architecture (rules, evidence, skills) with a feedback-driven curation loop. Tested on financial forecasting.
summary.technical_summary: The paper identifies a retention-forgetting dilemma in training-free verbal RL for LLM agents in non-stationary environments. It proposes a three-layer architecture: (1) rules layer capturing distilled experience from world outcomes, (2) evidence logs tracking rule reliability across episodes, and (3) skills layer governing rule application, conflict resolution, and abstention. A feedback-driven curation loop connects these layers. Evaluated on financial forecasting tasks where world feedback is abundant, noisy, and non-stationary.

title: Root-Selecting Fixed-Point Inversion for Rectified Flows via Trajectory Straightness
summary: Finding the initial noise that generates a given data sample, known as inversion, is a key component for downstream applications such as training-free image editing. Existing fixed-point inversion methods improve inversion accuracy by formulating each inversion step as a fixed-point problem, but they lack a principled mechanism for selecting among multiple fixed-point solutions that can arise in practice. We observe that different selections induce different inversion trajectories, leading to substantial variation in reconstruction and editing quality. For rectified flows, we further find that this variation is closely associated with trajectory straightness, motivating straightness as a principled selection criterion. We propose SelFix, a fixed-point inversion method that selects fixed-point solutions inducing straighter inverse trajectories while retaining convergence to an exact inverse root under standard local assumptions. Experiments on FLUX.1-dev and PIE-Bench show that SelFix improves fixed-point inversion, achieving stronger real-image reconstruction and better source-preserving prompt-based editing than prior inversion baselines. The code is available at https://github.com/seminkim/selfix.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper proposes a new method (SelFix) for finding the initial noise that generates a given image in rectified flow models, which is important for image editing. It selects among multiple possible solutions by preferring those that produce straighter inversion trajectories, leading to better reconstruction and editing quality.
summary.technical_summary: SelFix addresses the fixed-point inversion problem in rectified flows by introducing a principled selection criterion based on trajectory straightness. The method formulates each inversion step as a fixed-point problem, observes that multiple fixed-point solutions exist, and selects the one that induces the straightest inverse trajectory. This is grounded in the observation that straighter trajectories correlate with better reconstruction and editing quality. The method retains convergence guarantees under standard local assumptions. Evaluated on FLUX.1-dev and PIE-Bench, SelFix outperforms prior inversion baselines in real-image reconstruction and source-preserving prompt-based editing.
summary.data_or_benchmark: FLUX.1-dev, PIE-Bench
summary.main_contribution: Proposes trajectory straightness as a principled criterion for selecting among multiple fixed-point solutions in rectified flow inversion, with theoretical convergence guarantees and empirical improvements in image reconstruction and editing.
summary.limitations: Limited to rectified flows; evaluation only on image tasks; no direct connection to biomedical or clinical applications; computational overhead of selecting among multiple fixed-point solutions not thoroughly discussed.

title: Visored: A Controlled-Natural-Language Prover for LLM-Generated Mathematics
summary: We present a dependent-type-based prover designed around the way LLMs (and humans) tend to write mathematics, complementing existing systems such as Lean and Rocq. Its core design choices are a surface that imitates mathematical natural language and a rule-driven automation layer that closes the routine steps a textbook would omit, so that an accepted proof can be re-emitted as a checked Lean file. Early experiments suggest that, even without any prover-specific training data, LLMs can learn to use it effectively on the miniF2F benchmark. Lean output excerpts: https://github.com/xiyuzhai-husky-lang/visored/

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

It can automatically fill in routine steps and output checked proofs in Lean format.
summary.technical_summary: Visored is a dependent-type-based theorem prover with a controlled natural language surface that mimics informal mathematical writing. It includes a rule-driven automation layer to handle routine proof steps typically omitted in textbooks. The system can re-emit accepted proofs as verified Lean files. Early experiments on the miniF2F benchmark show LLMs can use Visored effectively without any prover-specific training data.

title: AoiZora: Topology-Aware Auto-Parallel Optimization for Inference of Diffusion Transformers
summary: Video diffusion has quickly grown into a key generative serving workload, yet producing each clip demands many denoising iterations over large spatio-temporal latents, which puts low-latency inference out of reach on a single device. A denoising step is therefore typically distributed across multiple accelerators, and TPU sub-slices have become an attractive and practical fabric for doing so. Current auto-parallel systems, however, search almost exclusively over logical device meshes and disregard how a chosen sharding is actually laid out on the physical TPU interconnect -- an oversight that leaves large, topology-dependent performance on the table. We address this gap with AoiZora, a compiler-mediated topology planner built for low-latency video diffusion inference on TPU sub-slices. Its guiding principle is to reconnect logical sharding with physical placement by drawing on different points in the compilation flow: AoiZora first eliminates weak sharding candidates from inexpensive pre-compilation IRs, then compiles only the ones that survive and orders their physical placements using compiled HLO together with a topology-aware communication model. The winning plan is realized along the ordinary compiler path, leaving model code, compiler lowering, collective kernels, and network routing entirely intact. On TPU v5e sub-slices, AoiZora reduces Wan 2.1 one-step denoising latency by as much as 1.42x relative to existing solutions.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents AoiZora, a system that optimizes how video diffusion models (like Wan 2.1) are distributed across multiple TPU chips for faster inference. It focuses on the physical layout of the TPU interconnect, not just the logical arrangement of devices, to reduce latency during the denoising steps required to generate video clips.
summary.technical_summary: AoiZora is a compiler-mediated topology planner for low-latency video diffusion inference on TPU sub-slices. It addresses the gap between logical sharding strategies and physical TPU interconnect topology. The approach first prunes weak sharding candidates using inexpensive pre-compilation IRs, then compiles surviving candidates and orders their physical placements using compiled HLO combined with a topology-aware communication model. The winning plan is executed through the ordinary compiler path without modifying model code, compiler lowering, collective kernels, or network routing. On TPU v5e sub-slices, it achieves up to 1.42x reduction in one-step denoising latency for Wan 2.1 compared to existing auto-parallel solutions.

Compiled HLO with topology-aware communication model

summary.main_contribution: A compiler-mediated topology planner that reconnects logical sharding with physical TPU interconnect placement, achieving up to 1.42x latency reduction for video diffusion inference without modifying model code, compiler lowering, or collective kernels.

title: Geometric Consistency Protocol for Foundation Model Features in Multi-View Satellite Imagery
comment: The manuscript is accepted as Oral Presentation in IEEE International Geoscience and Remote Sensing Symposium(IGARSS 2026)
summary: Standardized evaluation protocols are indispensable for robust benchmarking in remote sensing, particularly as foundation features are increasingly transferred across diverse sensors and complex imaging geometries. In satellite multi-view reconstruction, conventional evaluations relying on unconstrained 2D global matching are often misleading. The Rational Function Model (RFM) and its Rational Polynomial Coefficients (RPC) dictate a curved, height-dependent epipolar geometry that render flat 2D search spaces physically inconsistent. We propose a geometry-faithful and reproducible protocol tailored for the RPC framework. Our approach integrates an RPC-projected 3D consistency metric with a geometry-constrained dense matching proxy, specifically evaluating whether similarity responses remain localized and unique under physically plausible search manifolds. A pivotal finding of our joint reporting strategy is the decoupling of semantic agreement and geometric localization: high cross-view similarity at a projected 3D point does not guarantee reliable matchability in practical inference. Our benchmark demonstrates that incorporating geometric constraints is fundamental to the problem definition in satellite imagery. Furthermore, we show that state-of-the-art 2D backbones remain remarkably competitive against specialized 3D-aware models when subjected to this RPC-consistent evaluation.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper proposes a new evaluation protocol for testing AI foundation models on satellite imagery. It argues that standard 2D matching benchmarks are misleading for satellite images because the geometry is curved and height-dependent. The authors introduce a geometry-consistent metric and show that simple 2D models can perform as well as complex 3D models when evaluated properly.
summary.technical_summary: The paper addresses the problem of evaluating foundation model features for multi-view satellite imagery under the Rational Function Model (RFM) and Rational Polynomial Coefficients (RPC) framework. It identifies that conventional 2D global matching evaluations are physically inconsistent due to the curved, height-dependent epipolar geometry of satellite images. The authors propose a geometry-faithful protocol integrating an RPC-projected 3D consistency metric with a geometry-constrained dense matching proxy. Key finding: high cross-view semantic similarity at a projected 3D point does not guarantee reliable matchability. The benchmark reveals that state-of-the-art 2D backbones remain competitive against specialized 3D-aware models under RPC-consistent evaluation.
summary.data_or_benchmark: Multi-view satellite imagery benchmark with RPC-consistent evaluation protocol
summary.main_contribution: Proposes a geometry-faithful evaluation protocol for foundation model features in satellite multi-view reconstruction, demonstrating that standard 2D evaluation is misleading and that 2D backbones are competitive when evaluated correctly.
summary.limitations: Specific to satellite imagery and RPC geometry; no direct applicability to biomedical or omics data.

title: RT-Counter: Real-Time Text-Guided Open-Vocabulary Object Counting
summary: Text-guided open-vocabulary object counting (TOOC) aims to count objects belonging to the categories specified by natural language descriptions. Although vision-language pre-trained models have been successful applied to TOOC tasks, they still struggle with fine-grained spatial understanding and real-time inference requirements in counting scenarios. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a real-time TOOC framework, called the Real-Time Counter (RT-Counter), that achieves not only good counting accuracy but also high computational efficiency. RT-Counter designs a novel Visual Prototype Textualization (VPT) module that can project learned visual features into a text feature space and then generate features containing the abstract information that is hard to capture with visual prototypes and the detailed prototype information that is difficult to describe in text, enhancing the object-level visual-language model's counting capabilities. Additionally, RT-Counter incorporates our Weaving Transformer (Weaformer) layers, maintaining high descriptive power at a fraction of the computational cost. The Weaformer layer adopts a novel hybrid attention mechanism that can efficiently weave together local and global visual features. Extensive experiments on three public datasets show that RT-Counter successfully breaks the accuracy-speed trade-off in TOOC. While achieving a competitive MAE of 13.30 on FSC147, RT-Counter operates at 112.48 FPS, making it 7.4x faster and over 4$\times$ more parameter-efficient than the existing leading methods in TOOC. Our work aims at balancing high accuracy and real-time performance in TOOC. Code is available at: https://github.com/Jason-Mar1/RT-Counter.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents RT-Counter, a fast and accurate system for counting objects in images based on natural language descriptions (e.g., "count all red cars"). It uses a novel Visual Prototype Textualization module and Weaving Transformer layers to achieve high speed (112 FPS) while maintaining competitive accuracy, breaking the usual trade-off between speed and precision in open-vocabulary counting tasks.
summary.technical_summary: RT-Counter addresses text-guided open-vocabulary object counting (TOOC) by introducing two key components: (1) a Visual Prototype Textualization (VPT) module that projects visual features into text feature space, generating hybrid features that capture both abstract visual information and detailed prototype information difficult to express in text; and (2) Weaving Transformer (Weaformer) layers with a hybrid attention mechanism that efficiently combines local and global visual features at reduced computational cost. The model achieves 13.30 MAE on FSC147 at 112.48 FPS, 7.4x faster and 4x more parameter-efficient than prior methods.
summary.data_or_benchmark: FSC147 dataset (13.30 MAE), plus two other public object counting datasets
summary.main_contribution: Breaking the accuracy-speed trade-off in text-guided open-vocabulary object counting through efficient architectural design (VPT + Weaformer), achieving real-time performance with competitive accuracy.
summary.limitations: Limited to computer vision object counting tasks; no direct biomedical or clinical application; evaluated only on general-domain counting datasets; no multi-modal clinical data integration demonstrated.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low direct relevance. This is a pure computer vision paper on object counting with no connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or causal inference. The transformer efficiency techniques (Weaformer) are not novel architectural contributions that would transfer to biomedical prediction tasks.

However, this is a very loose connection and the paper itself offers no guidance on such adaptation.

title: Universal Image Restoration via Internalized Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
summary: Image restoration seeks to recover high-quality images from degraded inputs but becomes highly ill-posed under complex, mixed degradations. While unified all-in-one models are common, their performance declines as degradation complexity increases. Recent works adopt Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning for multi-round restoration using specialized modules. However, this approach faces two key limitations: (i) increased computational cost due to multi-step processing, and (ii) weak modeling of interactions between degradations during stepwise inference. We introduce CoTIR, a universal image restoration framework that internalizes CoT reasoning within a single model. Concretely, we view image restoration as a specialized subtask of image editing, which implies that a large-scale pre-trained editing model provides a more favorable optimization starting point. Building on this, we fine-tune the model for restoration and further encode structured CoT-style reasoning into the learning objective via a differentiable formulation inspired by Lagrangian optimization, enabling holistic restoration without chaining specialized restorers. To facilitate training and evaluation, we further present CoTIR-Bench, a large-scale benchmark comprising 5.2 million samples with CoT-style reasoning traces. Extensive experiments on CoTIR-Bench and broad real composite degradation scenes show that CoTIR achieves stronger perceptual quality and more competitive fidelity than both all-in-one models and multi-round restoration methods. The source code is available at https://github.com/gy65896/CoTIR.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents a new method for restoring degraded images (like blurry, noisy, or damaged photos) using a single AI model that internalizes a step-by-step reasoning process, inspired by chain-of-thought prompting. Instead of using multiple specialized models for different types of degradation, the authors fine-tune a large pre-trained image editing model to handle all restoration tasks in one pass, guided by structured reasoning traces. They also created a large benchmark dataset with 5.2 million samples to train and evaluate the approach.
summary.technical_summary: CoTIR frames image restoration as a subtask of image editing, leveraging a large pre-trained editing model as initialization. It encodes chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning into the learning objective via a differentiable formulation inspired by Lagrangian optimization, enabling holistic restoration without chaining multiple specialized restorers. The method avoids the computational overhead of multi-round restoration while maintaining strong performance on complex, mixed degradations. CoTIR-Bench, a dataset of 5.2M samples with CoT reasoning traces, is introduced for training and evaluation.
key_methods: Fine-tuning large pre-trained image editing models
summary.data_or_benchmark: CoTIR-Bench: 5.2 million samples with CoT-style reasoning traces for training and evaluation.
summary.main_contribution: Proposes CoTIR, a universal image restoration framework that internalizes chain-of-thought reasoning within a single model, avoiding the computational cost of multi-round restoration while achieving strong perceptual quality and fidelity on complex mixed degradations.
summary.limitations: The paper is in computer vision (image restoration) with no direct connection to biomedical data, proteomics, or epidemiology. The method is domain-specific to image processing and would require substantial rethinking to apply to tabular or omics data.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. The paper is in computer vision (image restoration) and has no direct connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, MR, PRS, or clinical prediction. The chain-of-thought reasoning concept is interesting but applied to pixel-level image restoration, not biomedical inference or multimodal prediction.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly applicable. The CoT reasoning internalization idea could theoretically inspire new ways to model sequential causal reasoning steps in MR or multi-omics integration, but the gap is large and the methodology is highly domain-specific.
summary.possible_research_idea: One could explore whether internalized chain-of-thought reasoning could be adapted for stepwise causal inference in multi-omics data (e.g., proteomics -> transcriptomics -> phenotype), but this would require a fundamentally different formulation and is not a near-term research direction.

title: An AI Security Agent for Banking: Multi-Vector Fraud and AML Detection Across Retail and Corporate Accounts
summary: Banks simultaneously face signature-based fraud (card-not-present attacks, account takeover, ATM cloning) and behavioural financial crime (structuring, layering, mule networks, business email compromise) -- two threat families with fundamentally different detection requirements. Static rule engines that reliably catch brute-force and high-velocity events are structurally blind to business-email-compromise (BEC) payment redirection, session hijacking, and money-laundering layering, which are engineered to appear indistinguishable from legitimate activity at the individual transaction or session level. This paper presents an AI security agent for retail and corporate banking that addresses this gap through a three-component fusion architecture operating on two parallel event streams: a transaction stream (card fraud, ACH/wire fraud, AML categories) and a session stream (account takeover, session hijacking, SIM-swap, insider abuse). Each stream combines an LSTM sequence model capturing per-account behavioural history, a statistical velocity/threshold monitor, and a graph/network module capturing account-counterparty relationship patterns (fan-in, fan-out, pass-through ratio) for money-laundering detection. Experiments on a synthetic event log of 237,669 transactions and 113,508 sessions across 13 threat categories and 3,470 simulated accounts demonstrate overall F1 of 0.787 (transaction stream) and 0.867 (session stream) for the proposed model, versus 0.562/0.733 for a rule-based baseline and 0.655/0.713 for an LSTM-only baseline. The agent includes a customer-facing transaction-verification chatbot (96.6% identity verification accuracy, 86.8% mass-reset attack detection) and an analyst case-summary assistant (99.3% action-recommendation F1), with Critical-tier automated response latency under 0.43 ms at the 95th percentile.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper presents an AI security system for banks that detects both traditional fraud (like card cloning) and complex financial crimes (like money laundering). It uses a three-part architecture combining LSTM neural networks for tracking account behaviour, statistical monitors for unusual activity, and graph analysis for detecting suspicious money flow patterns across accounts. The system was tested on synthetic banking data with 237,669 transactions and 113,508 sessions across 13 threat types.
summary.technical_summary: The paper proposes a multi-component AI security agent for banking fraud and anti-money laundering (AML) detection. It operates on two parallel event streams: transaction stream (card fraud, ACH/wire fraud, AML) and session stream (account takeover, session hijacking, SIM-swap, insider abuse). Each stream uses a three-component fusion architecture: (1) an LSTM sequence model for per-account behavioural history, (2) a statistical velocity/threshold monitor, and (3) a graph/network module for account-counterparty relationship patterns (fan-in, fan-out, pass-through ratio). Experiments on a synthetic dataset of 237,669 transactions and 113,508 sessions across 3,470 accounts and 13 threat categories show overall F1 of 0.787 (transaction) and 0.867 (session), outperforming rule-based (0.562/0.733) and LSTM-only (0.655/0.713) baselines. Additional components include a customer-facing transaction-verification chatbot (96.6% identity verification accuracy) and an analyst case-summary assistant (99.3% action-recommendation F1).

Graph/network module for account-counterparty relationship analysis
Synthetic event log generation for banking fraud
summary.data_or_benchmark: Synthetic event log of 237,669 transactions and 113,508 sessions across 13 threat categories and 3,470 simulated accounts. No real-world banking data used.
summary.main_contribution: A three-component fusion architecture combining LSTM, statistical monitoring, and graph analysis for multi-vector fraud and AML detection in banking, with demonstrated improvements over rule-based and LSTM-only baselines on synthetic data.
summary.limitations: Synthetic data only — no validation on real banking data. No discussion of regulatory compliance, privacy constraints, or deployment challenges in real banking environments. Limited novelty in individual components (LSTM, graph analysis are standard). The fusion architecture itself is the main contribution but is not deeply theoretically motivated.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Very low. This paper is about banking fraud detection and has no connection to osteoarthritis, proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, Mendelian randomisation, PRS, or any biomedical or epidemiological topic relevant to Weijie's research.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: None. The methods (LSTM + graph + statistical monitoring) are domain-specific to banking fraud and do not transfer meaningfully to proteomics, causal inference, or clinical prediction in osteoarthritis.
summary.possible_research_idea: No viable research ideas for Weijie's context. The fusion architecture concept (combining sequence models, statistical monitoring, and graph networks) could theoretically be applied to multi-modal clinical data, but the paper provides no biomedical insight or methodology that would directly translate.

title: Reversal Q-Learning
summary: Iterative generative modeling techniques, such as flow matching, provide powerful tools to model complex behaviors for effective offline reinforcement learning (RL). In this work, we propose a new off-policy RL algorithm that trains a flow policy based on prior data. Our idea starts from the "expanded" Markov decision process (MDP) framework, which treats individual flow refinement steps as separate actions in an MDP. To enable off-policy RL within this framework, we apply two techniques: we generate virtual on-policy trajectories (by "reversing" flows) to make this framework compatible with prior data, and we apply a bias-and-variance reduction technique to mitigate the curse of horizon in off-policy RL. We call the resulting algorithm Reversal Q-learning (RQL). RQL has several advantages over previous flow-based RL methods: it does not suffer from backpropagation through time, makes better use of the learned value function, and directly trains the full, expressive flow policy. Through our experiments on 50 challenging simulated robotic tasks, we show that RQL leads to the best average offline RL performance compared to state-of-the-art flow-based offline RL algorithms.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper introduces a new reinforcement learning algorithm called Reversal Q-Learning (RQL) that trains AI agents to make decisions by learning from existing data, without needing to interact with the environment. It uses "flow matching" techniques to model complex behaviors and improves upon previous methods by avoiding certain computational bottlenecks.
summary.technical_summary: RQL proposes an off-policy RL algorithm that trains a flow policy using prior data within an "expanded" Markov decision process (MDP) framework, where individual flow refinement steps are treated as separate actions. To enable off-policy learning, the authors generate virtual on-policy trajectories by reversing flows and apply bias-and-variance reduction to mitigate the curse of horizon. The method avoids backpropagation through time, better leverages the learned value function, and directly trains the full expressive flow policy. Evaluated on 50 simulated robotic tasks, RQL achieves state-of-the-art offline RL performance compared to prior flow-based methods.
key_methods: Virtual on-policy trajectory generation via flow reversal
summary.data_or_benchmark: 50 challenging simulated robotic tasks (offline RL benchmark suite)
summary.main_contribution: A new off-policy RL algorithm (RQL) that trains flow policies without backpropagation through time, achieving SOTA offline RL performance on robotic control tasks.
summary.limitations: Evaluated only on simulated robotic tasks; no biomedical or clinical applications; requires offline datasets with sufficient coverage; computational cost of flow-based policies may be high for large-scale applications.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. This is a pure machine learning / RL paper with no connection to proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, osteoarthritis, or any biomedical domain. The methods are not directly transferable to Weijie's research areas (causal inference, PRS, multimodal prediction, clinical risk modelling).

title: Continuous-time Optimal Stopping through Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Simulation based solvers for optimal stopping problems must discretize the stopping decision. Under classical dynamic programming, a coarse exercise grid with only a few stopping opportunities can materially undervalue the optimal expected reward, whereas on a very fine grid, approximation errors accumulate through the backward recursion. To remove this limitation, we develop a new reinforcement-learning inspired algorithm that enables us to learn the exercise rule at arbitrarily fine time resolution. Our CARLOS (Continuous-time Adaptive Reinforcement Learning for Optimal Stopping) algorithm utilizes an aggregate deep neural network (ADNN) to learn a joint space-time decision boundary. Starting from a coarse time grid, we progressively increase the frequency of stopping opportunities, while in parallel training the ADNN to refine its timing-value estimates. We moreover design an adaptive sampling strategy that gradually concentrates training effort near the stopping boundary. Benchmarked results show that CARLOS delivers higher prices than existing Bermudan solvers, approaching the American upper bound, and achieves high computational efficiency relative to non-RL comparators.

research_radar.main_topic: Proteomics, pQTL, multi-omics, and osteoarthritis biology

summary.plain_english_summary: This paper develops a deep reinforcement learning algorithm (CARLOS) for solving optimal stopping problems in continuous time, such as pricing American options. It uses a neural network to learn when to stop, progressively refining the time grid to approach the theoretical upper bound of the optimal reward.
summary.technical_summary: The authors propose CARLOS, a reinforcement learning algorithm that solves continuous-time optimal stopping problems without discretizing the stopping decision. It uses an aggregate deep neural network (ADNN) to learn a joint space-time decision boundary. Starting from a coarse time grid, it progressively increases stopping frequency while training the ADNN to refine timing-value estimates. An adaptive sampling strategy concentrates training near the stopping boundary. Benchmarked against Bermudan option pricing solvers, CARLOS achieves higher prices approaching the American upper bound with high computational efficiency.
key_methods: Deep reinforcement learning for optimal stopping
summary.data_or_benchmark: Benchmarked on American option pricing problems (financial datasets)
summary.main_contribution: A continuous-time RL algorithm that removes the discretization limitation of classical optimal stopping solvers, achieving near-optimal American exercise prices.
summary.limitations: Designed and tested only for financial option pricing; no biomedical or clinical application demonstrated; requires further adaptation for survival analysis or treatment timing problems.
summary.relevance_to_weijie: Low. The paper is purely about financial mathematics and optimal stopping theory with no connection to proteomics, osteoarthritis, genetics, or clinical prediction.
summary.possible_use_in_my_research: Unlikely to be directly applicable. The optimal stopping framework could theoretically relate to treatment timing decisions, but the paper provides no bridge to biomedical contexts.
summary.possible_research_idea: If adapted, the continuous-time optimal stopping framework could model when to intervene (e.g., joint replacement timing) based on longitudinal biomarker trajectories, but this would require substantial reformulation.
